pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives
pages.epigraph: “You cannot prepare for every moment, but you can prepare to meet those moments together.”

blocks.text: The Nursery Was Ready. The Family Plan Was Not.

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: A couple spent months preparing for their first baby. They compared prams, ordered a cot and bought enough newborn clothes to dress several babies. They attended appointments and kept a careful list of everything still to be purchased. Then someone asked them a different set of questions. Who will prepare meals during the first week? Who will handle visitors? What will happen when both parents are exhausted? Who can accompany the mother to an appointment if her partner is unavailable? What signs of emotional or physical difficulty should the family watch for after the birth?

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: They had not discussed any of these things. They had prepared the house for a baby. They had not yet prepared the household for parenthood. This is common. Baby products are visible and easy to organise. Emotional expectations, changing responsibilities and family support are less visible—but they often have a greater effect on the early weeks.
Preparation Has More Than One Meaning

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Preparing for a baby usually includes medical care and practical arrangements. It should also include conversations about how family life may change. A useful preparation plan considers five areas:

items: Medical care and important health information

blocks.text: You do not need a perfect plan for each area. You need enough clarity that important decisions do not have to be made for the first time during an exhausted night.

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Begin with Realistic Expectations

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: A newborn does not arrive with a predictable timetable. Feeding can take longer than expected. Sleep may arrive in short stretches. The mother may need more physical recovery and emotional support than the family anticipated. Household routines may become temporarily untidy. Visitors may be helpful—or may create additional work. A realistic expectation is not a negative one. It does not assume that everything will be difficult. It simply leaves room for recovery, learning and adjustment. Try replacing rigid expectations with flexible intentions.
Instead of:
“The baby will follow a routine immediately.”
Consider:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: “We will observe the baby’s needs and gradually build a safe rhythm.”
Instead of:
“I will manage everything myself.”
Consider:
“I will remain involved while accepting help with tasks that do not require me.”
Instead of:
“Breastfeeding must work in one particular way.”
Consider:
“I will seek qualified support, monitor whether my baby is feeding adequately and make informed decisions.”
Flexibility is not a lack of preparation. It is part of good preparation.
Discuss Responsibilities Before Everyone Is Tired

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Before the birth, list the work that keeps the household functioning. This may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: Protecting the mother’s opportunities to rest

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Then decide who can take primary responsibility for each task during the early weeks. “Helping when asked” is not always a complete plan. When one person must notice every task, request assistance and explain how it should be done, that person still carries much of the mental load. A clearer plan sounds like:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: One person monitors groceries and household supplies.
A family member provides meals on specified days.
The partner records medical questions and appointment information.
Someone is available to care for an older child during appointments.
Both parents understand the baby’s essential health information.

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Responsibilities can change. The purpose of the plan is not to create a permanent contract. It is to prevent one recovering parent from becoming the manager of every need.
Build a Support Map
A village does not have to be large. It has to be dependable. Your support map may include:
Medical support

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Practical support

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Emotional support

items: Someone who listens without immediately correcting you
A parenting or postpartum support group

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: One person may not be able to provide every type of support. A relative may be wonderful at preparing meals but unhelpful when discussing feeding choices. A friend may offer emotional reassurance but be unable to provide practical help. Use people for the support they can offer safely and respectfully.
Ask for Specific Help
People often say:
“Let me know if you need anything.”
A tired parent may not know how to answer. Specific requests are easier for everyone. You might ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: “Could you take our older child to school?”
“Could you sit with the baby while I shower?”
“Could you come to the appointment and take notes?”
“Could you hold calls and visitors until the weekend?”
“Could you help us fold laundry instead of bringing another gift?”

blocks.text: Support should reduce work, not create more of it.
Make a Visitor Plan

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Visitors may bring affection, encouragement and practical help. They can also interrupt feeding, rest and recovery. Discuss:

items: How many people can visit at once
Whether anyone should postpone visiting when unwell
Whether photographs may be taken or shared
What the mother will do when she needs privacy

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: The recovering mother should not have to defend every boundary herself. A partner or trusted family member can communicate the plan kindly and consistently. A useful sentence is:
“We are keeping visits short while the baby and mother settle. We will let you know when it is a good time.”
A boundary does not mean the family is ungrateful. It means the household is protecting recovery and adjustment.
Talk About Feeding Without Turning It into a Test

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Families may have strong expectations about how a baby should be fed. Those expectations can create pressure before the baby has even arrived. Before the birth, discuss principles rather than promises. For example:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: We will seek reliable feeding information.
We will ask for help early when something is painful or unclear.
We will monitor the baby’s wellbeing and feeding effectiveness.
We will avoid shaming the mother.
We will not allow relatives or social media to replace qualified medical guidance.
We will make decisions based on the needs of the baby and mother.

blocks.text: The goal is a safely fed baby and an appropriately supported mother. The exact path may not become clear until after the birth.

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Prepare for the Mother’s Recovery
Birth is not only the arrival of a baby. It is also a significant physical and emotional experience for the mother. Recovery needs vary according to:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Ask the maternity-care team:

items: What physical symptoms are commonly expected?
Which symptoms require an urgent call or assessment?
How should pain medicines be taken?
What wound or perineal care may be needed?
Who should be contacted outside clinic hours?
What emotional changes should the family watch for?
Which medicines are compatible with the chosen feeding method?

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: When can ordinary activities be resumed safely?
What support is available if recovery is more difficult than expected?

blocks.text: Record the answers. Exhaustion makes details harder to remember.
Emotional Changes Deserve Preparation Too
Many parents expect tiredness. Fewer prepare for the emotional intensity that may follow birth. A mother may feel:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: Grief about how the birth unfolded

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Several feelings can exist at the same time. A mother can love her baby and still find the transition difficult. She can feel grateful and still need help. She can have a supportive family and still experience postpartum depression or anxiety. Emotional difficulty is not evidence of poor character or inadequate love.
Create an Emotional-Support Plan
Before the birth, agree that someone will regularly ask the mother questions such as:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: Are you able to rest at all?
Do you feel persistently frightened or unable to relax?
Are you feeling unusually hopeless, detached or overwhelmed?
Are your thoughts making it difficult to care for yourself or the baby?
Would you like help contacting a professional?

blocks.text: The purpose is not to interrogate her. It is to make honest answers possible.
RED FLAG: Seek Urgent Help

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Contact emergency or urgent medical services immediately if the mother:

items: Has thoughts of harming herself or the baby
Appears severely confused or disconnected from reality
Sees or hears things that others do not
Has extreme agitation, unusual beliefs or rapidly worsening behaviour
Cannot safely care for herself or the baby
Has a severe physical symptom identified by her maternity team as requiring emergency assessment

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Do not leave her alone while urgent help is being arranged. Postpartum mental-health emergencies are medical emergencies. They deserve immediate, compassionate treatment—not secrecy or shame.
Prepare for Differences Between Partners

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Two caring parents can approach the same baby differently. One may respond quickly to every sound. The other may prefer to pause and observe. One may rely heavily on professional advice. The other may trust family traditions. These differences do not automatically mean that one person cares more. Before the baby arrives, discuss:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: Which medical professionals will guide health questions
What information may be shared with relatives
How each parent can get some rest
How each parent prefers to receive support
Which parenting practices are non-negotiable for safety
How cultural or family traditions will be evaluated

blocks.text: A helpful rule is:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Safety decisions follow reliable evidence and qualified guidance. Preference decisions can allow flexibility.
Make Space for the Other Parent to Learn

Sometimes the mother becomes the default expert because she has read more, attended more appointments or spent more time preparing. Then the other parent waits for instructions. This creates an exhausting pattern. Where possible, both parents should learn:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: How to hold and soothe the baby safely
How feeding support can be provided
How to recognise important warning signs
What the mother needs during recovery

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Competence grows through participation. If one parent corrects every small difference, the other may stop trying. Intervene when safety is involved, but allow room for each caregiver to develop a comfortable and responsible relationship with the baby.
Think About Older Children
A new baby changes the family system. An older child may feel excited, worried, proud, jealous or temporarily more dependent. Before the birth:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: Explain the coming changes in age-appropriate language.
Avoid promising that the baby will immediately become a playmate.
Keep important routines as predictable as possible.
Arrange care for the older child during the birth.
Let the child participate without creating responsibility.
Prepare for regression or increased need for reassurance.
Protect short periods of individual attention.
Avoid blaming every restriction on the baby.

blocks.text: Instead of saying:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: “I can’t play because of the baby.”
Try:
“I am feeding the baby now. When I finish, we can read one story together.”
The goal is not to prevent every difficult feeling. It is to show the older child that those feelings can be expressed without losing their place in the family.
Prepare the Essentials—Not an Entire Shop
A newborn’s practical needs are relatively simple. The family generally needs:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: A safe place for the baby to sleep
Feeding supplies appropriate to the family’s plan
A plan for obtaining additional supplies when needed

blocks.text: Products marketed to new parents are not automatically necessary or safe. Before buying an item, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: Is it recommended for my baby’s age?
Does it meet applicable safety standards?
Could it create a sleep, choking, strangulation or fall risk?
Can it be borrowed safely, or should it be purchased new?

blocks.text: We will examine home and sleep safety more closely in the next chapter.
A Simple First-Week Plan

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Complete this before the birth and keep it somewhere accessible.
Medical contacts

Maternity-care provider: __________________________ Hospital or birth centre: __________________________ Paediatrician: ___________________________________ Emergency contact: _______________________________ Feeding-support contact: __________________________ Mental-health support contact: _____________________
Practical support

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Meals will be supported by: ________________________ Groceries will be managed by: ______________________ Laundry and cleaning will be managed by: ___________ Older-child or pet care will be managed by: __________ Visitors will be coordinated by: ____________________ Transport to appointments: _________________________
Important information

Mother’s medicines and allergies: ___________________ Baby’s planned place of sleep: ______________________ Important medical documents are stored: _____________ The family member who knows the full plan: __________

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Common Mistakes

items: Preparing products but not preparing people
Assuming the mother will request help when she is already overwhelmed
Treating every family tradition as medical guidance

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: Expecting feeding, sleep or recovery to follow a rigid plan
Allowing visitors to take priority over rest
Buying products without checking their safety or usefulness
Assuming emotional distress will disappear without support
Allowing one parent to become the only informed caregiver
Avoiding difficult conversations because they feel premature
Believing that asking for professional help means the family has failed

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

items: Someone discourages you from contacting a doctor about a genuine concern.
A caregiver insists that a traditional practice cannot be questioned.
You are told that pain, emotional distress or feeding difficulty must simply be endured.
A product promises to prevent every sleep or feeding problem.

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

items: Someone asks you to ignore current safety advice because “all babies survived it before.”
A family member repeatedly overrides the parents’ agreed safety boundaries.
You feel pressured to make a medical decision you do not understand.

blocks.text: Reliable professionals should welcome reasonable questions and explain both benefits and risks.
Your Before-Baby Checklist
Medical preparation
☐ Maternity-care contacts are recorded.

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: ☐ A paediatrician or newborn-care plan has been considered.
☐ The family knows whom to call outside normal hours.
☐ The mother’s medicines, allergies and important health information are documented.
☐ Post-birth follow-up arrangements are understood.
☐ The family knows urgent maternal warning signs provided by the medical team.
Home preparation
☐ The baby has a safe sleep space.
☐ Safe transport home has been arranged.
☐ Essential supplies are available.
☐ Important documents are easy to locate.
☐ Household responsibilities have been discussed.

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: ☐ Older children or pets have a care plan.
Support preparation
☐ At least one dependable support person has been identified.
☐ Meals and groceries have been considered.
☐ Visitor boundaries have been discussed.
☐ Both parents know essential newborn-care information.
☐ Emotional-support contacts are available.
☐ The mother knows that she may ask for help without apology.
Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Preparing for a baby is not about predicting every difficulty. It is about ensuring that the mother and baby will not have to face every difficulty alone.
Try This Today
Sit with your partner or primary support person for fifteen minutes. Answer these three questions:

items: What will the mother need most during the first week?
Which household responsibilities can be reassigned before the birth?
Who will we contact if either the baby or mother needs additional support?

pages.chapterTitle: Becoming a Parent Before the Baby Arrives

blocks.text: Write down the answers. A plan does not need to be elaborate to make the first days feel safer.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby
pages.epigraph: “Your baby does not need a perfect nursery. Your baby needs a few safe spaces and caregivers who understand how to use them.”

blocks.text: The Room Was Beautiful—but the Safest Place Was the Simplest

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: A mother had prepared a beautiful nursery for her baby. There were cushions around the cot, a thick quilt, stuffed animals and a padded nest that promised to make the baby feel secure. Above the mattress hung a decorative mobile, and folded blankets waited nearby. Everything looked warm and comfortable. During a newborn-care appointment, she showed the nurse a photograph. The nurse gently explained that several of those beautiful items should not be inside the baby’s sleep space. The mother felt embarrassed. She had not been careless. She had tried to create the most comfortable place possible.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: The difficulty was that adult ideas of comfort do not always match infant-safety needs. For a baby, a safe sleep space often looks surprisingly empty. That emptiness is not a lack of care. It is protection.
Begin with Safety Zones, Not an Entire Perfect Home
You do not have to reorganise every cupboard before the baby arrives. Start by preparing the spaces where the baby will spend the most time:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: A clean place for bathing supplies
A place for medicines and hazardous products that children cannot access

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: As your baby begins rolling, crawling and walking, the safety needs of the home will change. A newborn cannot reach a cleaning bottle under the sink. A curious one-year-old may find it within seconds. Home safety is not one large task completed before birth. It is a plan that grows with the child.
The Safe-Sleep Space
For a healthy infant, current safe-sleep guidance consistently recommends:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Placing the baby on their back for every sleep
Using a firm, flat, non-inclined mattress
Using a fitted sheet intended for that mattress
Keeping pillows, loose bedding, quilts, cot bumpers, soft toys, nests and positioning products out of the sleep space

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Giving the baby a separate infant sleep surface in the parents’ room for at least the first six months
Keeping the baby’s face and head uncovered
Keeping the baby away from tobacco smoke and nicotine exposure
Using the same safety principles for daytime naps and nighttime sleep

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: The baby should be placed on the back at the beginning of every sleep. Once an older baby can roll independently in both directions, parents do not need to repeatedly turn the baby back, but the sleep space should remain clear and the baby should still initially be placed on the back. Individual medical circumstances may require advice from the baby’s doctor. American Academy of Pediatrics , NHS
What Does “Firm, Flat and Clear” Mean?
Firm

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: The mattress should not allow the baby’s head or face to sink deeply into it. Adult mattresses, sofas, beanbags, armchairs, cushions and padded loungers are not designed as infant sleep surfaces.
Flat

The sleeping surface should not be inclined or propped up unless a qualified clinician has given specific advice for a medical reason. Do not raise the mattress or place objects underneath it to treat ordinary spit-up or reflux without medical guidance.
Clear

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: The sleep space should contain the baby and a well-fitted sheet—not a collection of comforting objects. Keep out:

items: Strings, cords or hanging objects within reach

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Products sold for babies are not automatically safe for unsupervised sleep. Marketing words such as “breathable,” “orthopaedic,” “anti-roll” or “sleep support” do not replace evidence-based safety guidance.
The Baby’s Own Sleep Surface

The safest arrangement for a young infant is generally a separate cot, crib, bassinet or other approved infant sleep space close to the parent’s bed. Room-sharing allows caregivers to respond to the baby while keeping the sleep surface separate. The sleep product should:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Be intended and approved for infant sleep
Have a firm mattress that fits without dangerous gaps
Have no broken, loose or missing parts
Be used within the manufacturer’s age, size and weight limits
Be placed away from curtains, blind cords, electrical wires and hanging objects
Not be positioned next to a heater, open window or direct source of heat

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Read the instructions even when the product appears simple. Improvised assembly can create gaps, unstable sides or entrapment risks.
When a Baby Falls Asleep Somewhere Else
Babies often fall asleep during ordinary life:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: A car safety seat protects a baby during travel, but it is not intended to become the baby’s regular sleep space outside the vehicle. If a baby falls asleep in a sitting device, current guidance recommends moving the baby to a firm, flat, non-inclined infant sleep surface as soon as practical. American Academy of Pediatrics Never place a sleeping baby on a sofa or armchair. A tired adult may think:
“I will close my eyes while holding the baby for only a minute.”

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: But sofas and armchairs can create serious suffocation, entrapment and fall risks. If you feel yourself becoming sleepy, place the baby in the prepared sleep space or ask another alert adult to take over.
Bed-Sharing and Accidental Sleep

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Families may receive conflicting messages about sharing a bed with a baby. The safest planned place for a baby is a separate, clear infant sleep surface. However, exhausted parents sometimes fall asleep unintentionally while feeding or soothing. It is better to discuss this possibility honestly than to pretend it never happens. Never sleep with a baby on a sofa or armchair. Bed-sharing is especially dangerous when:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: The baby was born prematurely or had a low birth weight
Any adult in the bed smokes
An adult has used recreational drugs
An adult has taken medicine that causes drowsiness
Pillows, blankets or other soft items are near the baby

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Other children or pets are in the bed
The adult is unusually exhausted or difficult to wake
The baby could become trapped between surfaces

blocks.text: Discuss sleep arrangements with the baby’s paediatrician, particularly when the baby was born early, has a medical condition or the family is struggling to maintain a safe arrangement.
Swaddling Requires Care

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Some families use swaddling to settle a young baby. Swaddling is not required, and it has not been shown to prevent sudden infant death syndrome. If a family chooses to swaddle, obtain current guidance from the baby’s healthcare professional and follow the product instructions. Important precautions include:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Always placing the swaddled baby on the back
Keeping the baby’s face and head uncovered
Ensuring the swaddle is not too tight around the hips
Using secure material that cannot become loose bedding
Stopping swaddling when the baby begins showing signs of attempting to roll
Never placing a swaddled baby on the side or stomach
Avoiding weighted swaddles and weighted sleep products

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Do not continue a familiar practice after the baby’s development has made it unsafe.
Temperature and Clothing

Babies should be comfortably warm, not hot. Overheating can increase sleep-related risk. Check the baby’s chest or the back of the neck rather than judging only by the hands and feet, which may naturally feel cooler. Signs that a baby may be too warm include:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Remove hats and unnecessary outdoor layers when the baby comes indoors or enters a warm vehicle. Do not place the baby’s sleep space next to:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Choose clothing according to the room and climate. Because temperatures vary greatly across India and other regions, avoid relying on a single universal room-temperature number. Ask the baby’s healthcare professional for guidance appropriate to your climate, housing and baby’s health.
Create a Practical Feeding Area
A feeding space does not need specialised furniture. It needs to allow the caregiver to sit safely and obtain essential items without carrying the baby around unnecessarily. Useful items may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: A dim light for nighttime feeds
A phone charger placed safely away from the baby
Medicines or feeding equipment only when properly stored
A notebook or app for recording information when medically useful

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Keep hot drinks away from the baby. A newborn’s sudden movement can spill tea or coffee that appeared to be safely out of reach. If you use bottles or expressed milk, organise a clean area for preparation and storage. Follow current professional guidance for cleaning, sterilising, preparing formula and storing breast milk. Those details will be covered in Chapter 4.
Feeding Chairs and Sofas
Comfort matters, but so does the possibility of falling asleep. Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Can I sit and stand while holding the baby safely?
Could a tired caregiver fall asleep here?
Is the area free from loose cords?
Can another person take the baby if I become too tired?

blocks.text: Avoid placing the baby unattended on an adult bed or sofa, even if the baby is not yet rolling. The first roll is not always announced in advance.
Build a Safer Changing Area

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Babies can move suddenly. A newborn who appears almost motionless may push, twist or startle unexpectedly. A changing surface should therefore be:

items: At a comfortable height for the caregiver
Away from open windows, cords and heaters
Supplied before the baby is placed on it
Used with one hand kept on the baby whenever the surface is raised

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Never leave a baby unattended on a changing table, bed, sofa or other raised surface—not even to collect a nappy from across the room. If you need to step away, take the baby with you or place the baby safely on the floor or in the prepared cot.
What to Keep Nearby

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Any barrier cream recommended for the baby

blocks.text: Keep creams, powders, medicines and small objects out of the baby’s reach. Avoid shaking powder near a baby’s face, as fine particles may be inhaled.
Bathing: Prepare Before the Baby Enters the Water

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: A baby can drown silently and in very little water. Before beginning a bath, collect:
During the bath:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Keep one hand supporting the baby.
Keep hot water sources out of reach.
Do not carry hot water while carrying the baby.
Never leave the baby alone in water.
Do not rely on an older child to supervise.
Do not step away to answer a phone or door.
Empty the bath promptly after use.

blocks.text: Bath seats and supportive products do not replace adult supervision. If you must leave, take the baby with you.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Safe Transport Begins with the First Journey
The journey home from the hospital should be planned before the birth. A baby should travel in an age- and size-appropriate child restraint that:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Is installed according to the manufacturer’s instructions
Is appropriate for the baby’s height and weight
Supports the baby without unapproved padding
Is secured correctly on every journey
Is replaced or professionally evaluated when required after a collision
Has not passed its expiry or recommended service period
Has no missing parts or unknown damage history

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: The safest position and installation method depend on the vehicle, child restraint and applicable local rules. Follow the manufacturer’s instructions and obtain help from a trained installer or road-safety professional where available. Do not hold a baby in an adult’s arms during a car journey. An adult cannot reliably protect a baby during sudden braking or a collision.
Before Using a Second-Hand Child Restraint
Confirm:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: It has not been involved in a serious collision
It has no cracks, damaged straps or missing parts
It fits the vehicle and the baby

blocks.text: If its history is unknown, it may not be possible to judge whether it remains safe.
Avoid Unapproved Additions
Do not add:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Strap covers not approved by the manufacturer
Toys attached where they may become a hazard
Any product that changes the harness fit

blocks.text: The harness should be used exactly as the child-restraint manufacturer directs.
Think Ahead to Rolling, Crawling and Walking
Your newborn will not stay in one place forever. A useful home-safety plan has stages.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Stage 1 — Before Rolling
Focus on:

items: Falls from beds and changing surfaces

blocks.text: Stage 2 — Before Crawling
Get down to floor level and look for:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Objects that can be pulled down

blocks.text: Stage 3 — Before Walking and Climbing
Review:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Access to keys and external doors

blocks.text: Do not wait for the child to demonstrate a dangerous ability before preparing for it.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Anchor Furniture and Heavy Objects
Children pull on furniture when learning to stand and climb when exploring. Tall or heavy items may tip if unsecured, including:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Secure suitable furniture and televisions according to manufacturer guidance. Keep heavy items on lower shelves and avoid placing desirable objects on top of furniture that may encourage climbing. A closed drawer is not necessarily a locked drawer.
Medicines and Household Chemicals
Store medicines, vitamins, cleaning products, pesticides, toiletries and other hazardous substances:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Without transferring them to drink bottles or food containers

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Do not describe medicine as sweets. Children learn through imitation. If they regularly watch an adult taking tablets, those tablets may become especially interesting. Ask every regular caregiver—including grandparents—how medicines are stored in their home. A child-safe home must extend to the places the child visits.
Small Objects, Batteries and Magnets
Young children explore with their mouths. Items that can create serious harm include:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Nuts and hard foods inappropriate for age

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Button batteries and multiple high-powered magnets require particular caution because swallowing them can cause severe internal injury. Keep such items locked away, inspect battery compartments and seek urgent medical assistance if ingestion is possible. Do not wait for symptoms.
Cords, Strings and Window Coverings

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Keep blind cords, curtain cords, charging cables and other loops away from sleep and play spaces. Do not place a cot or climbable furniture beside a window. Check clothing, toys and sleep products for cords or attachments that could create strangulation risks. Decorative items should never hang within a baby’s reach.
Pets and Babies
A much-loved pet may still respond unpredictably to a newborn’s sounds and movements. Before the baby arrives:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Prepare the pet for changing routines.
Introduce baby sounds and equipment gradually.
Decide who will exercise and care for the pet after the birth.

blocks.text: After the baby arrives:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Never leave a baby alone with a pet.
Do not force the animal to approach.
Keep feeding and sleeping areas separate.
Watch for signs of fear, guarding or agitation.
Seek professional behavioural help when concerned.

blocks.text: Supervision means an alert adult is close enough to intervene—not watching from another room through a camera.
Older Children and the New Baby

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: An older sibling may want to carry, feed or comfort the baby. Encourage affection while keeping responsibility with adults. Teach the older child:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: To sit down before holding the baby
To ask an adult before picking the baby up
Not to place food or objects in the baby’s mouth
Not to enter the baby’s sleep space
To call an adult when the baby cries
That babies should never be shaken
That an adult remains responsible even when the older child is “helping”

blocks.text: Avoid making the older child responsible for preventing accidents. Children can participate without becoming substitute parents.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Hygiene Without Trying to Create a Germ-Free World
Newborns need reasonable protection from infection, but families do not need to sterilise every surface in the home. Useful habits include:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Washing hands before handling the baby when needed
Keeping pets away from feeding equipment
Following professional guidance for cord and skin care
Keeping routine vaccinations current according to medical advice

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Do not allow fear of germs to prevent every healthy interaction. The goal is sensible hygiene, not an impossible sterile home.
A Small-Home Safety Plan
A family does not need a large nursery to keep a baby safe. In a small or shared home:

items: Use a compact approved infant sleep space.
Store baby supplies vertically or in closed containers.
Separate medicines and cleaning products from food.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Create one dependable changing station, even if it is a mat on the floor.
Keep pathways clear during nighttime care.
Use a dim light to reduce trips and falls.
Discuss quiet and visitor arrangements with household members.
Establish which adult is responsible for the baby at any moment.

blocks.text: Safety comes from arrangements and habits, not from the number of rooms.
Common Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Assuming a product is safe because it is marketed for babies
Filling the cot with soft bedding or decorative objects
Allowing routine sleep in swings, sitting devices or adult beds
Placing the baby on the side or stomach for sleep without medical instruction
Leaving the baby unattended on a raised surface
Holding a baby in a moving vehicle
Adding unapproved padding to a child restraint
Preparing a bath before collecting everything needed

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Storing medicines in handbags, bedside drawers or low cupboards
Waiting until the baby crawls before securing the home
Leaving a baby alone with a pet or young sibling
Assuming a baby monitor replaces supervision
Making the home so complicated that exhausted caregivers cannot follow the safety plan consistently

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: A sleep product promises to prevent sudden infant death.
A device claims that monitoring alone makes an unsafe sleep arrangement safe.
A mattress or positioner promises to treat reflux without a doctor’s involvement.
A second-hand child restraint has no reliable history.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: A caregiver insists on placing the baby on the stomach because the baby “sleeps better.”
An exhausted adult is repeatedly falling asleep with the baby on a sofa or chair.
A product requires extra pillows, rolled towels or unapproved padding to support the baby.
Safety advice depends entirely on testimonials rather than recognised standards and professional guidance.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Home cardiorespiratory monitors should not be used as a substitute for safe-sleep practices unless a clinician has prescribed monitoring for a specific medical reason. American Academy of Pediatrics
Your Home-Preparation Checklist
Sleep
☐ The baby has a separate, firm and flat infant sleep surface.
☐ The mattress fits correctly.
☐ The sleep space contains no pillows, toys, bumpers or loose bedding.
☐ Every caregiver knows to place the baby on the back.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: ☐ The sleep space is away from cords, curtains and heat sources.
☐ The family has discussed what to do if a caregiver becomes sleepy while feeding.
Changing and bathing
☐ Changing supplies are within the caregiver’s reach.
☐ The baby will never be left unattended on a raised surface.
☐ Bath supplies can be collected before the bath begins.
☐ Hot-water and burn risks have been reviewed.
☐ Medicines and creams are stored safely.
Travel
☐ An appropriate child restraint has been obtained.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: ☐ The family knows how to install and use it.
☐ No unapproved padding has been added.
☐ The first journey home has been planned.
☐ All regular caregivers understand that the baby must be restrained on every journey.
Household
☐ Medicines and cleaning products are inaccessible.
☐ Button batteries, magnets and small objects are secured.
☐ Heavy furniture and televisions have been assessed.
☐ Cords and strings are away from sleep and play areas.
☐ Pet interactions will always be supervised.
☐ The home will be reviewed again before rolling, crawling and walking begin.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

blocks.text: Remember This
A safe home is not the one with the most baby products. It is the one in which a few important safety practices are understood and followed by every caregiver.
Try This Today
Choose the place where your baby is expected to sleep. Remove everything except the approved mattress and fitted sheet. Then ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: Is the surface firm, flat and intended for infant sleep?
Is it away from cords, curtains and heat?
Does every caregiver know that the baby should be placed on the back?
What will we do if the person feeding the baby becomes too sleepy to remain alert?

blocks.text: One safe space, prepared carefully, is more valuable than an entire perfect nursery.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safe and Practical Home for Your Baby

items: American Academy of Pediatrics: Safe Sleep
American Academy of Pediatrics: Updated Recommendations for Reducing Sleep-Related Infant Deaths
NHS: Sudden Infant Death Syndrome and Safer Sleep
World Health Organization: Making Sure Newborns and Children Under Five Sleep Safely

blocks.text: Safety standards and product rules can change. Before publication, verify all edition-specific guidance against current Indian health, consumer-safety and road-transport requirements.

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another
pages.epigraph: “You are not expected to understand every sound your baby makes. You will learn one another gradually.”

blocks.text: The First Night at Home Felt Different

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: At the hospital, a mother had felt surrounded by help. If the baby made an unfamiliar sound, she could call a nurse. If feeding felt difficult, someone could watch. If the baby seemed unusually sleepy, a professional was nearby. Then the family came home. That evening, the baby sneezed several times. The mother wondered whether the baby was ill. A little later, the baby’s breathing seemed fast, then slow, then irregular. She watched the baby’s chest and worried again. During a nappy change, she noticed a dark, sticky stool unlike anything she had expected.

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: By midnight, she had searched for so many answers online that she no longer knew which advice to believe. The next morning, the paediatrician listened carefully and examined the baby. Several things the mother had noticed were ordinary newborn behaviours. Others were worth monitoring. The paediatrician told her:
“You do not have to diagnose your baby. Your job is to notice, respond and ask when you are unsure.”

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: That sentence gave her something no internet search had provided. A clear role. You are not expected to know what every sound, movement or colour means. You are learning what is usual for your baby while remaining alert to changes that deserve professional attention.
A Newborn Is Adjusting to an Entirely New World
Before birth, the baby’s environment was warm, dim and constant. After birth, the baby must adjust to:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Moving without the boundaries of the womb
Sleeping and waking in a new environment

blocks.text: Many newborn behaviours appear unfamiliar because they are unfamiliar to the parents—not necessarily because something is wrong. At the same time, newborns can become unwell quickly. Families therefore need two things:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Clear guidance about when to seek care

blocks.text: The aim is attentive observation, not constant fear.
What Newborn Behaviour May Look Like
A healthy newborn may:

items: Sleep for much of the day and night

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Turn toward touch near the mouth
Breathe somewhat irregularly for short periods
Cry when hungry, cold, uncomfortable or overstimulated

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Have hands and feet that appear cooler than the chest
Have temporarily swollen breasts or genitals due to maternal hormones
Pass dark, sticky first stools called meconium

blocks.text: Individual babies vary. A baby born prematurely, with a low birth weight or after a medical complication may have different care needs. Follow the baby’s discharge plan rather than comparing the baby with a general description.
Learn Your Baby’s Baseline

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: A baseline is what is ordinary for your baby. You gradually learn:

items: How strongly the baby usually feeds
The baby’s usual breathing when calm
What the baby’s stools usually look like
How the baby responds to touch and comfort

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: You do not need to measure everything repeatedly. The purpose of learning the baseline is to recognise a meaningful change. For example:
“My baby sleeps a great deal” may be ordinary.
But:
“My baby is much harder to wake than yesterday and is not feeding normally” deserves prompt professional attention.
The change matters.
Understanding Newborn Cues
Babies communicate before they cry. Early feeding cues may include:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Moving the head from side to side
Turning toward touch near the cheek

blocks.text: Crying is often a later cue. A baby may also communicate tiredness or overstimulation by:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Crying despite being fed and changed

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Responding to cues does not mean you will always interpret them correctly. Sometimes you will offer milk when the baby needs sleep. Sometimes you will try to settle a baby who needs a nappy change. Parenting is not a test in which every first answer must be correct. Try something safe, observe the response and adjust.
Feeding in the First Days
Newborns generally feed frequently because their stomachs are small and they are growing rapidly. The feeding pattern depends on:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: The baby’s ability to latch, suck and swallow

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: A breastfed newborn may feed eight to twelve times in twenty-four hours, sometimes in clusters. A formula-fed baby may follow a somewhat different pattern. Your baby’s healthcare professional should explain what is appropriate for your baby. Do not judge feeding only by the length of a feed. A more useful assessment considers:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Whether the baby wakes and shows feeding cues
Whether sucking and swallowing are effective
Whether the baby appears satisfied after at least some feeds
Whether urine and stool patterns are developing appropriately
Whether feeding is painful or persistently difficult
Whether the baby repeatedly coughs, chokes or struggles to breathe during feeds

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Chapter 4 will explore feeding in detail. For now, remember that persistent feeding difficulty deserves early support. Do not wait until the mother is in severe pain or the baby has become unusually sleepy.
Waking a Sleepy Newborn to Feed
Some newborns wake reliably when hungry. Others may need to be woken, particularly when:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: They have a low birth weight
A clinician has given a specific feeding schedule

blocks.text: Follow the discharge instructions from the baby’s healthcare team. If the baby is consistently difficult to wake or too sleepy to feed effectively, seek medical advice promptly. Do not assume that a very quiet baby is simply an “easy baby.”

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Wet Nappies and Stools Give Useful Information

Nappy patterns are one way to assess whether feeding and hydration may be progressing. In the first days, wet nappies usually increase as milk intake increases. The exact number can vary with the baby’s age and feeding circumstances. Your maternity or paediatric team should explain what they expect each day. Rather than memorising one number for every baby, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: How many wet nappies should we expect today?
When should we call if the baby has not passed urine?
What stool changes should we expect?
Does our baby have any reason to need closer monitoring?

blocks.text: Early stools
The first stool is usually meconium, which is dark, thick and sticky. As feeding continues, stool commonly changes:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: From black or very dark meconium
To greenish or brown transitional stool
To a lighter pattern influenced by the baby’s feeding method

blocks.text: Breastfed babies often develop yellow, loose or seedy stools. Formula-fed babies may have tan, yellow or brown stools with a different consistency. Colour and pattern can vary, but contact a healthcare professional promptly if stools are:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Accompanied by illness, repeated vomiting or poor feeding

blocks.text: A very pale stool may indicate that bile is not reaching the intestine and requires medical assessment.
Jaundice: Common but Important to Monitor

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Jaundice causes yellowing of the skin or the whites of the eyes. It happens when bilirubin builds up in the blood. Many newborns develop some jaundice during the first days because the liver is still becoming efficient at removing bilirubin. Most cases are mild. Some require monitoring or treatment. Because high bilirubin levels can become dangerous, jaundice should not be diagnosed or managed only by looking at photographs or pressing the baby’s skin. Contact the baby’s healthcare professional if:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Jaundice appears during the first twenty-four hours
The abdomen, arms or legs appear yellow
The whites of the eyes are yellow
The baby is difficult to wake
The baby is not feeding well
Stool is pale, white or grey

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: The baby has another sign of illness
The family was told the baby has a higher risk of significant jaundice
A follow-up bilirubin check was recommended

blocks.text: Jaundice can be more difficult to recognise in babies with darker skin. Look at the whites of the eyes and gums, and follow the baby’s scheduled bilirubin assessments. Do not use direct sunlight as a home treatment for jaundice. It does not provide a controlled or reliable substitute for medical assessment and prescribed phototherapy. American Academy of Pediatrics

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Breathing: What to Observe

A newborn’s breathing may sometimes appear irregular when the baby is calm or sleeping. Brief pauses can occur, followed by several quicker breaths. However, parents should not try to decide alone whether difficult breathing is normal. Observe the baby when calm. Seek urgent medical care if you notice:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: The skin pulling inward beneath or between the ribs
Blue, grey or unusually pale colour around the lips, face or tongue
The baby becoming floppy or unresponsive
A sudden and concerning change from the baby’s normal pattern

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: If you believe the baby is not breathing or is having a life-threatening emergency, contact emergency services immediately and begin emergency care if trained. Do not wait for an online reply.
Sneezing, Hiccups and Noisy Sleep

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Newborns may sneeze to clear their small nasal passages. Hiccups are also common. During sleep, babies may grunt briefly, squeak, stretch, startle or make sucking movements. These sounds can be surprising. The important question is not whether the baby makes noise. It is whether the baby appears comfortable, maintains a normal colour, feeds appropriately and breathes without persistent effort. A video recorded safely may help a doctor understand an intermittent sound. Do not delay urgent assessment to make a recording.
Newborn Skin and Appearance

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Common temporary findings may include:

items: Small white spots on the face
A small amount of vaginal discharge in a newborn girl
Minor scratches from the baby’s fingernails

blocks.text: Your baby’s healthcare professional should examine anything you do not recognise. Seek prompt advice for:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Increasing redness around the umbilical cord
A rash accompanied by fever or unusual sleepiness
Spots that look like bleeding beneath the skin
Blue, grey or very pale colouring
Any sudden change that concerns you

blocks.text: Photographs can be useful for documenting change, but they do not replace examination when a newborn may be unwell.

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Caring for the Umbilical Cord
The cord stump gradually dries and separates. Follow the instructions given by the maternity team. General guidance includes:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Keeping the stump clean and dry
Washing hands before touching the area
Folding the nappy away from the stump when practical
Allowing the stump to separate naturally
Not applying oils, powders, ash, herbs, ointments or other substances unless specifically advised by a qualified clinician

blocks.text: A small spot of blood around the time the stump separates can occur. Contact a healthcare professional if there is:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Redness spreading onto the surrounding skin
Any concern that the baby appears unwell

blocks.text: Keeping the Baby Warm—Without Overheating

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Newborns can lose heat more easily than older children and adults. Skin-to-skin contact with an alert caregiver can help maintain warmth and support bonding. The baby should be observed and positioned so the face remains visible and the airway stays clear. Dress the baby for the environment rather than applying a fixed number of layers in every climate. Check the chest or back of the neck. Seek medical advice when a newborn feels unusually hot or cold, particularly if accompanied by poor feeding, unusual sleepiness, breathing difficulty or abnormal behaviour.

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Do not rely only on touch when you suspect fever. Use a suitable thermometer and follow the baby’s medical team’s instructions.
The First Bath Can Wait

A newborn does not need frequent bathing. The World Health Organization advises delaying the first bath for at least twenty-four hours after birth where possible, helping protect warmth and allowing early contact and feeding. World Health Organization At home:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Avoid strong fragrances and unnecessary products.

blocks.text: On many days, cleaning the face, neck folds and nappy area is enough.
Bonding May Be Immediate—or Gradual

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Some parents feel an intense bond the moment they see their baby. Others feel relief, protectiveness, responsibility, exhaustion or even emotional distance. Bonding can grow through repeated ordinary moments:

items: Learning what helps the baby settle

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: There is no requirement to feel one dramatic emotion at one particular moment. However, persistent detachment, severe anxiety, hopelessness or frightening thoughts deserve compassionate professional support. The mother’s mental health is part of the baby’s care.
Skin-to-Skin Contact
When the parent is awake, alert and safely positioned, skin-to-skin contact can support warmth, feeding and connection. Safe skin-to-skin contact means:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: The adult is awake and responsive.
The nose and mouth remain unobstructed.
The neck is not bent sharply forward.
The baby is supported from falling.
Another adult is available when the parent is exhausted or medically unwell.
The baby is moved to a safe sleep space before the adult sleeps.

blocks.text: Skin-to-skin should never continue unsupervised when the adult may become drowsy.

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Accepting Help Without Handing Over Your Confidence

In many families, experienced relatives arrive ready to help. Their knowledge may be valuable. But experience does not make every old practice safe or suitable for every baby. You can listen respectfully and still ask:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Is this consistent with current medical guidance?
Is this appropriate for our baby?
What problem is it intended to solve?
Can we wait until we understand it better?

blocks.text: A calm response might be:
“Thank you. We are following the baby’s doctor’s guidance for this.”
You do not have to enter a long debate during recovery.
Follow-Up Care Is Part of Newborn Care

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: The baby may appear healthy at discharge and still need timely follow-up. Early appointments may assess:

items: The mother’s recovery and emotional wellbeing

blocks.text: Before leaving the birth facility, confirm:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: When should the baby be examined again?
Where will the appointment take place?
Were all newborn screening tests completed?
Which vaccinations or preventive treatments were given?
What is the next vaccination date?
Whom should we contact after hours?
Does our baby need additional monitoring?

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Keep discharge papers and screening results together.
Register and Organise Important Records
Create one physical or digital file containing:

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Feeding plan when one has been provided

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Do not share confidential medical documents casually in large messaging groups. Send information only through appropriate, trusted channels.
RED FLAG: A Newborn Who Seems Unwell Needs Prompt Attention
Seek urgent medical assessment if a newborn:

items: Is not feeding or suddenly feeds much less effectively
Is unusually floppy, weak or inactive
Has a seizure or repeated abnormal jerking

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Has difficult, persistently fast or laboured breathing
Grunts or pulls in beneath the ribs while breathing
Becomes blue, grey, very pale or unusually mottled
Has a fever or an abnormally low temperature
Feels unusually hot or cold and appears unwell
Has repeated vomiting, especially green vomit
Has significant diarrhoea or blood in the stool
Shows signs of dehydration or has much less urine than expected

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Has redness spreading from the umbilical cord
Has a rapidly spreading or non-fading rash
Looks or behaves markedly differently from normal
Causes the parent to feel that something is seriously wrong

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: For a newborn, do not give fever medicine or home remedies before obtaining appropriate medical advice. The exact route—paediatrician, hospital emergency department or local emergency service—depends on the severity and your healthcare system. Make that plan before you need it. The World Health Organization identifies poor feeding, reduced activity, difficult breathing, fever, convulsions and cold skin among important newborn danger signs requiring prompt care. World Health Organization
Trust Concern, but Avoid Constant Testing

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Parents are often told to “trust their instincts.” That advice can be useful, but it needs context. Instinct does not mean you must know the diagnosis. It may simply mean noticing:

items: “My baby is not waking as usual.”
“I need someone qualified to assess this.”

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: At the same time, anxiety can make every ordinary movement feel dangerous. If worry is preventing you from sleeping even when the baby is safely cared for, causing repeated checking or making daily care feel impossible, tell a healthcare professional. Supporting the parent’s anxiety is also part of protecting the baby.
A Simple Newborn Observation Page
Use this only when helpful or requested by the baby’s healthcare team. Date: ______________________ Baby’s age today: ______________________
Feeding

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Approximate number of feeds: ______________________ Feeding method: __________________________________ Any difficulty with latch, sucking, swallowing or bottle feeding: _________________________________________________
Nappies
Behaviour

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Baby wakes for feeds: Yes / No / Sometimes Baby seems alert for short periods: Yes / No / Unsure Baby settles at least sometimes: Yes / No / Unsure Change from yesterday: _________________________________________________
Health

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Temperature, if measured: __________________________ Skin or eye colour concern: _________________________ Breathing concern: _________________________________ Cord concern: ____________________________________ Question for the healthcare professional: _________________________________________________ Do not allow recording to replace responding. If the baby appears unwell, seek care rather than completing the page.
Common Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Assuming a quiet baby is always a content baby
Waiting several days to ask about persistent feeding difficulty
Judging milk intake only by how long the baby feeds
Comparing nappy patterns without considering the baby’s age

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Treating jaundice with sunlight instead of obtaining medical guidance
Applying traditional substances to the cord
Giving medicine or home remedies without professional advice
Allowing an exhausted adult to remain alone during skin-to-skin contact

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Missing scheduled follow-up because the baby appears well
Searching repeatedly online instead of contacting the baby’s clinician
Dismissing a mother’s concern because she is a first-time parent
Expecting the mother to remember every discharge instruction without written support

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: Someone tells you not to seek care because newborns are “always sleepy.”
A person recommends honey, herbal mixtures, gripe preparations or other substances without the paediatrician’s approval.
Jaundice is dismissed without assessing feeding, age and bilirubin risk.
A baby is repeatedly difficult to wake but is described only as “good.”

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

items: A practitioner promises to diagnose a newborn entirely through messages or photographs.
You are advised to delay urgent care while trying a home remedy.
Someone discourages follow-up testing that the baby’s medical team requested.
A commercial monitor is presented as a replacement for observing the baby and following safe-care guidance.

blocks.text: Your First-Days Checklist
Before leaving the birth facility

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: ☐ We know when the baby must be examined again.
☐ Feeding has been observed or discussed.
☐ We understand the baby’s jaundice follow-up.
☐ Newborn screening and pending results are documented.
☐ Vaccination information is recorded.
☐ We know whom to contact after hours.
☐ We have written newborn danger signs.
☐ The mother’s follow-up and warning signs are also understood.
At home
☐ The baby sleeps on the back in a safe sleep space.
☐ We are monitoring feeding without becoming obsessed with exact perfection.

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: ☐ We know what nappy pattern is expected for the baby’s current age.
☐ The cord is being kept clean and dry.
☐ The baby is kept comfortably warm without overheating.
☐ At least one other adult knows the baby’s essential information.
☐ Medical records are stored together.
☐ We will seek help promptly if the baby changes or appears unwell.
Remember This
You do not need to diagnose your newborn. Notice what is usual, respond to changes and seek qualified help whenever feeding, breathing, temperature, colour or alertness concerns you.

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Try This Today
Create a contact card and place it near the baby’s records. Write:
Then ask every primary caregiver:
“If the baby appears seriously unwell tonight, do we know exactly whom to call and where to go?”

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: A clear plan can save precious time.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

items: National Health Mission, Government of India: Child-Health and Home-Based Newborn-Care Guidelines
National Health Mission: Facility-Based Newborn Care Operational Guidelines
World Health Organization: Caring for Newborns
World Health Organization: Essential Newborn Care
American Academy of Pediatrics: Jaundice in Newborns

pages.chapterTitle: The First Days: Getting to Know One Another

blocks.text: Medical guidance and national programmes can change. All newborn danger signs, vaccination information and follow-up recommendations should be checked again against current Indian guidance before publication.
PART II
Understanding Your Baby’s Needs
Practical guidance, thoughtful reassurance and clear next steps.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt
pages.epigraph: “A feeding plan should nourish the baby and support the mother—not turn every feed into a judgement of her love.”

blocks.text: She Thought Every Feed Was a Test

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: A new mother had planned to breastfeed. Before the baby arrived, people told her it would be natural. She assumed that natural meant easy. After the birth, the baby struggled to latch. Feeds took a long time, her nipples became painful and the baby often seemed sleepy. One relative told her to continue trying without complaining. Another insisted that the baby needed formula immediately. A third warned her that introducing a bottle would ruin everything. The mother was no longer thinking clearly about what her baby needed.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: She was thinking about whether everyone believed she was a good mother. During a feeding consultation, a qualified professional watched the baby feed, reviewed the baby’s weight and nappy pattern, and helped the family make a short-term plan. The mother later said:
“The most helpful thing she did was stop treating feeding like a moral decision.”

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Feeding is important. So is the way we speak to mothers about it. Breast milk offers important health benefits, and skilled support can help many families establish breastfeeding. When breast milk is unavailable, insufficient or not the family’s chosen feeding method, correctly prepared infant formula can provide appropriate nutrition. Some families breastfeed. Some express milk. Some use formula. Some combine methods. Some feeding plans change over time. The aim of this chapter is not to rank mothers. It is to help families feed babies safely, recognise when feeding is going well and seek help early when it is not.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: What Babies Need During the First Six Months

The World Health Organization recommends exclusive breastfeeding for the first six months, followed by nutritionally adequate complementary foods while breastfeeding continues up to two years or beyond when mutually desired. Exclusive breastfeeding means that the baby receives breast milk without additional food or drink, including water, except for medically indicated medicines, vitamins or oral rehydration products. World Health Organization

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: When a baby is not receiving breast milk, a commercially manufactured infant formula appropriate to the baby’s age and medical needs is generally used under professional guidance. During the first six months, babies ordinarily should not receive:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Animal milk as the main drink
Formula intended for an older child
Any medicine or supplement not appropriately advised

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Hot climates do not automatically mean that a young exclusively breastfed baby needs water. Breast milk provides both food and fluid. A formula-fed baby should also receive formula mixed exactly as directed—not additional water intended to quench thirst. Individual medical circumstances can differ. Follow the baby’s paediatrician when a specialised feeding plan is required.
Feeding Is a Relationship, Not Only a Volume
Feeding provides nutrition, but it also gives the baby repeated experiences of:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Learning that fullness cues will be respected

blocks.text: A responsive feeding relationship can develop through breastfeeding or bottle-feeding. Responsive feeding means:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Offering food before the baby becomes extremely distressed when possible
Avoiding pressure to finish a fixed amount
Adjusting when the baby appears overwhelmed

blocks.text: The caregiver remains responsible for offering milk safely. The baby communicates whether to continue, pause or stop.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Recognising Hunger Cues
Early hunger cues may include:

items: Moving the head from side to side
Turning toward touch near the cheek

blocks.text: Later hunger cues may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: A crying baby may need calming before feeding effectively. Crying does not always mean hunger. Babies may also cry because of:

items: A desire to suck for comfort

blocks.text: Offer feeding when hunger is possible, but observe the baby’s response.
Recognising Fullness Cues

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: A baby may show fullness by:

items: Pushing the bottle away when developmentally able
Becoming distracted after an effective feed

blocks.text: Do not repeatedly push the teat back into the baby’s mouth after clear fullness cues. A bottle does not need to be finished simply because milk remains.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Breastfeeding: What “Natural” Does Not Tell You
Breastfeeding is a biological process, but it is also a skill learned by the baby and mother. It may require:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Time for milk production to increase
Monitoring the baby’s intake and weight

blocks.text: Difficulty does not mean the mother’s body has failed. It means the feeding process needs assessment.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Early Breastfeeding Patterns

Newborns commonly breastfeed frequently—often eight to twelve times in twenty-four hours, including at night. Some feeds are close together. This is often called cluster feeding. The baby may feed several times within a short period and then sleep for a somewhat longer stretch. Frequent feeding can help stimulate milk production, but frequency alone does not prove that milk transfer is effective. A baby may remain at the breast for a long time without obtaining enough milk if attachment, sucking or swallowing is ineffective.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Assessment should consider the whole picture:
What a Comfortable, Effective Latch May Look Like
During an effective breastfeed:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: The baby’s body is turned toward the mother.
The head and body are aligned rather than twisted.
The chin approaches or touches the breast.
The lips are turned outward rather than tucked in.
The baby takes more than the nipple into the mouth.
Swallowing can be seen or heard after milk begins flowing.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: The mother feels pulling or tugging rather than persistent pinching pain.
The nipple does not emerge severely flattened, creased or damaged.

blocks.text: Breasts and nipples vary, and there is no single perfect position. The key questions are:
Is the baby transferring milk effectively?
and:
Is feeding reasonably comfortable and sustainable for the mother?
Pain Should Be Assessed, Not Praised

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Some nipple tenderness can occur during early adjustment, but severe or persistent pain should not be treated as proof that the mother is working hard enough. Seek skilled support for:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: A baby who repeatedly slips off
Pain that makes the mother dread every feed

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Possible causes include positioning difficulties, ineffective latch, engorgement, inflammation, infection, milk-flow problems, pumping injury or anatomical and medical factors. The solution depends on the cause. “Just continue” is not a complete assessment.
Milk Production and Milk Transfer Are Different

Milk production refers to how much milk the breast produces. Milk transfer refers to how much milk the baby actually removes. A mother may produce milk while the baby struggles to transfer it because of:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Conversely, a mother may have concerns about milk production even when the baby is transferring milk and growing appropriately. That is why a complete feeding assessment should include both mother and baby.
Does Breast Size Determine Milk Supply?

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: No. Breast size largely reflects fatty tissue, not simply milk-producing capacity. Small breasts do not automatically produce less milk. Large breasts do not guarantee abundant production. What matters is the interaction of milk-producing tissue, hormonal response, effective and frequent milk removal, maternal health, and the baby’s feeding ability.
Colostrum Is the First Milk

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt
