blocks.text: Colostrum is produced in small quantities during the first days. It is concentrated and suited to the newborn’s small stomach. Families sometimes mistake the small visible quantity for “nothing.” This can create unnecessary panic. However, confidence in colostrum should not be used to dismiss signs that the baby is not feeding effectively. Monitor:
Reassurance and monitoring belong together.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: How to Know Whether a Breastfed Baby Is Getting Enough
You cannot usually measure intake directly at the breast. Look at several indicators. A well-fed newborn generally:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Appears satisfied after at least some feeds
Produces progressively more wet nappies during the first days
Has stools that transition away from meconium
Is alert during at least some periods
Begins regaining weight after the expected early change
Follows an appropriate growth pattern over time

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: By approximately days five to seven, many adequately fed newborns have six or more pale wet nappies in twenty-four hours, but the baby’s age and individual circumstances matter. Weight assessment by a healthcare professional is more reliable than parental guesswork alone. American Academy of Pediatrics Contact the baby’s clinician promptly if:

items: Wet nappies are not increasing as expected
Meconium is not transitioning as expected
The baby is hard to wake

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: The baby repeatedly falls asleep before feeding effectively
Swallowing is rarely seen or heard
Weight loss is greater than the clinical team expected
The baby has not begun regaining weight according to the follow-up plan
The mother believes milk is not transferring

blocks.text: Weight Changes Need Context

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Many newborns lose some weight after birth before beginning to regain it. The percentage, timing and significance should be interpreted by a paediatric or maternity professional who considers:
Do not use one internet percentage to decide that every baby is safe or unsafe. Attend the planned weight checks.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Expressing Milk
Milk may be expressed by hand or with a pump for reasons such as:

items: Feeding a baby who cannot latch effectively
Donating milk through an approved programme

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: A feeding or lactation professional can help the mother choose an appropriate technique.
Pumping basics

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Ensure the flange size is appropriate.
Avoid increasing suction to the point of pain.
Store milk in clean, suitable containers.
Label milk with the date and relevant information.
Follow current storage guidance from the healthcare team.
Discard milk when required by the applicable storage rules.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: More suction does not necessarily produce more milk. Pain, bruising or nipple injury indicates that technique or equipment may need adjustment.
Donor Human Milk

For some vulnerable or hospitalised babies, screened donor human milk from an established milk bank may be considered according to medical guidance and availability. Informal milk sharing carries uncertainties about:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Families considering donor milk should discuss regulated milk-bank options with the baby’s medical team.
Formula Feeding Is a Care Skill
Infant formula must be prepared carefully because powdered formula is not sterile and incorrect mixing can cause harm. Families should:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Use formula suitable for the baby’s age and medical needs
Use the scoop supplied with that product
Store and discard feeds according to instructions

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Never add extra powder to help a baby gain weight or sleep longer. Never add extra water to make formula last longer or treat thirst. Over-concentrated formula can burden the baby’s body and contribute to dehydration. Over-diluted formula may provide inadequate nutrition and disturb the baby’s salt balance.
Powdered Formula Is Not Sterile

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Powdered infant formula can occasionally contain harmful bacteria. The World Health Organization provides detailed guidance for safe preparation. Preparation instructions can vary according to national guidance, product type and the baby’s medical vulnerability. Follow the current instructions provided by the baby’s healthcare professional and the formula manufacturer. General principles include:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Clean and disinfect the preparation surface.
Use properly cleaned and sterilised feeding equipment as advised.
Add the exact number of level scoops specified.
Mix without touching the teat or inside of the bottle.
Cool the feed safely when necessary.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Discard unused milk according to current safety instructions.
Do not save milk left after the baby has fed from the bottle.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: WHO guidance has recommended using water no cooler than 70°C when preparing powdered formula to reduce bacterial risk, but product and national instructions may differ, and specialised formulas may have different requirements. Confirm the correct method with the baby’s paediatrician and the current product instructions rather than relying on memory. World Health Organization: Safe Preparation, Storage and Handling of Powdered Infant Formula
Do Not Use the Microwave

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Microwaves can heat milk unevenly and create hot spots that burn the baby’s mouth. If warming a prepared feed is appropriate:

items: Keep water away from the teat and bottle opening.

blocks.text: Many babies can take formula at room temperature when it has been prepared safely. Warming is not always necessary.
Water Safety Matters

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Use water that is safe for drinking and prepare it according to current local and product guidance. Do not assume that:

items: Filtered water is always safe for infant formula
Mineral water has an appropriate mineral balance
A household filter removes every relevant organism
Water from a dispenser is at the correct preparation temperature

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: If local water quality is uncertain, ask the baby’s paediatrician or public-health authority what water source and method should be used.
Choosing a Formula
A standard infant formula suitable from birth meets the needs of many formula-fed babies. Specialised products may be designed for babies with:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Do not switch repeatedly between formulas based only on advertising, social-media testimonials or ordinary newborn fussiness. Ask before changing:

items: What problem are we trying to solve?
Could the symptoms have another cause?
How long should we try the change?
When should the baby be reassessed?

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: “Premium,” “advanced” and “closest to nature” are marketing descriptions—not individual medical assessments.
Safe Bottle-Feeding
During a bottle feed:

items: Hold the baby in a supported, semi-upright position.
Keep the head aligned with the body.
Hold the bottle rather than propping it.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Watch the baby’s breathing and comfort.
Keep milk in the teat to reduce excessive air intake.
Stop when the baby shows fullness.
Never leave the baby alone with a bottle.
Do not force the baby to finish.
Avoid putting the baby flat during feeding.
Do not enlarge the teat hole yourself.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: A feed that flows too quickly may cause coughing, choking, leaking milk, distress or difficulty coordinating sucking and breathing. A flow that is too slow may cause fatigue and frustration. Use a teat appropriate to the baby’s age and feeding ability, but choose based on the baby’s response rather than age labels alone.
Paced Bottle-Feeding
Some families use paced bottle-feeding to help the baby control the flow. A general approach may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Touching the teat to the lips and allowing the baby to accept it
Holding the bottle more horizontally so milk does not pour rapidly
Watching for stress and fullness cues
Switching sides during the feed when comfortable
Ending the feed when the baby has had enough

blocks.text: Ask a feeding professional to demonstrate if your baby was born prematurely, has swallowing difficulty or becomes distressed during feeds.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Burping and Spit-Up
Babies swallow some air during feeding. Some need pauses to burp; others do not burp after every feed. You may try:

items: Holding the baby upright against your chest
Gently rubbing or patting the back
Pausing during and after the feed

blocks.text: Small amounts of milk may come back up after feeds. This is commonly called spit-up or posseting. Discuss frequent or concerning vomiting with the baby’s doctor. Seek urgent care for:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: A baby who appears seriously unwell

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Do not thicken feeds, add cereal or change the sleeping position without medical advice. Babies should still be placed on their backs for sleep unless a qualified clinician has given specific instructions for an unusual medical circumstance.
Combination Feeding
Combination feeding means using more than one feeding method, such as breastfeeding and formula, or direct breastfeeding and expressed milk. Families may combine feeds because of:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: If maintaining breast-milk production is important to the mother, reduced milk removal may reduce supply. A feeding professional can help the family plan:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: When the plan should be reviewed

blocks.text: Combination feeding does not have to be treated as an all-or-nothing failure. It is a feeding plan.
Supplementation Should Have a Clear Purpose
A baby may need supplementary milk for medical or practical reasons. When supplementation is recommended, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: How will the baby’s intake and weight be monitored?
What would allow the plan to change?
When will the baby be reassessed?

blocks.text: A clear plan protects both nutrition and parental confidence.
Feeding Twins or Multiples

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Feeding more than one baby can place significant demands on time, milk production, sleep and recovery. Support may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Individual feeding assessment for each baby
Tracking each baby’s weight and nappies separately
Another adult taking responsibility for burping and settling
Protecting the mother’s meals, hydration and rest

blocks.text: Twins may have different feeding abilities and needs. They are two babies, not one feeding project.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Medicines and Breastfeeding
Many medicines are compatible with breastfeeding, but not all medicines and situations are the same. Before taking:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: ask an appropriately qualified professional to check the specific substance, dose, timing, baby’s age and baby’s health. Do not stop an essential prescribed medicine without medical advice. Stopping maternal treatment can also create risk. Ask:

items: Is this medicine compatible with breastfeeding?
If feeding pauses, how can milk production be protected?

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Use trusted medicine-information services and the prescribing clinician rather than general internet lists alone.
Alcohol, Tobacco, Nicotine and Other Substances

Avoiding tobacco smoke and nicotine exposure protects the baby. If a parent smokes or uses nicotine, seek professional support to reduce exposure and stop where possible. Important precautions include:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Keeping the home and vehicle smoke-free
Changing contaminated outer clothing when appropriate
Never sharing a sleep surface with the baby when any caregiver smokes

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Alcohol, recreational drugs and sedating substances can affect caregiving and feeding safety. Obtain professional guidance rather than relying on timing myths. An impaired or unusually drowsy adult should not be the only person responsible for feeding or settling the baby and should never share a sleep surface with the baby.
The Mother Needs Feeding Too
Producing milk and recovering from birth require energy, fluids and rest. The mother does not need a perfect diet to produce valuable breast milk. She does need regular access to:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Family support should focus less on supervising the mother’s plate and more on ensuring that food reaches her. Avoid unnecessary dietary restrictions unless there is a medically supported reason. Many traditional foods can be enjoyed as part of a varied diet, but no single food guarantees milk production.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Low Milk Supply Should Be Assessed
A mother may suspect low supply because:

items: The baby takes milk from a bottle after breastfeeding

blocks.text: None of these signs alone proves low supply. A proper assessment considers:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: The baby’s health and oral function

blocks.text: Before using herbs, medicines or products marketed to increase milk:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Identify whether supply is genuinely low.
Check safety for mother and baby.

blocks.text: A supplement cannot correct ineffective milk transfer by itself.
When Breastfeeding Must Pause or Change
Some clinical circumstances may require temporary or permanent changes to feeding. These decisions can involve:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Inability to produce or transfer sufficient milk

blocks.text: The decision should be made with qualified professionals familiar with the specific condition. Avoid making major feeding changes because of vague advice such as:
“All antibiotics are unsafe.”
or:
“Every medicine is fine.”
Both statements are too broad.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Returning to Work
A mother who wants to continue providing breast milk may need a practical plan before returning to paid work. Consider:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Where and when milk can be expressed
The baby’s age and feeding pattern
Gradual practice before the first workday

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: The plan should be realistic. A schedule that requires impossible pumping conditions is not a supportive plan. Chapter 8 will discuss the mother’s needs and family responsibilities more broadly.
RED FLAG: Seek Prompt Feeding Support
Contact the baby’s healthcare professional promptly if:

items: The baby suddenly feeds much less
The baby is difficult to wake for feeds
The suck is weak or ineffective

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: The baby repeatedly coughs, chokes or turns blue during feeding
The baby has fewer wet nappies than expected
Stool is not progressing as expected in the early days

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Weight loss or weight gain concerns have been identified
The mother has severe pain, fever or spreading breast redness
Something has changed from the baby’s usual pattern

blocks.text: A newborn who cannot feed, is very difficult to wake, has breathing difficulty, becomes blue or grey, has a seizure or appears seriously unwell requires urgent medical care.
Common Feeding Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Waiting until the baby cries intensely before offering every feed
Assuming long feeds always mean effective milk transfer
Measuring breastfeeding success only through pump output
Forcing a bottle-fed baby to finish

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Adding extra powder to improve weight gain
Giving water, honey or herbal preparations to a young baby
Preparing bottles without following hygiene instructions
Saving milk left after a feed for later
Treating frequent feeding as proof that the mother has no milk

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: Treating formula use as proof that the mother did not try
Treating breastfeeding as more important than a baby’s monitored nutritional needs
Allowing relatives or social media to replace clinical assessment

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: A feeding product promises guaranteed milk production.
A person recommends a formula without asking the baby’s age or health.
A professional dismisses nipple damage or severe pain.
Someone tells you to dilute formula to make it last longer.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: A seller suggests a specialised formula without a clinical reason.
An adviser asks you to stop prescribed medicine without contacting the prescriber.
A person claims that every sleepy baby is simply content.
A feeding plan has no method for checking weight, hydration or jaundice.
A mother is shamed for needing supplementation.
A baby’s nutrition is being compromised to preserve a particular feeding identity.

blocks.text: Your Feeding Review

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Baby

Feeding method: __________________________________ Approximate feeds in twenty-four hours: ______________ Baby wakes independently for feeds: Yes / No / Sometimes Swallowing seen or heard: Yes / No / Unsure Baby generally appears satisfied after some feeds: Yes / No / Unsure Wet nappies appropriate for current age: Yes / No / Unsure Stools progressing appropriately: Yes / No / Unsure Jaundice concern: Yes / No / Unsure Weight follow-up date: _____________________________
Mother

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Persistent nipple or breast pain: Yes / No Cracks or bleeding: Yes / No Breast redness, fever or flu-like symptoms: Yes / No Feeding feels emotionally manageable: Yes / No / Sometimes Help needed with: _________________________________________________
Questions for a professional
Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: A successful feeding plan is not the one that earns the most approval. It is the one that safely nourishes the baby, protects the mother’s health and can be sustained with informed support.
Try This Today
Observe one complete feed without judging it. Notice:

items: One question that still needs an answer

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: If something concerns you, write the question down and take it to the baby’s paediatrician or a qualified feeding professional. You do not have to solve every feeding problem alone.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

items: World Health Organization: Breastfeeding Questions and Answers
World Health Organization: Infant and Young Child Feeding
World Health Organization: Safe Preparation, Storage and Handling of Powdered Infant Formula
National Health Mission, Government of India: IMNCI Participant Module
American Academy of Pediatrics: Breastfeeding Policy Explained
American Academy of Pediatrics: How to Tell Whether a Breastfed Baby Is Getting Enough Milk

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Baby Without Fear or Guilt

blocks.text: Formula products, preparation instructions and infant-feeding guidance can change. Before publication, verify all procedural details against current Indian public-health guidance and the instructions for products legally available in the intended market.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect
pages.epigraph: “A baby who wakes is not a badly behaved baby, and a tired parent is not a failing parent.”

blocks.text: Everyone Asked the Same Question
Whenever relatives called, they asked the new mother:
“Is the baby sleeping through the night yet?”

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: At first, she answered honestly. The baby woke every few hours. Sometimes the baby fed and returned to sleep. Sometimes settling took longer. Sometimes the baby slept well for several nights and then began waking frequently again. Each time the mother described this, someone offered a new explanation. The baby was sleeping too much during the day. The baby was not feeding enough. The baby had become too dependent on being held. The mother was responding too quickly. The mother was not responding quickly enough. Soon, she began to believe that normal night waking was evidence that she had created a problem.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Her paediatrician asked a different set of questions:

items: Is anyone in the household becoming dangerously exhausted?
Has the baby’s sleep changed suddenly alongside signs of illness?

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Those questions changed the conversation. Sleep is not only about how long the baby remains quiet at night. Healthy infant sleep involves safety, adequate total rest, development, feeding, comfort and the wellbeing of the caregivers.
Baby Sleep Is Different from Adult Sleep
Adults usually organise sleep around a long nighttime period. Newborns do not begin life with that pattern. A newborn may sleep for short periods throughout the day and night because:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: The internal body clock is still developing
Day and night are not yet clearly distinguished
The baby needs help regulating temperature, comfort and emotion

blocks.text: In the early months, waking is expected. It is not evidence that the baby is manipulative, stubborn or “spoiled.”
How Much Sleep Do Babies Need?

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Sleep needs vary. The World Health Organization gives general twenty-four-hour ranges, including naps:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: These are population-level ranges, not hourly prescriptions for every child. World Health Organization A baby may sleep somewhat more during one period and less during another. Look at:

items: Total sleep across the day and night

blocks.text: Do not judge sleep from one difficult night. Patterns are more informative than isolated moments.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Newborn Sleep: The First Three Months
Newborn sleep is often irregular. A baby may:

items: Sleep for one or two hours at a time
Become more wakeful during the evening
Need significant help to fall asleep
Sleep longer on one day and less on another
Make noises and move during active sleep

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: The goal during this stage is not to create perfect independent sleep. The priorities are:

items: Helping the baby distinguish day from night gradually
Protecting the caregivers from unsafe exhaustion

blocks.text: Safe Sleep Comes Before Convenient Sleep
Chapter 2 explained the safe-sleep environment in detail. For every sleep:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Place the baby on the back.
Use a firm, flat, non-inclined infant sleep surface.
Keep the sleep space free from pillows, loose blankets, bumpers, soft toys, nests and positioning products.
Keep the baby’s face and head uncovered.
Keep the environment free from tobacco smoke and nicotine.
Use a separate infant sleep surface in the parents’ room for at least the first six months.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Never sleep with a baby on a sofa or armchair.
Move a sleeping baby from a sitting device to an appropriate flat sleep surface as soon as practical.

blocks.text: These principles apply during daytime naps too. A nap is still sleep.
“But My Baby Sleeps Better on the Stomach”

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Many babies appear to sleep more deeply on the stomach. Deeper or longer sleep does not make the position safer. Healthy babies should be placed on their backs for every sleep during the first year unless a qualified clinician has given specific instructions for an unusual medical circumstance. Back sleeping does not increase choking risk in healthy infants, including babies with ordinary reflux. The baby’s airway anatomy and protective reflexes help protect against aspiration. American Academy of Pediatrics
Do not use side sleeping as a compromise. A baby can roll from the side onto the stomach.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: When the Baby Begins Rolling

Continue placing the baby on the back at the beginning of every sleep. Once a baby can roll independently from back to stomach and stomach to back, the baby may be allowed to remain in the position reached independently. Continue to keep the sleep space completely clear. Do not:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Continue swaddling once rolling attempts begin

blocks.text: The ability to roll changes what the baby can do. It does not make soft bedding safe.
Day and Night Develop Gradually
A newborn does not know that families prefer sleeping at night. You can gently help the baby begin distinguishing day from night.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: During the day

items: Offer age-appropriate interaction during awake periods.
Take the baby outdoors safely when appropriate.
Avoid keeping the home dark and silent for every nap.
Do not deliberately keep a tired newborn awake for long periods.

blocks.text: During the night

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Change the nappy when needed without creating more stimulation than necessary.
Return the baby to the safe sleep space after feeding and care.
Avoid screens shining brightly near the baby.

blocks.text: These habits do not force the baby to sleep through the night. They provide repeated cues that daytime and nighttime feel different.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Understand Sleep Pressure and Overtiredness

Sleep pressure is the biological need for sleep that builds while a person is awake. Too little sleep pressure may make settling difficult. Too much wakefulness can also make settling difficult because an overtired baby may become agitated. Possible tired cues include:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Rubbing the face or eyes in an older baby

blocks.text: Try beginning the settling process when early cues appear. Do not become so focused on a timetable that you stop observing the baby. A schedule is a tool. It is not the child.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Wake Windows Are Estimates, Not Medical Rules

Parents may encounter charts stating exactly how long a baby should remain awake at each age. These “wake windows” can provide rough observations, but they are not precise biological deadlines. Babies differ because of:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Use age-based suggestions as starting points. Then observe:

items: When does my baby become tired?
What happens if settling begins earlier?
What happens if the baby has been awake longer?
Is the baby alert and content?

blocks.text: Avoid repeatedly waking a sleeping baby or forcing an alert baby to sleep solely because an app says a window has ended—unless the child’s healthcare professional has provided a specific plan.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Sleepy Does Not Always Mean Well
A newborn who sleeps for long periods may appear easy to care for. But excessive sleepiness can also accompany:
Contact the baby’s healthcare professional if the baby:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Cannot remain awake long enough to feed
Is weak, floppy or less responsive
Shows a sudden change in sleep and alertness

blocks.text: Do not assume that every quiet baby is simply a good sleeper.
Night Feeding

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Young babies often need feeds during the night. Whether a baby can safely go for a longer interval depends on:
Do not stop waking a young baby for feeds simply because the baby slept longer one night. Ask the baby’s paediatrician:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Do we need to wake the baby to feed?
What is the longest interval currently appropriate?
What weight or feeding milestone would change that advice?
Which signs would mean we should resume more frequent feeding?
Does our baby have any special risk?

blocks.text: As babies grow and feed effectively, some naturally sleep for longer stretches. Others continue waking. Both patterns can occur in healthy babies.
What Does “Sleeping Through the Night” Mean?

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: The phrase sounds precise, but it is not. For one family, it means five uninterrupted hours. For another, it means eight. For a baby, sleep cycles still include brief partial waking. A child who appears to sleep through may wake and return to sleep without signalling. Do not allow the phrase to become a competition. The more useful questions are:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Is the baby getting enough total sleep?
Is the baby developing and growing?
Does the family need additional support?

blocks.text: A Bedtime Routine Is a Cue, Not a Guarantee
A simple, repeated routine can help a baby recognise that sleep is approaching. For a young baby, it might include:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Placement in the safe sleep space

blocks.text: For an older baby, it may include:
Keep the routine:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Possible for more than one caregiver to perform

blocks.text: A routine should not require an hour of complicated steps. The family should still be able to follow it on an ordinary tired evening.
Feeding to Sleep

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Many babies fall asleep while feeding, particularly during the early months. This is biologically ordinary. Feeding to sleep is not automatically a harmful habit. However, as babies grow, some families find that the child expects the same feeding conditions every time sleep cycles shift. If this is working for the family, there is no need to create a problem merely because someone else disapproves. If it is no longer sustainable, change gradually. You might:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Move the feed slightly earlier in the routine.
Ask another caregiver to settle the baby sometimes.
Introduce a song or phrase that remains consistent.
Place the baby down after feeding but before complete sleep when developmentally appropriate.
Change one sleep period at a time.
Respond differently to some wakings when feeding is not needed.

blocks.text: Do not abruptly remove every familiar form of comfort during a difficult developmental period unless there is a safety reason.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Rocking, Holding and Contact Naps

Young babies often sleep well when held because they experience warmth, movement, scent and closeness. Holding a baby does not spoil the baby. The safety issue is whether the adult remains awake and whether the baby’s airway stays clear. If the baby naps in your arms:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Ensure the nose and mouth are unobstructed.
Do not sit on a sofa or chair if you may fall asleep.
Transfer care to another alert adult when exhausted.
Place the baby in the safe sleep space before sleeping yourself.

blocks.text: Contact naps may be emotionally pleasant and practical for some families. They may be exhausting for others. You are allowed to enjoy them, change them or combine them with cot naps.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Drowsy but Awake Is an Option, Not a Commandment

Parents are often told to place babies down “drowsy but awake.” Some babies respond well to this. Others become fully alert and cry. The approach is more likely to become useful after newborn sleep begins maturing, rather than as a strict expectation from birth. You can experiment gently:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Choose a time when the baby is calm.
Pick the baby up when necessary.
Stop if the method is creating prolonged distress for everyone.

blocks.text: A technique is not a measure of parental competence.
Self-Soothing Develops with Support

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Self-soothing is not something a newborn can be forced to master. Regulation develops gradually through repeated experiences of being helped. Early co-regulation may include:
Over time, the child may begin using:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: A baby’s ability to settle varies with age, temperament, hunger, illness and development. Independence grows from a foundation of safety.
Should Parents Wait Before Responding?

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Not every sound requires immediate intervention. Babies make noises and move between sleep cycles. When the baby is safe, you can sometimes pause briefly and observe:

items: Is the baby returning to sleep?

blocks.text: Observation is different from ignoring. If the baby needs feeding, comfort, a nappy change or medical attention, respond.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Sleep Training Is Not One Single Method
“Sleep training” is used to describe many different approaches. Some involve:
Before trying any structured method, consider:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Whether night feeding is still needed
Whether the sleep environment is safe
What level of crying the family is comfortable with

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Discuss structured sleep interventions with the baby’s paediatrician, particularly for young infants, premature babies, babies with feeding or growth concerns, and children with medical or developmental conditions. No sleep programme should instruct parents to ignore:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: A cry that is suddenly unusual
A caregiver’s sense that something is wrong

blocks.text: Sleep Regressions Are Usually Changes, Not Losses
Parents often use the term “sleep regression” when a baby who had been sleeping predictably begins waking more often. Possible reasons include:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Learning to roll, sit, crawl or stand

blocks.text: The baby has not forgotten how to sleep. Development has changed what the baby is experiencing. Return to the basics:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Avoid changing five things at once.
Give the pattern time when the baby is otherwise well.

blocks.text: Naps Matter

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Daytime sleep supports development and helps prevent excessive tiredness. Newborn naps are usually irregular. With age, naps often become more predictable and gradually reduce in number. Avoid forcing a universal nap schedule. Review:

items: Whether the baby is becoming overtired
Whether the schedule fits childcare and family life
Whether a recent change is temporary

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: A nap in a cot is not automatically better than every assisted nap. The aim is safe, sufficient and sustainable rest.
Sleep During Illness
Illness may increase sleep or disrupt it. Offer comfort and follow medical advice. Seek care when sleep changes accompany:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect
Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Do not use pillows, sleep positioners or an inclined mattress to treat congestion, cough or reflux without medical guidance. Safe-sleep principles still apply during illness unless the child’s clinician provides specific instructions.
Snoring and Noisy Breathing
An occasional sound during a cold may not indicate a lasting problem. Regular loud snoring, gasping, choking sounds or pauses in breathing deserve medical discussion. Record:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Whether the child seems to struggle

blocks.text: A short video may help the clinician if recording can be done safely. Seek urgent care for active breathing difficulty or colour change.
Teething and Sleep

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Teething may cause temporary discomfort, drooling and disrupted sleep. Not every fever, severe illness or prolonged sleep disturbance should be blamed on teething. Ask a healthcare professional before using:
Avoid necklaces and other items that can create choking or strangulation risks.
Sleep Products: Ask What the Evidence Shows

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Be cautious with products claiming to:

items: Make a newborn sleep through the night
Hold the baby in a “safer” position
Recreate the womb through heavy or weighted materials

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: A baby monitor may help caregivers hear or see a baby in another room, but it does not make an unsafe sleep environment safe. Home cardiorespiratory monitors should not be used as a strategy to prevent sudden infant death unless monitoring has been prescribed for a specific medical reason.
The Parent’s Sleep Is a Safety Issue Too
Extreme sleep deprivation affects:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Risk of accidentally falling asleep with the baby

blocks.text: A family sleep plan should not focus only on changing the baby. It should also protect the adults.
Divide nighttime responsibilities
Depending on the feeding method and family circumstances, another adult may:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Protect one uninterrupted rest period for the mother

blocks.text: A breastfeeding mother may still carry most feeding responsibility, but she does not have to carry every other responsibility too.
Create shifts

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: If two adults are available, consider planned shifts. For example:

items: One adult rests earlier in the evening.
The other handles settling and non-feeding needs.
Responsibilities change after a set time.
The mother is woken for breastfeeding when needed but does not manage every nappy and resettling task.
The family reviews the plan when feeding changes.

blocks.text: The exact arrangement matters less than making responsibility visible.
Protect one rest opportunity

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: A parent may not be able to obtain a full night of sleep. A protected rest period can still help. Ask:

items: Who can hold the baby while the mother naps?
Can a visitor bring food instead of expecting conversation?
Can someone manage the morning routine?
Can the parent avoid driving after a dangerously poor night?
Can the family purchase or arrange practical support?
Is anxiety preventing sleep even when the baby is safely cared for?

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: When the Parent Cannot Sleep
Sometimes the baby is asleep, another adult is available and the mother still cannot rest. Possible reasons include:

items: Fear that the baby will stop breathing

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Persistent inability to sleep, especially with agitation, confusion, unusual beliefs, hallucinations or thoughts of harm, requires urgent professional attention. Do not assume every severe sleep problem is simply part of motherhood.
A Gentle Sleep Review
Complete this over three ordinary days if sleep is becoming difficult.
Baby

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Sleep environment

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Baby placed on back: Yes / No Firm, flat infant sleep surface: Yes / No Sleep space clear: Yes / No Room shared with caregiver: Yes / No Smoke-free environment: Yes / No Baby appears too hot: Yes / No / Unsure
Parents

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Who handles each waking? ___________________________ Longest rest period for the mother: ___________________ Longest rest period for the other caregiver: ____________ What feels least sustainable? _________________________________________________ One change we can try: _________________________________________________ Do not record the night minute by minute unless a clinician requests it. Excessive tracking can make tired parents more anxious.
Common Sleep Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Comparing the baby with another family’s child
Expecting a newborn to follow an adult schedule
Treating every waking as a behavioural failure
Keeping a newborn awake to force longer nighttime sleep
Placing the baby on the stomach because sleep appears deeper
Continuing swaddling after rolling attempts begin
Allowing routine sleep in swings or car seats outside travel

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Falling asleep with the baby on a sofa or armchair
Following an app more closely than the baby’s cues
Assuming every sleep change is a regression
Removing night feeds without checking growth and medical readiness
Ignoring snoring, breathing pauses or difficulty waking
Expecting the mother to manage every waking alone
Purchasing products that promise guaranteed sleep

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Treating parental exhaustion as an unavoidable private problem

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

items: A programme guarantees that every baby will sleep through the night by a specific age.
A method tells you to ignore signs of illness, breathing difficulty or feeding need.
A product claims to prevent sudden infant death.
A sleep consultant advises a young baby without asking about age, growth, feeding or health.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: A caregiver routinely places the baby on the stomach or side.
An exhausted adult is repeatedly falling asleep with the baby on a sofa or chair.
Sedating medicines or herbal products are suggested solely to make the baby sleep.
A weighted sleep product is recommended.
You are told that responding to a young infant will spoil the child.
The parent’s mental or physical health is deteriorating while everyone focuses only on the baby’s schedule.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Your Family Sleep-Safety and Sustainability Checklist
Baby
☐ Baby is placed on the back for every sleep.
☐ Sleep surface is firm, flat and clear.
☐ Baby sleeps in the caregiver’s room on a separate surface during the early months.
☐ Night feeding follows medical and developmental needs.
☐ Swaddling will stop when rolling attempts begin.
☐ Sleep products have been reviewed for safety.
☐ Health or breathing concerns will be discussed promptly.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Parents
☐ Nighttime responsibilities have been discussed.
☐ A plan exists for dangerously tired moments.
☐ No adult will sleep with the baby on a sofa or armchair.
☐ At least one rest period is protected where possible.
☐ Driving and other high-risk tasks will be avoided after severe sleep loss.
☐ Persistent inability to sleep or cope will be discussed with a professional.
☐ The family understands that asking for nighttime help is a safety decision.
Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Healthy baby sleep is not measured only by uninterrupted hours. It is sleep that is safe, developmentally realistic and supported by a family plan that does not leave caregivers dangerously exhausted.
Try This Today
Do a five-minute nighttime safety review. Check:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: Is the sleep surface firm, flat and completely clear?
What will the feeding parent do if becoming drowsy?
Who can take over after the feed?
Which household task can be removed tomorrow so that one parent can rest?

blocks.text: You do not need to solve the baby’s sleep tonight. Begin by making tonight safer.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

items: World Health Organization: Sleep for Newborns and Children Under Five
World Health Organization: Guidelines on Physical Activity, Sedentary Behaviour and Sleep for Children Under Five
American Academy of Pediatrics: Safe Sleep
American Academy of Pediatrics: Updated Recommendations for Reducing Sleep-Related Infant Deaths
NHS: Helping Your Baby to Sleep
NHS: Sleep and Tiredness After Having a Baby

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep: What Babies Need and What Parents Can Expect

blocks.text: Sleep and safety recommendations should be checked again against current Indian paediatric and public-health guidance before publication.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond
pages.epigraph: “Sometimes you will solve the crying. Sometimes your loving presence will be the only answer available.”

blocks.text: She Had Tried Everything

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: The baby had been crying for nearly an hour. The mother had offered a feed. She had changed the nappy. She had burped the baby, walked around the room, sung softly and checked the baby’s clothing. Nothing seemed to work. Her partner began searching online. One page said the baby had gas. Another suggested reflux. A video recommended a herbal remedy. A relative advised leaving the baby alone so that crying would not become a habit. As the crying continued, the mother felt panic turning into frustration. Then her sister arrived.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: She checked that the baby appeared medically well, took the baby into her arms and told the mother:
“Go drink some water. I will hold her for ten minutes. You are not abandoning her. You are making sure you can stay gentle.”

The baby did not stop crying immediately. But the mother returned calmer. That mattered. Parents often believe that successful soothing means making every cry stop quickly. It does not. A responsible response to crying may involve:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Remaining present when the cause is unclear
Asking another adult to take over
Placing the baby safely in the cot while the caregiver calms down

blocks.text: No parent can settle every baby every time. The goal is not instant silence. The goal is a safe baby and a caregiver who can continue responding without causing harm.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Crying Is Communication
A baby cannot say:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Crying brings the caregiver closer. It is designed to be difficult to ignore. That does not mean every cry has one clear translation. You may understand some cries quickly. Others will remain uncertain even after you have checked everything carefully.
Crying Commonly Increases Before It Improves

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Many babies begin crying more at around two weeks of age. Crying often becomes most intense during the following weeks and then gradually decreases by approximately three to four months. Late afternoon and evening can be especially difficult. This pattern does not occur identically in every baby, but it helps parents understand that a temporary period of increased crying can be developmental rather than evidence that they have created a bad habit. Normal does not mean easy. A predictable developmental pattern can still leave a family exhausted.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Begin with a Calm Safety Check
When a baby cries, begin by observing before rushing through ten solutions. Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Is the baby alert and responsive?
Is there a fever or unusual temperature?
Has the baby been feeding normally?
Does the baby appear seriously unwell?

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: If there is a danger sign, seek medical care. If the baby appears medically stable, move through ordinary needs.
A Practical Crying Checklist
Hunger
Look for:

items: Feeding cues occurring earlier than expected

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Young babies do not always follow the clock. A baby may need to feed again sooner than an adult expected. However, repeated crying despite very frequent feeds deserves a feeding assessment rather than the automatic conclusion that the baby is simply hungry.
Nappy and skin discomfort
Check for:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Hair or thread wrapped around a toe, finger or genital area
A fastener pressing into the body

blocks.text: A hair wrapped tightly around a small body part can reduce circulation and requires prompt removal and medical help if it cannot be removed completely.
Temperature

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Feel the baby’s chest or the back of the neck. Check whether:

items: The baby has come indoors wearing outdoor layers

blocks.text: A young infant who feels unusually hot or cold and appears unwell needs prompt medical assessment.
Tiredness
A tired baby may:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Reduce stimulation and use a calm, repetitive approach.
Overstimulation
A baby may become overwhelmed by:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Repeated passing from one person to another

blocks.text: Move to a quieter space. Dim the lights. Use one soothing method for several minutes rather than changing techniques every few seconds.
Need for closeness

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Babies may cry because they need touch, warmth and reassurance. Holding a young baby does not create manipulation. The baby does not yet have the cognitive ability to design a strategy for controlling the family. Responding builds familiarity and safety.
Feeding discomfort
Observe whether crying occurs:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Persistent distress around feeding deserves professional assessment.
Illness or pain
Consider illness when crying:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Sounds weak, high-pitched or markedly different
Continues when the baby is not otherwise fussy
Is associated with swelling, injury or a concerning rash

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Do not diagnose every prolonged cry as colic. Colic is considered only after the baby is otherwise well and more serious causes have been excluded.
Try One Soothing Method at a Time

When adults become anxious, they may change the baby’s position, sound, room and activity every few seconds. This can add stimulation. Choose one safe method and give the baby time to respond. Possible approaches include:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Skin-to-skin contact with an awake, alert adult

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Taking a walk in a pram
Offering a warm bath when appropriate
Placing a calm hand on the baby’s chest while the baby lies safely on the back
Asking another familiar caregiver to try

blocks.text: Not every method works for every baby. The same method may work one day and fail the next.
Use Repetition, Not Intensity
Babies often respond better to predictable rhythm than to increasingly forceful soothing. Keep movement gentle. Do not:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Swing the baby by the arms
Toss the baby into the air
Use loud sound close to the ears
Place the baby on a vibrating device not intended for unsupervised use
Drive while dangerously exhausted simply because the car sometimes settles the baby

blocks.text: More intense soothing is not necessarily more effective.
Check the Baby’s Position

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: A baby may become more distressed when:

items: The head and neck are unsupported
The chin is pressed tightly toward the chest
Pressure is placed on a painful area
The baby is held too tightly
The baby is passed rapidly between caregivers

blocks.text: Support the head and neck. Keep the face visible. Ensure the airway is clear.
Sucking Can Be Soothing

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Sucking is both a feeding behaviour and a calming behaviour. A baby may suck:

items: On a pacifier when the family chooses to use one
On the baby’s own hand as development allows

blocks.text: When using a pacifier:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Do not dip it in honey, sugar or other substances.
Do not tie it around the baby’s neck.
Do not attach unsafe cords or heavy objects.
Do not force the baby to use it.
Discuss timing with a feeding professional when breastfeeding is still being established.

blocks.text: A pacifier should not be used to delay a needed feed.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Swaddling and Soothing
Some young babies settle when safely swaddled. If swaddling is used:

items: Place the baby on the back.
Keep the face and head uncovered.
Ensure the material cannot become loose.
Stop when the baby begins trying to roll.
Never place a swaddled baby on the side or stomach.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Swaddling is a technique, not a requirement. If the baby dislikes it, choose another approach.
White Noise and Repetitive Sound
Soft, steady background noise may help some babies settle. Use it cautiously:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Keep the device away from the baby’s head and sleep space.
Do not allow cords within reach.
Turn the sound down or off once no longer needed.
Confirm that the device does not overheat.

blocks.text: A sound machine is not a substitute for responding to hunger, pain or illness.
Movement and Carriers
Gentle movement can be calming. When using a sling or carrier:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Follow the manufacturer’s age and weight guidance.
Keep the baby close and supported.
Ensure the nose and mouth remain clear.
Keep the chin away from the chest.
Do not feed the baby while the baby is enclosed in a sling.
Avoid cooking, hot drinks and dangerous activities.
Remove the baby if the caregiver becomes sleepy.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: A carrier should support safe positioning—not simply keep the baby attached to an adult.
What Is Colic?

Colic is a term used for repeated, prolonged crying in an otherwise healthy and thriving young baby when no clear medical cause has been identified. A traditional description is crying for more than three hours a day, on more than three days a week, for several weeks, but clinicians do not rely on the clock alone. A baby with colic generally:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Has normal periods between crying episodes
Becomes especially unsettled in the late afternoon or evening
May become red in the face
Is difficult to soothe during episodes
Improves with age, often by three to four months

blocks.text: Colic should be considered only after feeding, growth and health have been reviewed.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Colic Is Not a Parenting Failure
Colic is not caused by:

items: A mother holding the baby too much
Failure to establish a strict schedule

blocks.text: Parental stress may make the experience harder, but it does not mean the parent created the crying.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Do Not Assume Every Cry Is Gas

Crying babies often swallow air. They may then burp or pass gas. This can make gas appear to be the cause even when it is partly the result of crying. Burping may help some babies after feeds, but repeatedly treating every cry as trapped wind can delay assessment of:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Use “gas” as one possibility, not a complete diagnosis.
Reflux and Crying

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Many babies bring up small amounts of milk. Ordinary spit-up does not automatically explain prolonged crying. Reflux disease is more likely to need medical assessment when symptoms include:
Do not:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Place the baby on the stomach to sleep
Start acid-suppressing medicine without proper assessment

blocks.text: Safe sleep remains important for babies who spit up.
Food Allergy and Intolerance
Some babies have a genuine food allergy or another feeding-related condition. Possible signs may include combinations of:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Blood or significant mucus in stool

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: These symptoms require professional assessment. Do not remove several foods from a breastfeeding mother’s diet based only on fussiness. Unnecessary restriction can create nutritional stress without helping the baby. Do not switch formulas repeatedly without guidance. A proper trial should have a reason, a plan and a review date.
Products Marketed for Colic
Families may be offered:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Evidence and safety vary. Before using anything, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Is it approved for this age?
Could it interact with medicine or feeding?
Does the baby first need medical assessment?
How will we know whether it helped?

blocks.text: “Natural” does not mean risk-free.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Keep a Short Crying Record When Useful
A brief record may help a clinician identify patterns. Record:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Feeding before or during the episode
Whether the baby was normal between episodes
Who was caring for the baby

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Do not record every second. The aim is to identify patterns, not to turn the family into a monitoring centre.
The Caregiver’s Nervous System Matters
A baby’s cry creates a strong physical reaction. A caregiver may experience:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Having a frightening thought does not automatically mean the parent will act on it. But it is a signal to step away safely and obtain support. The most important moment may be recognising:
“I am no longer calm enough to hold the baby safely.”
A Safe Pause
If the baby appears medically stable but the crying is overwhelming you:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Place the baby on the back in the safe cot or crib.
Make sure the sleep space is clear.
Leave the room for a short, defined period.
Seek medical support if the crying remains concerning.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: A baby may cry during the pause. A few minutes of crying in a safe cot is safer than remaining in the arms of a caregiver who fears losing control.
Never Shake a Baby

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Never shake, throw, hit or handle a baby violently. A baby’s head is large relative to the body, the neck is weak and the brain is vulnerable. Violent shaking can cause bleeding, brain injury, permanent disability or death. Abusive head trauma often occurs when a caregiver becomes overwhelmed by crying. It is preventable when families understand the danger and prepare a safe response in advance. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
Every caregiver should know:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Crying can continue despite good care.
Walking away briefly is allowed when the baby is safe.
Shaking is never safe—not even briefly.
Rough play can also injure a young baby.
Anyone who fears losing control must hand the baby to a safe adult or place the baby in the cot.

blocks.text: Do not leave your baby with a person who:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: If a Baby May Have Been Shaken or Injured
Seek emergency medical care immediately. Do not wait for visible bruising. Possible signs of abusive head trauma may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Tell medical professionals honestly what may have happened. Accurate information helps them provide the right care.
Create a Crying-Support Plan Before You Need It

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Write down:
People who can take over
People available by phone
Medical contacts

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: My signs that I need a break
My safe-pause plan

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: “I will place the baby safely in ____________________, step away for ______ minutes and contact ____________________.”
Every regular caregiver should know the plan.
Share the Hardest Time of Day
If the baby commonly cries during the evening, plan support for that period. Possible adjustments include:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Asking another adult to arrive before the crying usually begins
Allowing the mother to rest beforehand
Completing older children’s essential tasks earlier
Avoiding important family discussions during the most exhausted hour

blocks.text: Do not wait for the crisis to begin before organising help.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Crying Can Affect the Parent-Child Relationship
Persistent crying may make a parent feel:

items: Afraid of being alone with the baby

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: These feelings deserve support. A parent can love a baby and still find the crying extremely difficult. Speak with a healthcare or mental-health professional if crying is affecting:

items: The ability to care for the baby
Thoughts about oneself or the baby

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Seek Urgent Medical Care
Seek urgent assessment if the crying occurs with:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Blue, grey, pale or blotchy colour
Fever or an unusually low temperature in a young infant
A rash that does not fade with pressure

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: A weak, high-pitched or very unusual cry
A hair tightly wrapped around a toe, finger or genital area
A sudden change from the baby’s normal behaviour
Continuous inconsolable crying that concerns the caregiver

blocks.text: Contact emergency services when the baby appears life-threateningly unwell. Trusting your concern does not mean diagnosing the cause. It means obtaining appropriate assessment.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Common Mistakes

items: Feeding repeatedly without assessing whether feeding is effective
Changing soothing methods every few seconds
Diagnosing colic before the baby has been examined
Giving herbal or traditional remedies without checking safety
Restricting the mother’s diet without clinical reason

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Raising the cot mattress for reflux
Placing the baby on the stomach to stop crying
Holding the baby while the caregiver is becoming dangerously angry
Believing that taking a safe break is abandonment
Leaving an overwhelmed person alone with the baby
Telling parents to enjoy every moment
Treating a baby’s crying as deliberate manipulation
Dismissing a mother’s concern because the baby looks well in a short video

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Forgetting that parental exhaustion also needs treatment and support

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

items: A remedy is recommended for every crying baby.
Someone promises to cure colic immediately.
An adviser recommends forceful manipulation of a young baby.
A product contains ingredients that are not clearly disclosed.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: Someone tells you that shaking is harmless when done gently.
A caregiver becomes angry when the baby does not stop crying.
You are advised to ignore a weak, unusual or continuous cry.
A baby with poor feeding or weight concerns is labelled “colicky” without assessment.
A parent says they are afraid they may hurt the baby.
Family members respond with blame instead of arranging relief.

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: A statement such as “I am scared I may lose control” should be taken seriously and compassionately. Place the baby safely, remove the overwhelmed caregiver from the immediate situation and arrange support.
Your Crying Review

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Baby’s age: ______________________________________ Crying usually begins: ______________________________ Approximate duration: ______________________________ Baby is normal between episodes: Yes / No / Unsure Feeding is going well: Yes / No / Unsure Wet nappies are appropriate: Yes / No / Unsure Weight is being monitored: Yes / No Vomiting or stool concern: Yes / No Fever or temperature concern: Yes / No Cry sounds different: Yes / No / Unsure What helps most often: _________________________________________________

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: Remember This
You are not failing when a well-cared-for baby continues to cry. Check for illness and ordinary needs, offer calm comfort, and step away safely before frustration becomes dangerous.
Try This Today
Teach every regular caregiver this sentence:

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

blocks.text: “If the crying becomes too much, place the baby safely on the back in the clear cot, step away briefly and call for help. Never shake the baby.”
Then write the names of two people who can be contacted during a difficult crying period. Preparation protects both the baby and the person providing care.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

pages.chapterTitle: Why Babies Cry and How to Respond

items: American Academy of Pediatrics: Responding to Your Baby’s Cries
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention: Abusive Head Trauma
World Health Organization: Essential Newborn Care—Assessment and Continuing Care

blocks.text: Medical warning signs and emergency pathways should be verified against current Indian paediatric and public-health guidance before publication.

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety
pages.epigraph: “A secure relationship is not built in one perfect moment. It is built through thousands of ordinary moments of noticing, responding and reconnecting.”

blocks.text: She Loved Her Baby—but Did Not Feel What She Expected

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: During pregnancy, a mother imagined the first moment she would hold her baby. She expected overwhelming love. Instead, after a long and frightening birth, she felt relief that the baby was safe—and then exhaustion. People around her cried with happiness. She smiled for photographs. Inside, she felt strangely distant. During the following days, she cared for the baby carefully. She fed, changed and held him, but kept waiting for the powerful feeling everyone had promised. When she finally told her doctor, she expected judgement.
The doctor said:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: “Bonding is not a deadline. Let us support your recovery while you and your baby get to know one another.”

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: The mother’s relationship with her baby did grow. Not through one dramatic moment. It grew during quiet morning feeds, through the weight of his sleeping body against her chest and through the first time he turned toward her voice. Some parents feel an immediate connection. For others, love arrives gradually. Both experiences can belong to caring parents. What matters is not performing the expected emotion. What matters is creating repeated experiences of safety, care and connection—and asking for help when emotional distance, anxiety or depression makes that difficult.

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Bonding and Attachment Are Related but Different
The words are often used as though they mean the same thing.
Bonding
Bonding commonly describes the caregiver’s growing emotional connection with the baby. It may begin:

items: During the following weeks or months
After recovery from illness, separation or birth trauma

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Attachment
Attachment describes the child’s developing relationship with caregivers who provide protection, comfort and a secure base. The baby gradually learns:

items: Who comes when I need help?
Whose voice and touch are familiar?
Can I explore and return for comfort?
What happens when I become distressed?

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Attachment is not created by one birth experience, one missed feed, one childcare arrangement or one imperfect day. It develops through patterns over time.
What Is Responsive Caregiving?

The World Health Organization defines responsive caregiving as the caregiver’s ability to notice, understand and respond to a child’s signals in a timely and appropriate way. It supports safety, feeding, health, early learning, trust and social relationships. WHO Nurturing Care Practice Guide Responsive caregiving involves three steps:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Consider what the child may need.
Respond in a safe and appropriate way.

blocks.text: For example:

items: The baby turns away during play.
The caregiver pauses and reduces stimulation.

blocks.text: Or:

items: The baby roots and brings hands to the mouth.

blocks.text: Or:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: The baby cries when an unfamiliar person approaches.
The caregiver holds the baby and allows time to observe.
The caregiver does not force immediate contact.

blocks.text: Responsive does not mean instantly granting every future request. It means adjusting expectations to the child’s developmental ability and responding to the need beneath the signal.
You Will Not Interpret Every Cue Correctly

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: No caregiver correctly understands every cry, expression or movement. You may think the baby is hungry when the baby is tired. You may begin play when the baby needs quiet. You may try rocking when the baby wants to be put down. A secure relationship does not require mind reading. A healthy pattern looks more like this:

items: Over time, you learn the baby’s patterns.

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Misunderstanding is part of learning. The important skill is remaining curious enough to try again.
Responsiveness Is Not Perfection
A parent may imagine that secure attachment requires:

items: Never allowing the baby to cry

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: These expectations are impossible. Real caregiving includes:
Emotional safety grows when the overall relationship is sufficiently predictable, caring and responsive. One difficult evening does not define the bond.
Repair Is Part of the Relationship

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: There will be moments when you speak too sharply, become distracted or respond later than you wanted. Repair may look like:

items: Picking the baby up when calm enough
Saying, even to a young baby, “That was a difficult moment. I am here now.”
Changing a pattern that repeatedly leads to distress

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: The baby does not understand every word. The baby experiences the change in your tone, touch, face and availability. Repair teaches that relationships can remain safe even after strain.
Everyday Care Builds Connection
Bonding does not require expensive classes or elaborate activities. It grows during:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Resting together while the adult remains awake

blocks.text: During a nappy change, you might say:
“I am lifting your legs now. Here is the clean nappy.”
During feeding:
“You were hungry. I see you.”
During dressing:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: “This sleeve feels tricky. We are almost done.”
The words do not need to be clever. Your voice, rhythm and responsiveness matter more than the script.
Follow the Baby’s Lead
A baby may invite interaction by:
The baby may need a pause by:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: When the baby looks away, you do not always need to bring the face back toward you. Pause. Wait. See whether the baby returns. This back-and-forth rhythm helps the child learn that communication has an effect.
Serve and Return

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: You may hear early interaction described as “serve and return.” The child offers a signal—through a look, sound, gesture or movement. The caregiver responds. For example:
This simple exchange supports communication, attention and social learning. It does not require educational toys. It requires noticing.

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Eye Contact Should Be Invited, Not Forced
Babies often enjoy looking at familiar faces from close range. But they may look away when:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Do not force the baby’s face toward you. Offer your face, voice and expression. Allow the baby to engage and pause. Persistent concerns about eye contact, responsiveness, hearing or social communication can be discussed during developmental follow-up without assuming a diagnosis from one behaviour.
Touch and Physical Closeness
Safe, affectionate touch can support connection. This may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Resting a calm hand on the baby
Responding when the baby reaches toward you

blocks.text: Observe the baby. Some babies enjoy long periods of close contact. Others become warm, restless or overstimulated sooner. Touch should be gentle and responsive—not forced.
Skin-to-Skin Contact

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Skin-to-skin contact can support warmth, feeding and bonding when the adult is awake and alert. Keep the baby:

items: With the nose and mouth clear
With the neck in a neutral position

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Move the baby to a safe sleep surface before the adult sleeps. Skin-to-skin is valuable, but it is not the only path to connection. Parents separated from a baby by medical care, adoption, surrogacy or other circumstances can build deep relationships through later responsive care.
Bonding After Separation
Some families experience early separation because:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: The mother requires surgery or intensive care
Adoption or surrogacy arrangements require transition
The mother or baby has an infection
The family faces a medical emergency
Work or caregiving responsibilities create unavoidable separation

blocks.text: Separation can be painful. It does not prevent attachment. Depending on the situation, connection may be supported through:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Speaking or singing to the baby
Providing expressed milk when possible and desired
