Using a familiar cloth or scent only when approved by the clinical team and never in an unsafe sleep space
Asking staff how to become involved

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Parents may need emotional support to process fear, grief or helplessness associated with separation.
Fathers and Partners Are Not Assistants
A baby can build close, secure relationships with more than one caregiver. Fathers and partners can:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Bottle-feed when part of the feeding plan

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: The relationship grows through participation. If one parent always directs and corrects, the other may remain uncertain. Safety practices should be consistent, but ordinary caregiving styles can differ. One parent may sing. Another may walk. One may be playful. Another may be calming. The baby can feel secure with both.
Grandparents and Other Caregivers
Regular, loving caregivers can become important attachment figures. This may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: A strong relationship with another safe caregiver does not reduce the parent’s importance. It expands the child’s circle of security. However, every caregiver should follow essential family safety practices. They should know:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Which practices the parents do not permit
How to respect the child’s signals

blocks.text: The Baby May Prefer One Caregiver at Times
A baby may settle more easily with one person during a particular period. This may reflect:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Preference does not mean permanent rejection of another caregiver. Build familiarity during calm periods rather than only when the baby is exhausted and distressed. The less-preferred caregiver might:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Offer comfort while the primary caregiver remains nearby initially

blocks.text: Relationships grow through repetition.
Attachment and Childcare
Parents sometimes fear that using childcare will damage attachment. The more useful questions are:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Does the caregiver notice the child’s signals?
Are distress and feeding handled appropriately?
Is communication with the family respectful?
Does the child have opportunities to form a stable relationship?
How does the child behave before, during and after care?

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: A child can remain strongly attached to parents while developing a meaningful relationship with a childcare provider. Consistency and quality matter more than guilt.
Separation and Reunion
Some babies protest when a parent leaves. This often becomes more noticeable as the baby develops a stronger awareness that people continue to exist when out of sight. Support separation by:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Beginning with short periods when possible
Keeping the goodbye calm and clear
Returning when promised as far as practical
Allowing the child time to reconnect
Recognising that crying at goodbye does not automatically mean the arrangement is harmful

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: A simple goodbye might be:
“Mama is going now. You are safe with Nani. I will come back after your nap.”
The baby may not understand every word, but tone and repetition create familiarity.
Do Not Test Attachment
Parents may become anxious and begin testing the relationship. For example:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Repeatedly leaving to see whether the baby cries
Comparing how the baby responds to different adults
Trying to make the baby jealous
Asking older children to choose a favourite parent
Interpreting every independent moment as rejection
Interpreting every clingy moment as damage

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Attachment is not measured through family experiments. If development or connection concerns you, discuss them with a qualified professional who can observe the child and relationship in context.
Responding Does Not Spoil a Young Baby

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: A young baby does not cry to manipulate adults. Responding to hunger, distress and need for closeness helps the baby experience caregivers as dependable. This does not require preventing every cry. Sometimes you are already holding the baby and the baby continues crying. Sometimes you need a moment to wash your hands, make feeding safe or attend to another child. Responsive care is a pattern. It is not a promise that the baby will never wait.
Attachment Is Not the Same as Constant Entertainment
Babies need interaction. They also need:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Independent movement in a safe space

blocks.text: You do not have to perform for the baby all day. Place the baby safely on a mat during an alert period. Sit nearby. Observe. Respond when the baby invites interaction. Connection includes comfortable quiet.
Temperament Shapes the Relationship

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Babies differ from the beginning. One baby may:
Another may:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Temperament is not character failure. A highly reactive baby is not badly behaved. An easy-to-settle baby is not better parented. Responsive caregiving asks:
“What helps this particular child feel safe enough to engage?”
When the Parent’s History Affects Bonding
Caring for a baby can bring back memories of:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: A difficult relationship with one’s own parents

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: A baby’s crying, dependence or physical closeness may trigger feelings the parent did not expect. This does not make the parent dangerous or unloving. It does mean support may be valuable. A trauma-informed therapist can help the parent understand the response without shame and build safer patterns.
When the Birth Was Traumatic
A parent may struggle to look at or hold the baby because the baby’s arrival is connected with a frightening birth experience. Possible responses include:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Feeling detached from the body or baby

blocks.text: Do not tell the parent:
“The baby is healthy, so forget what happened.”
A healthy baby does not erase trauma. The parent may need:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Treatment for anxiety, depression or post-traumatic symptoms

blocks.text: Bonding can grow while the parent receives treatment.
When Bonding Feels Difficult
A parent may find connection difficult because of:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety
Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Support should address the actual barrier. Telling the parent to “enjoy the baby” does not treat depression, pain or trauma.
Signs a Parent May Need Support
Speak with a healthcare or mental-health professional if the parent:

items: Feels persistently detached from the baby
Avoids caring for or looking at the baby
Experiences constant fear about the baby
Cannot sleep even when the baby is safely cared for

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Feels intense anger toward the baby
Feels that the baby would be better without them
Is no longer able to perform basic care
Is concerned that the bond is not developing

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Intrusive thoughts can occur in postpartum anxiety and other conditions. They deserve confidential, professional assessment. Having an unwanted thought is not the same as wanting to act, but safety must be evaluated properly.
RED FLAG: Seek Urgent Help
Obtain emergency help if a parent:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Has thoughts or plans to harm themselves or the baby
Believes the baby is possessed, evil or sending special messages
Hears voices or sees things others do not
Has not slept and is becoming increasingly disorganised
Is behaving in a dangerously impulsive way
Has threatened or harmed the baby

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Do not leave the parent alone with the baby while urgent help is being arranged. Postpartum psychosis and other severe mental-health crises are medical emergencies.
Caring for the Caregiver Supports the Child

The emotional wellbeing of caregivers affects their ability to provide nurturing care. UNICEF and WHO therefore include caregiver wellbeing and social support as part of early-childhood programmes—not as optional luxuries. UNICEF Caring for the Caregiver Support may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety
Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: A mother should not have to become completely depleted to prove attachment.
Bonding Is Not Only the Mother’s Job
Families sometimes speak as though the mother alone determines the child’s emotional future. This creates unfair pressure and ignores the family system. A nurturing environment is supported by:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Healthcare professionals who support mental health

blocks.text: A mother’s responsiveness matters. So does whether anyone is caring for her.
Family Conflict and Emotional Safety

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Babies may not understand the words adults use, but they respond to:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Ordinary disagreements occur in families. The concern is repeated intimidation, aggression, coercion or violence. A parent experiencing abuse may need confidential safety planning. Do not encourage a person to confront a violent partner without appropriate support. Contact qualified local domestic-violence or emergency services when safety is at risk. Protecting the caregiver is part of protecting the baby.
A Daily Connection Practice
Choose one ordinary routine. For example, the first morning nappy change. During that routine:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Put the phone aside when possible.
Notice when the baby needs a break.
End with the same gentle phrase.

blocks.text: The practice may last only two minutes. Attachment is not created by length alone. It is created through repeated availability.
A Relationship Observation Page

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: When my baby wants interaction, I notice:
When my baby needs a pause, I notice:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: My baby is often soothed by:
Our easiest time together is:
Our most difficult time is:
Another caregiver my baby trusts:

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Support I need to feel more present:
_________________________________________________ This page is not a test of attachment. It is a way of becoming curious about the relationship.
Common Mistakes

items: Treating delayed bonding as proof of failure
Believing that one difficult day damages attachment

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Continuing stimulation when the baby looks away
Correcting another safe caregiver’s every small difference
Treating fathers or partners as assistants
Believing childcare automatically weakens the parent-child bond
Testing whether the baby prefers one parent

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Assuming responsive care means constant entertainment
Ignoring maternal depression, anxiety or trauma
Telling a distressed parent to be grateful
Focusing on the baby while leaving the caregiver unsupported
Using attachment language to frighten or blame mothers

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: Someone claims that one missed moment permanently damaged the baby.
A programme promises to create a perfectly attached child.
A parent is told never to leave the baby with another safe caregiver.
Attachment language is used to shame a mother for working.

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

items: A caregiver repeatedly ignores the baby’s hunger, pain or distress.
A parent appears frightened of being alone with the baby.
A caregiver handles the baby roughly or responds with threats.
A parent’s severe anxiety, depression or trauma is being dismissed.
Family conflict makes the baby or caregiver unsafe.
A professional makes a diagnosis based on one brief behaviour without assessing the child and family more fully.

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Your Family Connection Checklist
Baby
☐ We observe the baby’s signals rather than relying only on schedules.
☐ We respond to hunger, pain and distress.
☐ We allow pauses during interaction.
☐ We provide safe touch and closeness.
☐ We make room for quiet observation.
☐ We understand that temperament affects behaviour.
☐ We will discuss developmental concerns without self-diagnosing.
Caregivers

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: ☐ More than one safe adult is learning the baby’s care.
☐ Essential safety practices are consistent.
☐ Caregivers may develop their own gentle styles.
☐ The mother has practical and emotional support.
☐ We repair after difficult moments.
☐ We know where to seek mental-health support.
☐ We will act urgently if a caregiver or baby becomes unsafe.
Remember This

Secure attachment does not require a perfect mother. It grows when a child repeatedly experiences care, protection, comfort and reconnection—and when caregivers receive enough support to keep providing them.

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Try This Today
Choose one routine—feeding, changing, bathing or settling. For two minutes:

items: Watch your baby’s face and body.
Respond to one sound or movement.
Pause when your baby looks away.
Notice what helps your baby return.

blocks.text: You are not performing an exercise for your baby. You are practising the heart of responsive caregiving:
“I notice you. I am learning you. I am here.”

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Authoritative References for This Chapter

items: World Health Organization: Nurturing Care Practice Guide
World Health Organization: Nurturing Care for Early Childhood Development
World Health Organization: Parenting for Lifelong Health—Infants
NHS: Building a Close Relationship with Your Baby
American Academy of Pediatrics: Ways to Bond with Your Baby

pages.chapterTitle: Bonding, Attachment and Emotional Safety

blocks.text: Mental-health resources, safeguarding procedures and emergency contacts should be localised and verified for the intended Indian edition before publication.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother
pages.epigraph: “The mother is not the background in her baby’s story. Her health is part of the baby’s care.”

blocks.text: Everyone Asked About the Baby

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: After the birth, visitors asked the same questions. Was the baby feeding? Was the baby sleeping? Had the baby gained weight? Did the baby cry often? Very few people asked the mother how much pain she was in, whether she had eaten, whether she could sleep when someone else held the baby, or whether she felt frightened by her own thoughts. She answered every question about the baby carefully. When asked about herself, she said:
“I’m fine. Just tired.”

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: But she was not only tired. She was physically uncomfortable, emotionally overwhelmed and afraid that admitting difficulty would make people question her ability to care for her child. At a postnatal appointment, a nurse spoke to her alone. The nurse asked about bleeding, pain, sleep, mood, appetite, support and safety at home. For the first time since the birth, the mother understood something important: Postnatal care was not an optional kindness. It was healthcare. A baby needs feeding, safety, warmth and responsive care.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Providing those things becomes harder when the mother’s pain, illness, depression or exhaustion is ignored. Supporting the mother is not taking attention away from the baby. It is strengthening the baby’s care.
Recovery Is Not the Same for Every Mother
Postnatal recovery is influenced by:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: The length and type of labour
The amount of practical support available

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: One mother may move comfortably within days. Another may need significantly more time and medical care. Neither recovery should be used as the standard for everyone else.
The First Six Weeks Matter

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: The World Health Organization identifies the first six weeks after birth as a critical period for the health and wellbeing of both mother and newborn. Recommended postnatal care includes attention to physical recovery, feeding, mental health, social support and danger signs—not only one final appointment after everything has already become difficult. World Health Organization Before leaving the birth facility, confirm:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: When the mother should be reviewed
How wounds or stitches should be managed
Whether blood pressure or blood tests require follow-up
Whom to contact outside normal hours
How mental-health concerns will be assessed
What contraception and birth-spacing information will be offered

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Do not skip the mother’s appointment because the baby appears well.
Physical Recovery: What to Monitor
After birth, the mother may experience:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother
Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Some discomfort can be expected. Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be assessed. The mother should not have to decide alone whether a symptom is “normal enough.”
Postnatal Bleeding
Bleeding and discharge after birth are often called lochia. The amount and colour usually change over time. Follow the instructions provided by the maternity team and seek urgent help for:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Large clots, particularly with increased bleeding
A sudden return to heavy bleeding after it had reduced

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Do not remain alone while feeling faint or severely unwell. Postpartum haemorrhage can occur after the mother has left the birth facility.
Caesarean and Perineal Wound Care
Follow the maternity team’s instructions for cleaning, drying, pain relief and physical activity. Contact a healthcare professional if there is:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: A general feeling of becoming unwell

blocks.text: Pain that prevents walking, feeding, sleeping or caring for basic needs deserves review. The goal is not to tolerate the greatest amount of pain. The goal is safe recovery.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Blood Pressure Problems Can Continue After Birth
High blood pressure and pregnancy-related hypertensive conditions do not always end when the baby is born. Seek urgent medical help for symptoms such as:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Severe pain in the upper abdomen
Sudden swelling of the face or hands
A strong feeling that something is seriously wrong

blocks.text: Follow any blood-pressure monitoring plan exactly. Do not stop prescribed medicine because the pregnancy has ended unless the clinician advises it.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Blood Clots and Breathing Symptoms
Pregnancy and the postnatal period can increase the risk of blood clots. Seek urgent help for:
Do not massage a painful, swollen leg while waiting for assessment.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Urination and Bowel Care
After birth, some mothers experience:
Discuss symptoms early. Seek medical help for:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Pelvic-floor concerns are common and treatable. They should not be dismissed as the price of motherhood.
Pelvic-Floor Recovery

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: The pelvic floor supports the bladder, bowel and uterus. Pregnancy and birth can affect:
A pelvic-health physiotherapist or appropriately trained clinician may help with:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Do not begin an intense exercise programme solely because social media suggests that the body should “bounce back.” Recovery is rehabilitation—not a race.
Nutrition, Fluids and Medicines

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: The mother needs regular access to food and water. This sounds obvious, but many mothers spend hours feeding the baby while no one prepares food for them. Practical support includes:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: A breastfeeding mother does not need a perfect diet to provide valuable milk. She does need adequate nourishment for her own health and recovery. Avoid severe dietary restrictions unless there is a clear clinical reason.
Medicines
Before starting or stopping a medicine, discuss:

items: Risks of leaving the mother untreated

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Many medicines are compatible with breastfeeding. Some require adjustment or monitoring. Do not assume that all medicines are dangerous or that all are harmless.
Sleep Is a Health Need
“Sleep when the baby sleeps” sounds simple. It may be impossible when:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: The baby sleeps only while held
The mother has returned to work

blocks.text: A better question is:
“What must change so the mother has a genuine opportunity to rest?”
Possible changes include:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Another adult handling nappies and settling
Visitors bringing food instead of expecting hosting
Sharing bottle feeds when part of the feeding plan
A family member holding the baby while the mother naps
Seeking help for pain or anxiety that prevents sleep
Avoiding driving after severe sleep deprivation

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Sleep deprivation is not a badge of good parenting. It affects safety and health.
Rest Is More Than Sleep
When sleep is fragmented, rest may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Receiving a massage only when medically appropriate and desired
Allowing the home to remain imperfect

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Rest will not replace necessary sleep indefinitely. But it can reduce strain while the family reorganises responsibilities.
Protect the Mother from Becoming the Household Manager

Sometimes relatives offer help but wait for the mother to direct every task. She must notice that groceries are low, assign someone to shop, explain what to buy and check whether it was done. That is not complete relief. A practical support plan gives other adults ownership. For example:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother
Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: The mother can still make important decisions. She should not have to manage every process.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Visitors Should Support Recovery
A helpful visitor may:

items: Hold the baby while the mother showers

blocks.text: An unhelpful visit may require the mother to:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Accept unwanted photographs or social-media posts

blocks.text: A simple family rule may be:
“Visits are short and arranged in advance. Please do not visit when unwell. The mother may leave to feed or rest at any time.”
The Couple Relationship Changes Too
The arrival of a baby can affect:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Do not attempt every difficult conversation at two in the morning. Create a short weekly check-in. Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Where is one person carrying too much?
What practical task can move to someone else?
What does each person need in the next seven days?
Is either parent worried about the other’s wellbeing?

blocks.text: The purpose is not to decide who is more tired. Both may be struggling differently.
Intimacy and Sexual Recovery

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: There is no universal date by which a mother must feel ready for sexual activity. Readiness may be affected by:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Consent is required within marriage and every other relationship. Pressure, guilt or forced sexual activity is not an acceptable part of postnatal recovery. Discuss pain, contraception, birth spacing and sexual health during postnatal care. Persistent pain deserves assessment.
Baby Blues, Depression and Anxiety Are Not the Same
Baby blues
During the first days after birth, hormonal shifts, exhaustion and adjustment may cause:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: These feelings often peak during the first week and improve within approximately two weeks. Support, rest and observation are important.
Postnatal depression
Postnatal depression is more persistent and can affect daily functioning. Possible symptoms include:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Difficulty sleeping even when there is an opportunity

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: It is common, treatable and not caused by lack of love.
Postnatal anxiety
Anxiety may involve:

items: Constant fear that something will happen to the baby
Difficulty allowing another safe person to hold the baby

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Some worry is expected. Anxiety deserves assessment when it is persistent, intense or interfering with care and daily life.
Intrusive Thoughts

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: A parent may experience an unwanted, frightening thought or mental image. The thought may feel completely opposite to what the parent wants. Because of shame, parents may hide these experiences. Intrusive thoughts can occur with anxiety, depression, obsessive-compulsive symptoms, trauma and other conditions. They require sensitive assessment. An unwanted thought is not automatically the same as an intention to act. However, a qualified professional should assess:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Whether it is wanted or unwanted
Whether the parent fears acting on it
Whether there is a plan or intent
Whether the parent is avoiding care
Whether the baby and parent are safe

blocks.text: Parents should not be punished for asking for mental-health help. They should be supported and assessed properly.
Postpartum Psychosis Is an Emergency

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Postpartum psychosis is rare but serious. It often begins suddenly, commonly within the first two weeks after birth, though it can occur later. Symptoms may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Very little sleep without feeling tired
Beliefs about the baby that are clearly disconnected from reality

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Postpartum psychosis requires urgent same-day emergency assessment. It can worsen quickly and place both mother and baby at risk. NHS Do not:

items: Leave the mother alone with the baby
Wait to see whether sleep solves it
Rely only on relatives to manage the crisis

blocks.text: Arrange emergency medical help.
Previous Mental-Health Conditions Need Continued Care

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Pregnancy and breastfeeding do not erase a history of:
A mother with a known condition should have a care plan that addresses:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Do not stop psychiatric medicine suddenly without speaking to the prescribing clinician. Untreated illness also carries risk.
Mental-Health Screening Is Good Postnatal Care

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Validated screening tools can help identify depression and anxiety. They do not replace a clinical conversation. A mother should be asked privately about:
A screening score is not a character judgement. It is a way to identify who may benefit from further assessment.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Treatment Can Include Different Forms of Support
Depending on the condition and severity, professional care may include:

items: Treatment of pain, thyroid disease, anaemia or other medical contributors

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Treatment should consider both maternal health and infant-feeding goals without assuming that the mother must sacrifice one to protect the other.
The Mother’s Identity Has Not Disappeared
Caring for a newborn can temporarily reduce life to feeding, changing and sleeping. The mother may miss:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Missing an earlier part of life does not mean regretting the baby. Identity can expand slowly. Begin with small opportunities:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: A conversation unrelated to the baby
Planning future learning or work without pressure to begin immediately

blocks.text: The mother does not have to choose between loving the baby and remembering herself.
Returning to Work or Remaining at Home

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Some mothers want to return to work quickly. Some want a longer pause. Some have no financial choice. Some feel conflicted. The emotional and practical preparation may involve:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: No single choice proves greater devotion. The family should focus on safety, sustainability and informed decision-making. Later books in the Mom’s Village series can explore career identity in greater depth. Here, the immediate goal is to ensure that the mother’s care responsibilities do not make her health invisible.
Domestic Abuse May Begin or Worsen Around Pregnancy
A mother may experience:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother
Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Abuse is not normal conflict. If a mother is being harmed or threatened, she may need confidential support and safety planning from qualified local services. Do not encourage a direct confrontation when it may increase danger. In immediate danger, contact emergency services. A healthcare appointment may offer a safe opportunity to ask for help privately.
The Postnatal Support Meeting
Once a week during the early months, ask:
Physical health

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Is urination or bowel function difficult?
Is there a severe headache, breathing problem or leg swelling?

blocks.text: Mental health

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: How has the mother’s mood been?
Can she sleep when given the chance?
Is she feeling hopeless or detached?

blocks.text: Practical support

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Is one adult carrying most responsibilities?
What support is needed this week?

blocks.text: This meeting should lead to action. Do not collect the mother’s concerns and then return every responsibility to her.
RED FLAG: Seek Urgent Physical Care
Seek immediate or urgent medical assessment for:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Very heavy or suddenly increasing bleeding
One-sided leg swelling, redness or pain
A wound that is opening or appears infected

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Severe breast redness or pain with fever
Any symptom the maternity team identified as an emergency

blocks.text: Do not delay care because the baby has no alternative caregiver. Call for help and take the baby with appropriate support when necessary.
RED FLAG: Seek Urgent Mental-Health Care
Obtain emergency help if the mother:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Has thoughts, intent or a plan to harm herself or the baby
Hears voices or sees things others do not
Has unusual beliefs disconnected from reality
Has gone without sleep and is becoming increasingly energised or disorganised
Cannot safely care for herself or the baby
Appears to be experiencing postpartum psychosis
Is in immediate danger from another person

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Keep the baby with a safe caregiver. Do not leave the mother alone.
Common Mistakes

items: Asking only about the baby during postnatal visits
Assuming severe pain is an unavoidable part of motherhood
Expecting the mother to host visitors

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Offering help but requiring her to manage it
Treating sleep deprivation as proof of dedication
Pressuring the mother to resume sex
Treating anxiety as ordinary concern when it prevents daily functioning

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Confusing postpartum psychosis with baby blues
Hiding mental-health symptoms because of stigma
Leaving all nighttime responsibility with the feeding parent
Assuming a supportive family prevents mental illness
Believing self-care means buying something rather than changing responsibilities

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: Someone tells the mother that every symptom is normal after birth.
A practitioner dismisses heavy bleeding or a severe headache without assessment.
A family member prevents the mother from seeking medical care.
A partner pressures the mother into sexual activity.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: A mother says the family would be better without her.
A severely distressed mother is told only to sleep more.
Hallucinations or delusions are described as spiritual insight without urgent medical assessment.
The mother is afraid to disclose how she feels.
Medicines are stopped because of breastfeeding without discussing alternatives.
The person offering help increases the mother’s work or removes her decision-making power.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: The Mother’s Postnatal Check-In
Physical recovery

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Pain level today: __________________________________ Bleeding concern: Yes / No / Unsure Wound or stitches concern: Yes / No / Unsure Headache or vision concern: Yes / No Breathing or chest concern: Yes / No Leg swelling or pain: Yes / No Urination or bowel concern: Yes / No Feeding-related breast concern: Yes / No Question for my clinician: _________________________________________________
Emotional wellbeing
During the past week, I have often felt:
☐ Calm enough to manage
☐ Tearful
☐ Anxious
☐ Hopeless
☐ Irritable
☐ Detached
☐ Unable to enjoy anything

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: ☐ Unable to sleep when I have the chance
☐ Frightened by my thoughts
☐ Unsafe
Support

My most reliable support person is: _________________________________________________ The task I most need someone else to own is: _________________________________________________ My next medical or mental-health appointment is: _________________________________________________ This page is not a diagnostic test. Any urgent symptom requires immediate professional attention.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Remember This
A mother does not become less important when her baby is born. Protecting her recovery, sleep, mental health and safety is one of the most practical ways a family can protect the baby.
Try This Today
Ask the mother four questions—and allow honest answers:

items: When did you last have a genuine opportunity to rest?
Which responsibility can someone else completely take over today?

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Then act on one answer. Support is not only saying:
“Tell me if you need anything.”
It is noticing what is needed and carrying part of it.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

items: World Health Organization: Recommendations on Maternal and Newborn Care for a Positive Postnatal Experience
World Health Organization: Health Services After Childbirth
World Health Organization: World Health Day 2025—Maternal and Newborn Health Messages
National Health Mission, Government of India: Revised CEmONC Curriculum—Postpartum Care
American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists: Optimising Postpartum Care

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Baby While Caring for the Mother

blocks.text: Emergency numbers, mental-health pathways, domestic-violence resources and postnatal schedules must be localised and verified for the intended Indian edition before publication.
PART III
Growing Through the Early Years
Practical guidance, thoughtful reassurance and clear next steps.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development
pages.epigraph: “Milestones are guideposts—not grades, deadlines, or a competition between children.”

blocks.text: At a family gathering, two babies were placed beside each other on the carpet. They were almost the same age. One was crawling across the room. The other sat quietly, turning a toy over in her hands and studying every tiny detail. Within minutes, the comparisons began. “She isn’t crawling yet?” “He doesn’t say any words?” “Mine was walking by this age.” The mothers smiled politely, but I could see the questions forming behind their smiles. Is my baby falling behind? Comparisons can create worry very quickly. Yet children rarely develop every skill at exactly the same pace.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: One baby may begin moving early but take more time with words. Another may communicate beautifully before showing any interest in walking. A third may observe quietly for weeks and then surprise everyone with several new skills at once. There is room for individual variation. But there is also value in knowing what development generally looks like, noticing when something may need attention, and seeking help early rather than waiting indefinitely. That is what this chapter will help you do.
Growth and Development Are Different

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: We often use the words growth and development as though they mean the same thing. They are connected, but they describe different parts of your child’s progress.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development
Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: A child’s growth is usually monitored by plotting measurements on an appropriate growth chart. The pattern over time is generally more informative than one isolated measurement. A single weight or height does not tell the whole story. Development is observed through the skills a child is gaining and using in everyday life. For example:

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: Does your baby look towards familiar voices?
Are their movements becoming more controlled?
Do they reach for interesting objects?
Do they communicate through sounds, expressions or gestures?
Are they becoming curious about people and their surroundings?

blocks.text: You do not need to assess your child like an examiner. Your role is to observe, enjoy and share any concerns with a qualified professional.
What Is a Developmental Milestone?

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: A developmental milestone is a skill or behaviour that most children can do by a particular age. Milestones help families and healthcare professionals understand how a child is progressing. They are not guarantees that every child will do the same thing on the same day. Think of milestones as signposts on a journey. They help you see the general direction of development. They can also tell you when it may be sensible to stop, look more carefully and ask for guidance. Milestones should never be used to:

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: label a baby as clever or slow;

blocks.text: A milestone checklist is an observation tool, not a report card.
The Five Main Areas of Development
Children develop across several areas at the same time. Looking at the whole child is more helpful than focusing on one skill alone.
1. Gross Motor Development

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: These are movements involving the larger muscles of the body. Examples include:

items: crawling or moving across the floor;

blocks.text: Children do not all use exactly the same route towards independent walking. Some crawl on hands and knees. Some shuffle. Others use different ways to move. The quality, symmetry and progression of movement are often as important as the particular method a child uses.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: 2. Fine Motor and Self-Help Skills
These involve smaller movements of the hands and fingers, as well as increasing participation in daily activities. Examples include:

items: picking up small pieces of food;

blocks.text: These skills gradually allow a child to explore more independently.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: 3. Language and Communication
Communication begins long before the first spoken word. It includes:

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: A child growing up with more than one language may distribute words across those languages. When discussing their development, tell the professional about every language the child hears and uses.
4. Cognitive Development and Play
This is how children think, learn, remember, solve problems and understand their surroundings. You may notice it when a child:

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: uses an object for its purpose;

blocks.text: Play is not separate from learning. For babies and toddlers, play is one of the main ways learning happens.
5. Social and Emotional Development
These skills help children connect with other people and express their needs and feelings. They include:

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: beginning to play alongside or with others.

blocks.text: Social development does not require every child to have the same temperament. A quiet, observant child may interact differently from an outgoing child while still developing healthy connections.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: A Broad Picture of the First Two Years

The following table offers an approximate overview of abilities that often emerge during infancy and toddlerhood. It is not a diagnostic checklist. Use a current milestone guide and your child’s healthcare professional for age-specific assessment.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

rows: Abilities you may begin to notice
Looking at faces, responding to voices or sounds, brief head lifting, increasing social response and gradually smoother movements
Reaching and grasping, vocalising, stronger head and upper-body control, increased interest in people and surroundings
Sitting with less support, transferring objects, varied babbling, responding to familiar people or their name, exploring actively

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

rows: Abilities you may begin to notice
Moving into or out of sitting, pulling towards standing or moving while supported, using gestures, understanding familiar words and picking up small objects
Increasing independent movement, pointing or showing, using meaningful words, following simple familiar directions and imitating everyday activities
More confident walking, running and climbing, growing vocabulary, early word combinations, pretend play and increasing participation in self-care

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Some children gain a skill earlier or later than this broad description. The important questions are:

items: Is your child continuing to gain abilities?
Are there skills that seem unusually difficult?
Has your child stopped using a skill they previously had?

blocks.text: If you are uncertain, ask. You do not need to wait until a concern becomes severe.
Development Is a Pattern, Not One Performance

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: A baby may refuse to demonstrate a skill during an appointment. They may be tired, hungry, uncomfortable or cautious around unfamiliar people. That is why developmental observation considers a pattern rather than one moment. Healthcare professionals may ask questions such as:

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: What does your child usually do at home?
How do they communicate when they want something?
How do they play with familiar objects?
Do they use both sides of the body?
How do they respond to familiar sounds and people?
Have you noticed any loss of skills?

blocks.text: Short videos from home can sometimes help demonstrate something that does not appear during the appointment.
What Is Corrected Age?

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Babies born early may need to be viewed according to their corrected age , especially during the first two years. Corrected age accounts for the number of weeks a baby was born before 40 weeks of pregnancy. The calculation is: Corrected age = actual age − number of weeks born early For example, imagine a baby was born at 32 weeks. That baby arrived eight weeks early. When the baby is four months old by the calendar, their corrected age is approximately two months. It may therefore be more appropriate to compare some developmental abilities with those expected around two months rather than four months.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics’ parent guidance explains that corrected age is commonly used for developmental expectations during the first two years. Your baby’s neonatal or paediatric team can explain how it applies to your child. HealthyChildren.org: Corrected Age for Preemies Corrected age should not be used to dismiss concerns. A premature baby may benefit from closer developmental follow-up, particularly if there were medical complications.
Observation, Screening and Assessment
These terms are related, but they are not interchangeable.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Developmental Surveillance
This is the continuing process of observing a child’s development over time. It includes:
Developmental Screening

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Screening uses a structured, age-appropriate tool to identify children who may benefit from a closer assessment. A screening result is not a diagnosis. It may indicate that:

items: development appears to be progressing as expected;
the child should be monitored more closely;

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Widely used paediatric guidance recommends standardised developmental screening at 9, 18 and 30 months, with autism-specific screening at 18 and 24 months—or whenever a concern arises. Screening schedules and tools may differ between countries and healthcare systems, so follow the plan advised by your child’s paediatrician. CDC: Developmental Milestones
Developmental Evaluation
This is a more detailed assessment conducted when there is a concern. Depending on the child’s needs, it may involve:

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: a psychologist or another qualified specialist.

blocks.text: An evaluation helps determine the child’s strengths, areas needing support and appropriate next steps.
When “Wait and See” May Not Be Enough

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Parents are often told: “Every child develops differently.” That statement is true. But it should not become a reason to ignore a genuine concern. Early assessment does not automatically mean something is seriously wrong. It simply helps families understand what is happening sooner. If support is needed, beginning early can give the child more opportunities to practise important skills while development is moving rapidly. You do not need to choose between panicking and doing nothing. There is a sensible middle path:
Notice. Record. Ask. Assess if advised. Support.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Signs Worth Discussing Promptly
Speak with your child’s healthcare professional if you notice concerns such as:

items: limited response to sound or familiar voices;
limited eye contact or social response that concerns you;

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: difficulty using one side of the body;
limited use of gestures, sounds or words for the child’s stage;
development that appears to have stopped progressing;
any loss of a previously acquired skill.

blocks.text: Loss of skills should always be discussed promptly. This list cannot cover every possibility. A parent’s concern is itself a valid reason to ask for an assessment.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: A Concern Does Not Need to Become a Conclusion
Perhaps your baby does not respond consistently when called. That observation does not tell you why. Possible next steps might include checking:

items: whether the baby was engaged in something else;
whether the response occurs in other situations;
whether communication and social development need closer observation.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Good assessment begins with curiosity, not assumptions. Try to avoid diagnosing your child from a short social-media video or a single online checklist. Many developmental signs can have more than one explanation. Use online information to prepare questions—not to replace professional evaluation.
How to Prepare for a Developmental Appointment
If you have a concern, a few notes can make the conversation more useful. Write down:

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: whether it happens occasionally or consistently;
what your child can already do;
whether any skills have been lost;
pregnancy, birth or neonatal information that may be relevant;
any family history the clinician should know;
feeding, sleeping, hearing or vision concerns.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: If appropriate, bring a short video showing the behaviour or difficulty at home. Questions you might ask include:

items: Is this variation within the expected developmental range?
Would a formal developmental screening be helpful?
Should hearing or vision be checked?
What can we practise naturally at home?
Which changes would require an earlier appointment?

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Ask for explanations in plain language. You deserve to understand the plan.
Supporting Development at Home
Children do not need shelves full of specialised toys to develop well. Many of the most valuable learning experiences happen through ordinary interaction.
Talk
Describe what you are doing. “You’re holding the red cup.” “Let’s put your socks on.” “Can you hear the dog?” This gives language meaning and context.
Listen and Respond

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: When your baby makes a sound, respond. When your toddler points, notice what they are showing you. These small exchanges teach the rhythm of communication.
Read

Read simple books, name pictures and allow the child to turn pages when able. You do not need to finish every story. Following the child’s interest is often more useful than completing the book perfectly.
Sing

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Songs combine language, memory, rhythm and connection. Your voice does not need to be trained. To your child, it is already familiar and valuable.
Allow Safe Floor Play
Supervised floor play gives babies opportunities to stretch, turn, reach and explore movement. Use an age-appropriate safe space and follow medical advice if your baby has specific positioning needs.
Follow the Child’s Lead

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: If your child is studying a spoon, talk about the spoon. If they are stacking cups, join the activity. Learning is often strongest when it begins with the child’s own curiosity.
Avoid Turning Play into Constant Testing

Try not to spend the day asking: “What colour is this?” “What sound does this make?” “Show me the ball.” Sometimes simply playing together creates better learning than repeatedly testing what a child knows. Responsive caregiving—notice, respond and interact—supports early learning and development. WHO: Care for Child Development

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Early Support Is Not a Label

Some parents fear that seeking help will place a label on their child. Try to think of early support differently. If a child has difficulty seeing, we do not wait for them to struggle more before arranging an eye examination. If hearing is uncertain, we check it. Development deserves the same practical approach. Support may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: help for the family in adapting routines.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: India’s Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram includes screening and referral pathways for developmental delays and other childhood conditions, including support through District Early Intervention Centres in participating public-health systems. Availability and referral procedures vary locally, so ask your paediatrician, community health worker or government health facility what services operate in your area. National Health Mission: Early Childhood Development and RBSK
Seeking support does not change who your child is. It simply gives you better information about how to help them.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Comparing Siblings, Twins and Friends

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Even children raised in the same home can develop differently. One sibling may speak early. Another may be physically adventurous. Twins may also reach milestones at different times, particularly if they were born prematurely or had different medical experiences. Comparison becomes harmful when it prevents us from seeing the child in front of us. A more useful comparison is: What can my child do now that they could not do a few weeks or months ago? That question helps you notice progress without turning childhood into a race.
Common Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Mistake 1: Treating a Milestone Age as an Exact Deadline
Developmental ages are guides. Look at the overall pattern and use current professional guidance.
Mistake 2: Comparing Only One Skill
A child’s development includes movement, communication, learning, play and social connection.
Mistake 3: Assuming Premature Babies Should Always Be Compared by Calendar Age
Discuss corrected age and follow-up with the baby’s medical team.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Mistake 4: Waiting Because Someone Says, “Boys Are Always Late”
General statements about sex, temperament or family history should not replace developmental screening.
Mistake 5: Believing Screening Means Diagnosis
Screening identifies whether closer evaluation may be helpful. It does not, by itself, diagnose a condition.
Mistake 6: Using Drills or Devices to Force Development

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Children usually benefit more from safe opportunities to move, play, communicate and interact than from pressure to perform.
Mistake 7: Ignoring a Loss of Skills
Regression should be discussed promptly with a qualified professional.
🚩 Red-Flag Warning
Seek professional advice promptly if:

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: your child loses a skill they previously used;
movement is consistently one-sided, unusually stiff or unusually floppy;
you are concerned about hearing, vision, feeding or swallowing;
your baby rarely responds to sounds, faces or interaction;
communication or movement appears to have stopped progressing;
your instinct repeatedly tells you that something is not right.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: If one professional dismisses a persistent concern without assessing it, it is reasonable to seek another qualified opinion. Early assessment is not overreacting. It is responsible observation.
Your Developmental Check-In
Use this short checklist before a routine appointment.
What I Have Noticed
☐ New movements or physical skills
☐ New sounds, gestures or words
☐ New ways of playing or solving problems
☐ New social responses
☐ Feeding and self-help progress
☐ Any skill that seems difficult

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: ☐ Any skill that has disappeared
☐ Questions about hearing or vision
☐ Questions about corrected age
☐ Videos or examples I would like to show
You do not need to complete a perfect record. A few clear observations are enough to begin a useful conversation.
💛 Remember This

Milestones help us notice development; they should never make a child feel measured against someone else’s journey. Variation can be normal. Persistent concerns deserve attention. And asking early is always better than worrying alone.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: 🌼 Try This Today
Spend ten quiet minutes watching your child play without directing the activity. Notice:

items: how they solve a small problem;
how they respond when you join them.

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Then write down: One ability that is growing: ________________________________ One thing I would like to ask about: ________________________________ That small observation is not an examination. It is simply a mother getting to know her child more clearly.
Authoritative References

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

items: World Health Organization — Improving Early Childhood Development
National Health Mission — Early Childhood Development and RBSK

pages.chapterTitle: Understanding Growth and Development

blocks.text: Editorial note for the Indian edition: Developmental screening tools, referral pathways and public services should be verified against current national and state guidance before publication. This chapter provides general education and does not replace an individual developmental assessment.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby
pages.epigraph: “Beginning solid food is not about replacing milk overnight. It is about helping your baby discover food—one safe spoonful at a time.”

blocks.text: When Ananya’s baby was almost six months old, everyone seemed to have advice. Her mother suggested mashed banana. Her mother-in-law recommended soft khichdi. A friend sent her a detailed feeding chart with exact quantities for every hour of the day. Social media offered another hundred opinions. “Never begin with fruit.” “Only use single-grain cereals.” “Baby-led weaning is the only natural method.” “Do not allow the baby to touch the food.” “Your baby should finish this much at every meal.” By the time Ananya placed the first spoon in front of her daughter, she was no longer excited.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: She was nervous. What if the food was wrong? What if the baby refused it? What if she gagged? What if she developed an allergy? Her daughter accepted half a spoonful, pushed most of it back out and then tried to grab the bowl. Ananya thought the meal had failed. It had not. Her baby had tasted something unfamiliar, felt a new texture and discovered that food could be held, smeared and explored. That was learning. The first weeks of complementary feeding are not a test of how much your baby eats. They are the beginning of a gradual transition from milk feeding towards family food.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: There will be mess. There will be rejected foods. There will be days when your baby eats happily and others when two spoonfuls seem to be enough. Your responsibility is to provide safe, nourishing and age-appropriate food. Your baby gradually learns how much to eat.
What Is Complementary Feeding?

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Complementary feeding means offering foods and drinks in addition to breast milk or infant formula when milk alone is no longer sufficient to meet a growing baby’s nutritional needs. The World Health Organization recommends beginning complementary foods at around six months of age. The complementary-feeding period generally continues from 6 to 23 months while children gradually learn to eat family foods. WHO: Complementary Feeding The word complementary is important.
Food initially complements milk; it does not suddenly replace it. For a baby between 6 and 12 months:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: breast milk or appropriately prepared infant formula remains an important source of nutrition;
food introduces additional energy and nutrients;
the number, variety and texture of meals increase over time.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Families who breastfeed may continue for two years or beyond if it works for mother and child. UNICEF India similarly advises beginning soft, diverse, home-cooked complementary foods at six months while continuing breastfeeding. UNICEF India: Starting Solids Right If your baby has a medical condition, was born prematurely, has difficulty swallowing or is growing poorly, ask the child’s healthcare team for an individual feeding plan.
Is My Baby Ready?

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Age matters, but readiness matters too. Most babies become developmentally ready for complementary foods at around six months. Look for several signs together:

items: the baby can sit upright with support and remain reasonably steady;
head and neck control are good;
the baby shows interest in food;
the mouth opens when food is offered;
the baby can move food backwards and swallow it rather than pushing everything out automatically;
the baby reaches for objects and brings them towards the mouth.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: One sign alone is not enough. For example, watching you eat does not automatically mean a four-month-old is ready for solid food. Introducing complementary food before four months is not recommended. Around six months, discuss readiness during your baby’s health visit, particularly if you are uncertain. CDC: Introducing Solid Foods
Readiness Is Not the Same as Teething

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: A baby does not need teeth before beginning soft foods. Babies can manage many appropriately prepared textures with their gums. The important questions are whether the baby is developmentally ready and whether the food’s shape and texture are safe.
What Should the First Food Be?
There is no single compulsory first food for every baby. A first food should ideally be:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: suitable for the family’s culture and diet;
easy to offer in a small quantity.

blocks.text: Indian families have many useful options, including:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: softly mashed khichdi enriched with a little oil or ghee;
soft ragi or another cereal preparation;
well-cooked egg in an appropriate texture;
finely prepared meat or fish without bones, where eaten;
soft tofu or paneer prepared safely.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: The goal is not to identify one “perfect” food. The goal is to build variety over time. A watery rice preparation may fill the stomach without providing enough energy or nutrients. Complementary food should generally be thick enough to stay on the spoon rather than being mostly water.
Building a Balanced Bowl
Babies have small stomachs. That means each spoonful should work hard. A useful meal can combine foods from several groups.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

rows: Rice, wheat, ragi, oats, millet, potato, sweet potato
Dal, beans, chickpeas, egg, fish, chicken, meat, tofu, paneer
Pumpkin, carrot, spinach, peas, beans, banana, papaya, pear, seasonal fruit

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

rows: A small amount of oil, ghee, nut paste or seed paste prepared safely
Unsweetened pasteurised curd, paneer or cheese in safe textures

blocks.text: A simple bowl might be:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: a small amount of oil or ghee;
egg, fish, meat, curd or another suitable protein food.

blocks.text: Not every bowl must contain every food group. Balance can be achieved across the day.
Iron Deserves Special Attention
Around six months, babies require reliable sources of iron in addition to milk feeding. Iron supports blood production and brain development. Useful iron-containing foods include:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: meat, chicken or fish where eaten;
green leafy vegetables as part of a varied diet.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Iron from plant foods is generally absorbed less easily than iron from animal foods. Offering vitamin-C-rich foods—such as suitable fruits or vegetables—alongside plant sources can help. Do not begin iron medicines or supplements unless advised by a qualified healthcare professional. Some babies, including those born prematurely, may receive an individual supplementation plan.
How Much Food Should I Offer?
In the beginning, your baby may eat only a few spoonfuls. That can be completely normal. Appetite varies according to:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: WHO guidance generally suggests:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

rows: Meals 3–4 times a day, with 1–2 nutritious snacks as needed

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: These are broad population guidelines, not a rule that a baby must finish a fixed bowl at every meal. WHO: Complementary Feeding Begin with small portions. Offer more if the baby remains interested. Stop when the baby clearly shows fullness.
Responsive Feeding

Responsive feeding means paying attention to the child’s hunger and fullness signals while offering patient encouragement. It is neither complete adult control nor leaving a baby entirely alone with food.
Possible Hunger Signals
A baby may:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Possible Fullness Signals
A baby may:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Responding to fullness teaches children that their bodies’ signals are respected. A parent decides:

items: what safe and nourishing food is offered;
when and where the meal occurs.

blocks.text: The child gradually communicates:
Encouragement Is Helpful; Force Is Not
You may gently offer a food again. You may eat with the child, smile and demonstrate. You may allow the baby to touch and explore. But avoid:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: chasing the child around with a spoon;
distracting with a screen to make the child swallow;
insisting that the bowl must be emptied.

blocks.text: Force-feeding can turn mealtimes into a struggle and make it harder for a child to recognise hunger and fullness.
Texture Must Grow with the Child
Beginning food does not mean serving smooth purée for many months. Food texture should progress with the child’s abilities.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

rows: Soft mashed, puréed or thick semi-solid food
Mashed food with soft lumps and thicker consistency
Soft finger foods and increasingly varied textures
Chopped or modified family food that is soft and safely prepared

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: The exact progression depends on the child. The aim is to provide safe opportunities to learn:

items: biting and chewing with the gums;

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Keeping food completely smooth for too long may limit opportunities to practise more advanced eating skills. Indian infant and young-child feeding guidance encourages age-appropriate progression rather than relying indefinitely on thin or fully blended food. National Health Mission: Infant and Young Child Feeding Practices
Spoon-Feeding, Finger Foods or Both?

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Families sometimes feel pressured to choose one feeding philosophy. You do not have to. Some babies enjoy being spoon-fed. Some prefer picking up food. Many benefit from a combination. What matters is that:

items: food is soft and prepared safely;
the baby is allowed to participate;

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: A baby who wants to hold the spoon can be given another spoon. A baby who enjoys soft finger foods can explore them while also receiving mashed food from a caregiver. There is no prize for following a feeding label perfectly.
Gagging and Choking Are Not the Same
This distinction is important.
Gagging
Gagging is a protective reflex that helps move food away from the airway. A gagging baby may:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: It can look frightening, especially when textures are new.
Choking
Choking occurs when the airway is blocked. A choking child may:

items: be unable to cry, cough or make sound;

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Choking is an emergency. Every caregiver should learn age-appropriate choking first aid from a qualified instructor. Reading about it is not a substitute for practising the correct response. Never put your fingers blindly into a baby’s mouth to remove food. This can push an object deeper.
Preventing Choking
To reduce risk:

items: seat the baby upright for every meal;
match texture and shape to the child’s ability;

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: remove bones, seeds and hard pits;
cut round foods lengthwise and into safe shapes;
spread nut butter thinly or mix a small amount into another food;
avoid eating while walking, crawling, lying down or travelling in a moving vehicle;
keep small non-food objects out of reach.

blocks.text: Common choking hazards include:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: spoonfuls or lumps of nut butter;
hard raw carrot or apple pieces;
sausages cut into round coin shapes;
hard pieces of food the child cannot manage.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Food preparation changes the risk. A nut ground into a smooth powder or paste is different from a whole nut. A cooked, mashed carrot is different from a hard raw piece. CDC: Choking Hazards
Introducing Foods That Commonly Cause Allergy
Common allergenic foods include:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: For most babies, these foods do not need to be delayed unnecessarily once complementary feeding has begun and the food can be prepared in a safe form. Introduce one new allergenic food at a time, in a small amount, during the day when you can observe the child. Examples include:

items: smooth peanut paste thinned into another familiar food;
finely ground nut powder mixed safely into food;
well-cooked fish with every bone removed.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Whole nuts and thick spoonfuls of nut butter are choking hazards. If your baby has severe eczema, a known food allergy or a previous immediate reaction, speak with the child’s doctor before introducing peanut or other concerning foods. HealthyChildren.org: Introducing Common Food Allergens
Possible Signs of an Allergic Reaction
These may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: swelling of the lips, face, tongue or eyes;

blocks.text: Breathing difficulty, facial or tongue swelling, collapse or a severe widespread reaction requires emergency medical care. A delayed change in stool or a mild rash may have many causes. Record what happened and discuss it with the child’s healthcare professional rather than diagnosing the allergy yourself.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Foods and Drinks to Avoid
Honey Before 12 Months
Do not give honey to a baby younger than 12 months because of the risk of infant botulism. This includes honey:
CDC: Foods and Drinks to Avoid or Limit
Cow’s Milk as the Main Drink Before 12 Months

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Cow’s milk should not replace breast milk or infant formula as the main milk drink before 12 months. Pasteurised curd, cheese and other suitable milk products may be used as complementary foods if tolerated. After 12 months, pasteurised whole cow’s milk may form part of a varied diet, but excessive milk can reduce appetite for iron-rich food. Ask your paediatrician about quantity and alternatives if your child has an allergy or follows a restricted diet.
Unpasteurised Food and Drink

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Avoid raw or unpasteurised milk, cheese and juice because they may contain harmful organisms.
Added Sugar and Sugary Drinks
Babies and young toddlers do not need:

items: sweets or desserts as routine foods.

blocks.text: Whole fruit provides fibre and other nutrients that juice does not.
Excessively Salty and Highly Processed Food
Limit:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: commercial foods marketed to toddlers that are high in salt or sugar.

blocks.text: A baby can share modified family food, but remove the baby’s portion before adding excessive salt, chilli or sugar.
Tea and Coffee
Tea and coffee are not appropriate drinks for babies. They can displace nutritious food and may interfere with iron absorption.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Water and Cups

Once complementary feeding begins at around six months, small amounts of clean, safe drinking water can be offered with meals. Encourage cup drinking gradually. An open cup held by an adult or an age-appropriate training cup can help build drinking skills. Avoid allowing a child to carry a bottle throughout the day or fall asleep with a bottle containing milk or sweet drinks. Prolonged contact can contribute to dental problems and may reduce appetite for meals.
Food Hygiene

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Babies are more vulnerable to food-borne illness than adults. Use simple but consistent hygiene practices:

items: Wash your hands before preparing or feeding food.
Wash the child’s hands before meals.
Use clean utensils, bowls and cups.
Cook eggs, meat and fish thoroughly.
Separate raw foods from cooked foods.
Refrigerate perishable food promptly where refrigeration is available.
Reheat stored food thoroughly and allow it to cool safely.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: Discard food contaminated by the baby’s saliva rather than repeatedly storing and reheating it.
Avoid feeding from damaged, swollen or leaking packages.

blocks.text: A separate elaborate kitchen is not necessary. Clean preparation is.
A Practical Day of Family Food
The following example is intended to show variety—not prescribe exact quantities.
For a Baby Beginning Complementary Food

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: Breast milk or infant formula according to the baby’s usual pattern
A small serving of thick mashed dal and rice

blocks.text: For an Older Baby Managing More Textures

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: Dal, rice and cooked vegetables with a little oil or ghee
Soft egg, paneer, tofu, fish or another suitable protein food

blocks.text: For a Young Toddler

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: Regular milk feeds appropriate for age
Three family meals modified for texture and spice

blocks.text: A child does not need a completely different menu from the family. Family food can often be adapted by changing:
When a Baby Refuses Food

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Refusal does not always mean dislike. The baby may be:

items: full from a recent milk feed;

blocks.text: Try again another day without pressure. Some children need many calm exposures before accepting a food. You can help by:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: ending the meal when the child is clearly finished.

blocks.text: Do not immediately replace every refused meal with biscuits, juice or another preferred snack. That can teach the child to hold out for the alternative.
Feeding During Illness
