Appetite often decreases during illness. Continue appropriate milk feeds and offer:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: After recovery, appetite and meal frequency may need gentle encouragement to help restore lost nutrition. Seek medical advice if the child:

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: becomes unusually sleepy or difficult to wake;
is losing weight or not recovering as expected.

blocks.text: Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Making Food Too Thin

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Watery food can fill the stomach without providing enough energy. Offer thick, nutrient-dense food suitable for the child’s swallowing ability.
Mistake 2: Depending on One Food
Rice cereal, banana, dal water or khichdi alone cannot provide every nutrient. Build variety gradually.
Mistake 3: Delaying Texture Progression
Children need safe opportunities to practise chewing and handling soft pieces.
Mistake 4: Comparing Portions

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: One child’s appetite is not a standard for another child. Look at growth, development and eating patterns over time.
Mistake 5: Using Screens to Make the Baby Eat
A screen may increase the amount swallowed in the moment while preventing the child from noticing food and fullness signals.
Mistake 6: Adding Sugar So the Baby Will Accept Food
Babies can learn to enjoy the natural tastes of food.
Mistake 7: Force-Feeding

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Pressure can turn eating into conflict.
Mistake 8: Believing Packaged Baby Food Is Automatically Better
Commercial food can sometimes be convenient, but home-cooked food can be equally appropriate when it is balanced, hygienic and safely prepared. Read labels for:
🚩 Red-Flag Warning

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Speak with a healthcare professional if your baby:

items: coughs, chokes or struggles to breathe repeatedly during feeds;
has a wet or unusual voice or breathing sound after swallowing;
takes an unusually long time to complete feeds;
appears distressed or in pain while eating;
has poor weight gain or weight loss;
develops signs of an allergic reaction;

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: is unable to sit or control the head sufficiently for safe feeding at the expected stage;

blocks.text: Feeding difficulties are not always caused by behaviour. They can be related to swallowing, oral-motor skills, reflux, allergy, sensory needs, pain or another medical concern.
Your Complementary-Feeding Checklist
Before beginning, ask:
☐ Is my baby around six months old and developmentally ready?
☐ Can my baby sit upright with good head control?

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: ☐ Have I discussed any medical or allergy concerns with the doctor?
☐ Is the food soft and safely prepared?
☐ Does the meal include nutrient-dense food rather than only liquid?
☐ Is an adult supervising continuously?
☐ Do I know the difference between gagging and choking?
☐ Have regular caregivers learned infant choking first aid?
☐ Am I progressing textures as skills improve?
☐ Am I responding to hunger and fullness rather than forcing?
☐ Am I gradually building variety?
☐ Am I continuing appropriate milk feeds?

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: You do not need to perfect all of this in one week. Complementary feeding is a gradual education—for both baby and parent.
💛 Remember This

Your job is to offer safe, nourishing food with patience. Your baby’s job is to learn, explore and gradually show how much they need. A rejected spoon is not failure. A messy tray is not failure. A small appetite on one day is not failure. The aim is not to raise a baby who always empties the bowl. It is to help your child build a safe and healthy relationship with food.

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: 🌼 Try This Today
Choose one ordinary family meal. Now ask:

items: How could I make this soft enough for my baby?
Which ingredient provides iron or protein?
Could I add a vegetable or fruit?
What should I remove because it is too salty, spicy, hard or unsafe?
How can I allow my baby to participate?

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Write down one adapted family meal: Our meal: ________________________________ Baby’s safe version: ________________________________ That is the beginning of something wonderfully practical. Your baby does not need a perfect feeding chart. Your baby needs safe food, growing variety, patient practice—and someone calm enough to let learning take time.
Authoritative References

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

items: World Health Organization — Guideline for Complementary Feeding of Infants and Young Children 6–23 Months
National Health Mission — Guidelines for Enhancing Optimal Infant and Young Child Feeding Practices
CDC — When, What and How to Introduce Solid Foods
CDC — Foods and Drinks to Avoid or Limit

pages.chapterTitle: Feeding Your Growing Baby

blocks.text: Editorial note for the Indian edition: Feeding guidance should be checked against current Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, National Health Mission and Indian Academy of Pediatrics recommendations before publication. Portion needs, allergy advice and feeding plans may differ for premature babies and children with medical, developmental or growth concerns. This chapter provides general education and does not replace individual medical or dietetic advice.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights
pages.epigraph: “Good sleep is not created by winning a battle with your child. It grows from safety, rhythm, patience and realistic expectations.”

blocks.text: When Kavya’s son was nine months old, a familiar question followed her everywhere. “Is he sleeping through the night yet?” Sometimes she answered honestly. Sometimes she simply smiled. Her son could fall asleep peacefully one evening and wake three times the next. A new tooth, a cold, an exciting developmental change or a day spent away from home could alter everything. Kavya began to wonder whether she had created a “bad sleeper.” Then an older mother told her something she never forgot. “Your baby is not giving you a performance review. He is growing.”

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: That did not make the interrupted nights easy. But it helped Kavya stop treating every difficult night as proof that she had done something wrong. Sleep changes continually during the first two years. Babies gradually develop longer sleep periods, but they still wake. Toddlers may resist bedtime because they are discovering independence. Illness, travel, teething, hunger, separation anxiety and developmental changes can all disturb an established routine. Healthy sleep habits matter. So does accepting that no routine will make childhood completely predictable.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: What Normal Sleep Looks Like

Sleep is not one long, unbroken state. Children and adults move through sleep cycles and briefly surface between them. Sometimes a child returns to sleep without help. At other times, they call for a caregiver. The phrase sleeping through the night can be misleading. It does not always mean twelve uninterrupted hours. People may use it to describe five, six or eight hours, or a child who wakes briefly and returns to sleep. Instead of chasing one popular definition, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: Is my child getting enough sleep across 24 hours?
Does the child usually seem rested and alert?
Is the routine manageable for our family?
Are frequent wakings connected to hunger, discomfort, illness or another concern?
Is the child’s sleep improving gradually with development?

blocks.text: How Much Sleep Does a Child Need?
Sleep needs vary. The following ranges include naps and nighttime sleep.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights
Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: These ranges are guides, not exact targets that every child must reach daily. Some children naturally sleep near the lower end and remain healthy and energetic. Others need more. Look at the child as well as the clock. Signs that sleep may be insufficient include:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: regular struggles caused by an overtired child;
sleeping considerably more or less than expected alongside other symptoms.

blocks.text: If you are concerned, record your child’s sleep for one or two weeks and discuss the pattern with the child’s doctor. HealthyChildren.org: Healthy Sleep Habits

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Sleep Changes Across the First Two Years
Around 6–9 Months
Many babies begin to develop more recognisable nap and nighttime patterns. However, sleep may change when babies:

items: learn to roll, sit or crawl;
experience separation from caregivers more strongly;
develop discomfort from illness or teething;

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Some babies still need nighttime feeding. Whether your baby needs it depends on age, growth, milk intake, health and individual circumstances. Do not remove night feeds simply because another baby of the same age no longer needs them. Ask your paediatrician if you are unsure, particularly if your child was premature or has growth concerns.
Around 9–12 Months

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: The child may become more aware that you can leave the room. Bedtime protests can increase, even when the child is tired. A calm and consistent response helps communicate: “You are safe. It is time to rest. I will return.”
Around 12–18 Months
Many children gradually move towards one main daytime nap, although the timing varies. Toddlers may resist sleep because:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Around 18–24 Months
Bedtime boundaries often become more important. A toddler may ask for:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: a parent to remain in the room.

blocks.text: These requests do not make the child manipulative. Toddlers are learning how much influence they have and whether routines remain predictable. Warm, clear and consistent limits usually work better than anger or lengthy negotiation.
What People Call a “Sleep Regression”

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Parents often hear about four-month, eight-month, twelve-month or eighteen-month sleep regressions. These labels may help families describe a temporary change, but sleep does not follow a precise regression calendar. A previously settled child may begin waking more because of:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Instead of assuming every difficult period is a predictable regression, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: Is the child unwell or uncomfortable?
Is the child getting too much or too little daytime sleep?
Is the sleep environment safe and comfortable?
Does the pattern persist long enough to need professional advice?

blocks.text: A Rhythm Is More Useful Than a Rigid Schedule

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: A rigid schedule says: “The child must sleep at 10:02.” A rhythm says: “After lunch, we slow down and prepare for a nap.” Young children often respond well to predictable sequences. For example: Wake → milk or breakfast → play → meal → quiet time → nap The exact clock time may change slightly while the order remains familiar. A family rhythm can include:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: a reasonably consistent morning wake time;
active floor play and outdoor time where practical;

blocks.text: Predictability helps the body and mind prepare for sleep. It does not require running your home like a military camp.
Watching the Child’s Sleep Signals

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Children often show tiredness before they become extremely upset. Early signals may include:
Later signals may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: An overtired child does not necessarily fall asleep more easily. They may become harder to calm. Try to begin the wind-down routine when the child is tired but not completely overwhelmed.
Building a Bedtime Routine
A bedtime routine is a short sequence of familiar, calming activities repeated in approximately the same order. It might look like this:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: Offer the final appropriate feed or snack.
Change the nappy and put on sleep clothes.
Read one or two short books.
Give a cuddle and say the same goodnight phrase.
Place the child in the safe sleep space.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: The routine does not need to be elaborate. Fifteen to thirty calm minutes may be enough. The most useful routine is one your family can repeat consistently—even on a tiring day. A nightly bath is optional. If bathing excites your child or is impractical, leave it out. The NHS recommends a simple, soothing and predictable bedtime routine, with calmer activity as bedtime approaches. NHS: Helping Your Baby to Sleep
Day and Night Should Feel Different
To support the developing body clock:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: During the Day

items: spend time outdoors where air quality and weather permit;
keep naps in a safe sleep environment without making the entire home unnaturally silent.

blocks.text: During the Night

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: complete feeds and nappy changes calmly;
return the child to sleep with minimal stimulation.

blocks.text: Over time, these differences help the child understand that daytime is active and nighttime is quieter.
Safe Sleep During the First Year
Healthy sleep must always begin with safe sleep. For every sleep during the baby’s first year:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: place the baby on the back to begin sleep;
use a firm, flat sleep surface;
keep the sleep space free from pillows, loose blankets, soft toys, cot bumpers and positioners;
keep the baby’s face and head uncovered;
keep the sleep environment free from tobacco and nicotine exposure;
do not use weighted blankets, weighted swaddles or weighted sleep products;
avoid sleeping with the baby on a sofa or armchair;

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: follow current medical guidance about room-sharing and infant sleep.

blocks.text: If a baby independently rolls from back to front and front to back, continue placing the baby down on the back. You generally do not need to repeatedly turn a baby who can reposition independently, provided the sleep space remains firm, flat and empty. Stop swaddling when the baby shows signs of attempting to roll. A child with a medical condition may receive individual positioning advice. Follow the treating professional’s written guidance rather than general internet advice. CDC: Helping Babies Sleep Safely

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: “But My Baby Only Sleeps in My Arms”

Many babies fall asleep easily while being held because they feel warmth, movement and closeness. That is not a character flaw. Nor does comforting a baby automatically create a lifelong sleep problem. However, a sleeping baby can be at serious risk if an exhausted caregiver falls asleep:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: while sitting upright with the baby;
with the baby on an unsafe adult bed;
while surrounded by pillows or loose bedding.

blocks.text: If you feel you may fall asleep, place the baby in the safe sleep space or ask another alert adult to take over. Planning for exhaustion is more useful than believing you will always remain awake through determination alone.
Sleep Associations

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: A sleep association is something a child connects with falling asleep. Examples include:

items: a comfort object for an older child;

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Sleep associations are not automatically bad. The practical question is: Does this association work safely and sustainably for our family? Rocking a baby to sleep may be perfectly manageable. But if the child wakes every hour and requires twenty minutes of vigorous rocking each time, the family may want to make gradual changes. You do not need to remove every source of comfort. You may simply add other consistent signals:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Over time, the child learns that several cues—not only one—signal sleep.
Feeding to Sleep

Feeding naturally makes many babies sleepy. There is no reason to feel guilty if your baby sometimes falls asleep while feeding. As the child grows, you may decide to separate the final feed from sleep slightly if:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: the child wakes repeatedly and requires feeding every time;
feeding has become the only possible settling method;
you want another caregiver to manage bedtime;

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: A gradual change might be: Feed → brush teeth → story → cuddle → bed Move the feed earlier by a few minutes rather than changing everything at once. Night weaning should be considered individually. Age alone does not tell you whether a child is ready. Growth, nutrition, breastfeeding goals, health and family circumstances all matter.
Does a Baby Need to Fall Asleep Independently?

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Independent settling is a skill some families choose to encourage. It is not a measure of a baby’s intelligence, emotional security or parental success. There are many approaches, including:

items: placing the child down awake and remaining nearby;
sitting beside the cot and moving farther away over time;
continuing responsive settling until the child matures.

blocks.text: No single method suits every child or family. Before choosing an approach, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: Can the caregivers follow it calmly and consistently?
Does it respect our family’s values?
Does the method worsen parental distress?

blocks.text: Avoid anyone who promises that every healthy baby will sleep through the night within a fixed number of days.
What to Do When Your Child Wakes

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Before responding, pause long enough to understand what is happening—unless the child sounds distressed or unsafe. Ask:

items: Is this a brief sound between sleep cycles?
Is the child too hot or cold?
Is there fever, coughing, congestion or pain?
Has something changed in the routine?

blocks.text: Keep nighttime responses:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: This does not mean ignoring a child who needs you. It means communicating that nighttime is safe, gentle and uneventful.
Night Waking in Toddlers
A toddler who leaves the bed repeatedly may need a clear boundary. Try:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: Use one short phrase: “It is sleep time now.”

blocks.text: The first few nights may involve many repetitions. Consistency is difficult when you are tired, but changing the response every ten minutes can make the boundary harder to understand. If the child is frightened, comfort them. A boundary can be both kind and firm.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Naps
Naps support the sleep needs of babies and toddlers, but nap patterns change considerably. A child may be ready to drop a nap when they consistently:

items: take a very long time to fall asleep;
sleep too late in the day and then cannot settle at bedtime;
shift naturally towards a longer midday nap.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Do not remove a nap after one unusual day. Watch the pattern over one or two weeks. A child dropping a nap may temporarily need:

items: extra patience late in the day.

blocks.text: Screens and Sleep
Bright, stimulating screen content close to bedtime can make winding down harder. For toddlers and older children:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: stop screens before the bedtime routine;
avoid keeping televisions on in the sleep area;
do not use a phone as the child’s usual settling tool;
replace screen time with a book, song, quiet play or conversation.

blocks.text: For infants, screen viewing is not a substitute for responsive interaction. WHO guidance connects healthy sleep with active play and reduced sedentary screen time during early childhood. WHO: Guidelines for Children Under Five
Comfort Objects

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: A favourite blanket or soft toy may comfort an older baby or toddler, but loose soft objects should not be placed in an infant’s sleep space during the first year. After infancy, consider:

items: whether ribbons, cords or buttons create a hazard;
whether the child can use it safely.

blocks.text: A comfort object should support the routine, not create a new safety problem.
Teeth and Bedtime
Once teeth appear:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: clean them twice daily with age-appropriate fluoride toothpaste according to professional guidance;
include brushing in the bedtime routine;
avoid allowing a child to sleep with a bottle containing milk, juice or sweetened liquid;
avoid frequent sugary drinks and snacks.

blocks.text: If milk is given at bedtime, aim to brush afterwards as the routine develops. Ask your paediatrician or paediatric dentist about the appropriate toothpaste quantity and the timing of the first dental visit.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: When Parents Disagree About Sleep

One parent may want strict timing. Another may prefer following the child’s cues. A grandparent may believe the baby should be kept awake during the day to “make them sleep at night.” Before bedtime becomes an argument between adults, agree on a few essentials:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: how long to wait before responding;
what circumstances require feeding or medical attention;
which changes you will try consistently for at least several days.

blocks.text: The goal is not for every caregiver to have the same personality. The child simply benefits from receiving a reasonably predictable message.
Protecting the Parent’s Sleep

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: A child’s sleep problem often becomes a family health problem. Severe sleep deprivation can affect:
Whenever possible:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: ask a trusted person to help during the day;
rest rather than using every nap to complete chores;
tell someone if exhaustion is becoming unmanageable.

blocks.text: If you feel persistently hopeless, panicked, unusually angry or frightened that you may harm yourself or the baby, seek urgent professional help. You deserve care too.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: When Sleep Needs Medical Attention
Not every sleep difficulty is behavioural. Consult a healthcare professional if your child:

items: pauses, gasps or struggles to breathe during sleep;
becomes blue, grey or unusually pale;

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: sweats excessively while struggling to breathe;
appears unusually sleepy throughout the day;
has repeated unusual movements or episodes during sleep;
wakes persistently because of apparent pain;
has poor growth alongside feeding and sleep concerns;
sleeps very little despite a consistent routine;
has sleep disruption affecting development or family safety.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Record what you observe. A short video of unusual breathing or movement may help the clinician, provided filming does not delay emergency care. Breathing difficulty, blue colouring, prolonged pauses or inability to wake the child normally requires urgent medical attention.
A Seven-Day Sleep Observation
Before changing several things at once, observe for one week. Record:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights
Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

rows: How the child returned to sleep

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: After seven days, look for patterns. Perhaps bedtime is occurring too late. Perhaps the final nap ends very close to bedtime. Perhaps the child is genuinely hungry. Perhaps the routine changes every evening. One week of observation can provide more useful information than one exhausted night.
Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Expecting Every Baby to Sleep Through the Night at the Same Age
Sleep develops differently. Growth, feeding, temperament and health all matter.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Mistake 2: Keeping a Baby Awake to Create Better Night Sleep
An overtired baby may settle less easily and wake more.
Mistake 3: Changing the Method Every Night
Children need time to recognise a pattern.
Mistake 4: Treating Every Waking as Misbehaviour
Hunger, illness, discomfort and developmental needs should be considered.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Mistake 5: Ignoring Safe Sleep Because the Family Is Exhausted
Exhaustion is exactly when advance safety planning matters most.
Mistake 6: Making Bedtime Too Stimulating
Energetic play, bright lights and screens can make settling harder.
Mistake 7: Believing Comfort Prevents Independence
Children can receive comfort while gradually developing sleep skills.

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Mistake 8: Trying to Fix Naps, Night Feeding, Bedtime and Night Waking Simultaneously
Choose one manageable change and observe its effect.
🚩 Red-Flag Warning
Seek professional guidance if:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: your child regularly snores, gasps or pauses while breathing;
sleep changes suddenly alongside illness, pain or unusual behaviour;
your child is extremely sleepy during normal waking hours;
sleep disruption is affecting feeding, growth or development;

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: a caregiver is so exhausted that safe care is becoming difficult;
someone recommends unsafe sleep products or placing an infant on the stomach to prevent waking;
a programme promises guaranteed results for every baby;
you are advised to ignore signs of illness, hunger or breathing difficulty.

blocks.text: A sleep problem is never more important than a child’s safety.
Your Gentle Sleep Plan
Complete the following:

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Our Usual Morning Wake Time
Our Child’s Current Nap Pattern
Our Bedtime Window
Our Short Bedtime Routine

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: One Sleep Challenge We Want to Address
One Small Change We Will Try Consistently
When We Will Review It

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: ________________________________ One thoughtful change is easier to evaluate than five desperate changes made together.
💛 Remember This

A peaceful bedtime does not require a perfect sleeper. It requires a safe environment, a predictable rhythm and caregivers who can respond with as much calm as the night allows. Some nights will go beautifully. Some will not. A difficult week does not erase the habits you have built. Begin again the following evening.
🌼 Try This Today

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Create a bedtime routine that can be completed in twenty minutes or less. Choose four simple steps, such as:

Use the same short goodnight sentence every evening: “You are safe. It is time to rest. I love you.” Your child may not settle immediately. That is all right. The first purpose of a routine is not instant sleep. It is to make what happens next feel familiar.
Authoritative References

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

items: HealthyChildren.org — Getting Your Baby to Sleep
World Health Organization — Guidelines on Physical Activity, Sedentary Behaviour and Sleep for Children Under Five
NHS — Helping Your Baby to Sleep

pages.chapterTitle: Sleep, Routines and Restful Nights

blocks.text: Editorial note for the Indian edition: Safe-sleep recommendations should be reviewed against the latest guidance from the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Indian Academy of Pediatrics and other recognised paediatric authorities before publication. Children born prematurely or living with respiratory, neurological, feeding or developmental conditions may require individual advice. This chapter provides general education and does not replace medical assessment.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection
pages.epigraph: “Your child’s first classroom is not a room. It is every ordinary moment in which someone notices, responds and plays along.”

blocks.text: When Rhea’s daughter was ten months old, she worried that she was not doing enough. Another mother had enrolled her baby in a stimulation programme. Someone online displayed shelves filled with carefully arranged educational toys. A parenting video claimed that a particular set of cards could build a baby’s intelligence. Rhea looked around her home. Her daughter’s favourite objects were a steel bowl, a wooden spoon and an old scarf. She enjoyed removing clothes from a basket, putting them back and removing them again. She laughed when Rhea hid behind the curtain. She could spend several minutes transferring a lemon from one hand to the other while her expensive toys remained untouched.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Rhea wondered whether this counted as learning. It did. The steel bowl taught sound and cause-and-effect. The scarf introduced texture and hiding. The laundry basket created opportunities for movement, problem-solving and imitation. Peekaboo developed anticipation, memory and social connection. Babies and toddlers do not divide their days into subjects. They learn language, movement, relationships, problem-solving and emotional regulation together—through real experiences. The most important developmental tool in your home is not a product.
It is responsive interaction with another human being.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Play Is the Work of Childhood
To an adult, dropping a spoon repeatedly may look pointless. To a baby, it is an experiment.

items: What happens when I let go?
Can I make that happen again?

blocks.text: A toddler filling and emptying a container is learning about:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Play gives children a safe way to investigate the world. Through play, children practise:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: UNICEF describes play as a central way babies explore, practise skills and bond with caregivers. UNICEF: How Babies Learn Through Play
The Most Powerful Pattern: Notice and Respond

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Imagine your baby looks at the ceiling fan and makes a sound. You follow their gaze and say: “Yes, the fan is turning.” The baby looks back at you. You smile and pause. The baby moves an arm and makes another sound. You reply: “Round and round.” This simple exchange is an early conversation. The child expresses interest. You notice. You respond. The child responds again. These back-and-forth interactions are sometimes called serve-and-return exchanges. They help a baby learn:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: that sounds and gestures receive responses;
that another person can share attention with them.

blocks.text: You do not need to talk continuously. The pause is important. It gives the baby an opportunity to answer with:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: UNICEF India recommends talking, smiling and responding to babies’ sounds and gestures as part of everyday early learning. UNICEF India: Play, Love, Learn
Communication Begins Before Words
A baby communicates long before saying “Mama.” Early communication includes:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: looking between an object and a person.

blocks.text: These actions are not merely a warm-up for speech. They are communication. When you respond, you help the child understand: “My actions can send a message.”

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: A Communication Progression

rows: What the child may be learning

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

rows: What the child may be learning
Another person can share my attention
I can show and share an interest

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

rows: What the child may be learning
A sound can represent a person, object or idea
Words can be joined to create a more specific message

blocks.text: Development does not always move in a perfectly tidy line. A toddler may rely heavily on gestures while words are emerging. Another child may use many words but still need help with social communication. Look at the whole pattern.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: How to Talk to a Baby
Talking to a child does not require a prepared lesson. Describe real life.
During Dressing
“Here is your blue shirt.” “One arm in.” “Now the other arm.”
During Meals
“The banana is soft.” “You want more?” “All finished.”
During a Walk
“I hear a dog.” “That bus is very loud.” “The leaves are moving.”

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: During a Nappy Change

“Let’s clean your feet.” “You are kicking.” “Now we are finished.” Use short, clear and natural sentences. Repeat useful words. Allow your face and voice to show interest. You do not have to provide a constant stream of narration. Follow the child’s attention rather than naming everything in the room.
Baby Talk Is Not Meaningless Talk

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Adults often naturally use a warmer, slower and more melodic voice with babies. This style can help capture a baby’s attention. Helpful baby-directed speech usually involves:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: You can say: “Look at the little dog! The dog is running.” You do not need to deliberately replace every word with nonsense. As the child grows, expand the language naturally. Child: “Dog.” Adult: “Yes, a brown dog.” Child: “Dog run.” Adult: “The dog is running fast.” This adds information without correcting the child harshly.
Reading Before a Child Can Read
A baby does not need to understand the story for reading to be useful. Reading together introduces:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: the sound and rhythm of language;
a calm routine with a caregiver.

blocks.text: Begin with:

items: stories and songs from your own language and culture.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: How to Read with a Baby
You can:

items: let the baby touch the page;
follow what attracts the baby’s attention;
stop when the baby is finished.

blocks.text: How to Read with a Toddler
Try:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Do not turn every page into a test. Sometimes simply enjoying the story together is enough. Ten relaxed minutes can be more meaningful than forcing a restless child to sit through an entire book. UNICEF: Why Reading to Your Baby Matters
Singing, Rhythm and Rhyme
Songs help children notice:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Use:

items: rhymes in any language spoken at home.

blocks.text: Pause before a familiar word or movement. For example: “Twinkle, twinkle, little…” Wait. The child may smile, move, vocalise or eventually supply the missing word. That pause turns a performance into participation.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Movement Is Learning
Babies learn by moving through space. Movement helps them understand:

items: how much effort a movement requires.

blocks.text: Provide safe opportunities for:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: crawling or other forms of movement;

blocks.text: Avoid keeping an awake baby restrained for long periods in:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Equipment may be useful for short periods, but it cannot replace free movement on a safe surface. WHO recommends frequent physical activity through the day, especially interactive floor-based play for infants. WHO: Movement and Sleep Guidelines for Children Under Five
Play Ideas by Developmental Stage

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Age suggestions are approximate. Choose activities that match your child’s current abilities rather than forcing a skill.
Around 6–9 Months

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection
Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Around 9–12 Months

rows: Hiding a toy partly under a cloth

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

rows: Water play with cups under supervision

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Around 12–18 Months

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

rows: Matching an object to its use

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Around 18–24 Months

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection
Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: The same activity can support several skills. A child washing vegetables with you may be learning language, sensory differences, imitation, coordination and family participation at once.
Household Objects Can Be Excellent Toys

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: A toy does not need lights, batteries or an educational label to support learning. Useful household play materials include:

items: paper and thick crayons for older toddlers.

blocks.text: Check every object for:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Everyday objects still require supervision.
Choosing Toys Wisely
Before buying a toy, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: Can the child do something with it, or does it do everything by itself?
Can it be used in more than one way?
Does it match the child’s present abilities?
Are there small parts, button batteries or magnets?
Will it encourage interaction, movement or imagination?
Is it likely to remain useful for more than a few weeks?

blocks.text: An open-ended toy can be used in many ways. Examples include:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: A toy that flashes and plays continuously may hold attention, but it can also leave the child with little to initiate. The best toys invite the child to act.
Do Babies Need Classes?
Music, movement or parent-child groups can be enjoyable. They may offer:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: a reason to leave the house;

blocks.text: But classes are not a requirement for healthy development. A child does not fall behind because the family cannot afford a baby programme. Daily interaction, safe movement, conversation, books, songs and ordinary play provide rich learning. Choose a class because it suits your family—not because marketing has made you afraid.
Independent Play

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Children benefit from interaction with adults, but they do not need an adult to entertain them every waking minute. Short periods of independent play can help a child practise:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Begin nearby. Allow the child to explore while you remain available. Do not interrupt immediately if the child is safely concentrating. A baby turning a cup repeatedly may be doing important work. Independent play is not emotional neglect. It becomes valuable when it exists alongside warm and responsive connection.
Playing Without Taking Over

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Adults naturally want to show the correct way. But when a child is exploring, try to resist solving every problem immediately. If a block does not fit, pause. If the ball rolls under a chair, wait. If a container will not open, observe. Offer just enough help to keep the child engaged. You might say: “That lid is difficult.” “Shall we turn it?” “You found another way.” The aim is not to create frustration. It is to leave space for discovery.
Talking in More Than One Language

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Many Indian children grow up hearing two, three or more languages. A child may hear:

items: a regional language in the community;

blocks.text: Exposure to more than one language does not itself cause a communication disorder. Words may be distributed across languages. For example, a child might know:

items: a family word in Punjabi, Tamil, Bengali, Marathi or another language.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Count communication across all languages rather than judging the child by only one. Parents should generally speak the language in which they can communicate most naturally, warmly and richly. Do not abandon a home language merely because someone claims it will confuse the child. At the same time, bilingualism should not be used to dismiss a genuine delay. A child with difficulty communicating across all languages may still need assessment. HealthyChildren.org: Children Learning Multiple Languages
Screens Are Not the Same as Interaction

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: A baby may stare at bright colours and movement on a screen. That does not mean the baby is learning from it in the same way they learn from a person. Real-world interaction allows a child to:

items: receive a response tailored to their expression;
connect a word with a real object.

blocks.text: A prerecorded character cannot notice that your child has pointed towards the window and change the conversation accordingly.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Practical Screen Guidance

WHO guidance recommends no sedentary screen time for infants and one-year-olds. For children aged two years, no more than one hour is advised, and less is better. WHO: Children Need to Sit Less and Play More For very young children:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: do not use a screen as the usual feeding or calming method;
protect meals, play and bedtime from screens;
keep video calls interactive with an adult present;
if older toddlers view media, choose high-quality content and watch together;
connect what appears on-screen with real life.

blocks.text: A video call with a responsive grandparent is different from leaving a child alone with a stream of videos.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: The Adult’s Phone Matters Too
Sometimes the greatest screen distraction in the room belongs to the parent. No parent can remain fully attentive all day. You may need your phone for:
The goal is not guilt. It is awareness. Try creating small phone-free moments:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: for ten minutes of floor play;
when the child is trying to show you something.

blocks.text: A child does not need constant attention. But they do need regular moments in which your attention is truly available.
When a Child Does Not Play “Typically”
Some children:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: become overwhelmed by particular sounds or textures;
show intense interest in a particular object;
need more support with imitation or pretend play;
have physical, sensory or developmental differences.

blocks.text: Do not force the child to play exactly as another child does. Observe:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: whether play skills are expanding over time.

blocks.text: If the pattern concerns you, discuss it with a developmental professional. Support should build on the child’s strengths rather than trying to erase their individuality.
When Communication Deserves Assessment
Speak with your child’s healthcare professional if you notice:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: limited response to sounds or voices;
inconsistent response to the child’s name;
limited use of gestures such as showing, reaching or pointing;
difficulty understanding familiar words or simple directions;
very limited babbling, sounds or words for the child’s stage;
communication frustration that is becoming severe;
loss of words, gestures or social skills previously used;

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: communication difficulties across all languages used by the child.

blocks.text: A hearing assessment is often an important part of evaluating communication. Do not wait for a child to begin school if you are already concerned. Developmental evaluation does not force a diagnosis. It helps clarify whether the child needs support. HealthyChildren.org: Language Delays in Toddlers
Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Believing Expensive Toys Create Intelligence

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Children learn through active exploration and responsive relationships, not price tags.
Mistake 2: Quizzing Instead of Conversing
“What is this?” has a place, but comments and shared observations create richer interaction.
Mistake 3: Correcting Every Word
Model the correct form naturally without making the child feel unsuccessful. Child: “Car go.” Adult: “Yes, the car is going.”
Mistake 4: Talking Without Pausing

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Children need time to respond.
Mistake 5: Entertaining Constantly
Safe independent exploration has value.
Mistake 6: Using Screens as the Main Teacher
Babies and toddlers learn best from responsive, real-world experiences.
Mistake 7: Assuming Multiple Languages Cause Delay
Evaluate communication across every language.
Mistake 8: Waiting Indefinitely After Skills Are Lost

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Regression deserves prompt professional attention.
🚩 Red-Flag Warning
Seek professional advice if:

items: your child stops using words, gestures, play or social skills they previously used;
there is little response to sound or concern about hearing;
the child rarely shares attention or interest with caregivers;

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: movement, vision or sensory difficulties limit play;
frustration around communication is affecting daily life;
someone recommends removing a familiar home language as the only response to a delay;
a programme promises to make a baby exceptionally intelligent through flashcards, videos or expensive products;
a child is being pushed through repetitive drills that cause persistent distress.

blocks.text: Early support should increase a child’s ability to communicate and participate—not make the child feel constantly tested.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Your Everyday Learning Plan
Choose one small opportunity from each area.
Talk
During ____________________, I will describe what we are doing.
Read
We will look at a book or family photographs for ______ minutes.
Move
I will create a safe space for ____________________.
Play
We will explore ____________________ together.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Pause
When my child communicates, I will wait for a response before continuing.
Protect
One part of our day that will remain screen-free is ____________________. This is not a school timetable. It is simply a reminder that learning already fits inside family life.
A Fifteen-Minute Connection Routine
If your day feels too full, try this:
Five Minutes: Follow

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Sit near your child and observe what interests them. Do not direct immediately.
Five Minutes: Join
Copy the child’s sound or action. Describe what they are doing. Take turns.
Five Minutes: Expand
Add one small possibility:

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Then stop before the activity becomes forced. Fifteen attentive minutes can be more valuable than an hour spent beside a child while your attention is elsewhere.
💛 Remember This

Children do not need a perfect teacher. They need people who notice what interests them, respond to their communication and make room for discovery. You are not required to fill every minute. You are not required to buy every toy. You are not required to turn your home into a classroom. Talk. Listen. Read. Move. Play. Pause. Then do it again tomorrow.

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: 🌼 Try This Today
Choose one familiar household object that is safe for your child’s age. It might be:
Place it near your child and observe. Ask yourself:

items: What does my child do first?
What sound, word or gesture could I add?
Can I join without taking over?

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Write down one thing your child discovered: ________________________________ That small moment is early learning in its most natural form.
Authoritative References

items: World Health Organization — Nurturing Care Handbook
World Health Organization — Guidelines on Physical Activity, Sedentary Behaviour and Sleep for Children Under Five
UNICEF — How Babies Learn Through Play

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

items: UNICEF — Learning Activities for Babies and Toddlers

pages.chapterTitle: Learning Through Play, Talk and Connection

blocks.text: Editorial note for the Indian edition: Developmental and media guidance should be checked against the latest recommendations of the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Indian Academy of Pediatrics and recognised child-development authorities before publication. Children with hearing, vision, motor, sensory or developmental differences may benefit from adapted play and individual professional guidance. This chapter provides general education and does not replace developmental assessment.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries
pages.epigraph: “A toddler can need comfort and a boundary at the same time.”

blocks.text: The first time Meera’s eighteen-month-old threw himself onto the supermarket floor, she froze. He had wanted to carry a glass bottle. She had said no. Within seconds, he was crying, kicking and reaching towards the shelf while several people turned to look. One person smiled sympathetically. Another said, “He knows exactly how to control you.” Someone else advised, “Give him what he wants before he makes a bigger scene.” Meera felt embarrassed, angry and unsure. Was her child deliberately misbehaving? Should she comfort him?

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Would comforting him reward the tantrum? Should she become stricter? She moved the shopping basket aside, sat near him and said: “You really wanted that bottle. I will not let you hold it because it can break. I’m here when you’re ready.” He did not stop crying immediately. The boundary had not changed. The bottle remained on the shelf. But the child was not left alone with a feeling he could not yet manage. That is one of the central tasks of early parenting: Keeping the limit while helping the child through the feeling.
Connection does not require saying yes. A boundary does not require withdrawing love.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: What Is Happening Inside a Toddler?
During the second year, children become:

items: more aware of their own wishes;
more interested in doing things independently;
better at remembering what they want;
more frustrated when they cannot communicate or complete a task.

blocks.text: Their desires are growing faster than their ability to:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: This creates a predictable gap. The toddler thinks: “I want it.” The adult says: “You cannot have it.” The child does not yet have the mature skills to think: “I am disappointed, but I understand the safety issue and can select another activity.” Instead, the disappointment may appear as:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: This behaviour needs guidance. But it also needs to be understood developmentally. Toddlers are not small adults choosing to behave irrationally. They are young children with strong feelings and limited regulation.
Behaviour Is Communication—but Not Permission

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: When we say behaviour is communication, we do not mean that every behaviour should be allowed. We mean that behaviour gives us information.

rows: “I am finished,” “I am experimenting,” or “I want a reaction.”
“I want that toy,” “I am overwhelmed,” or “I cannot stop my body yet.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

rows: “I do not understand why the fun must end.”
“I want control,” “The texture bothers me,” or “I am tired.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Understanding the possible message helps you choose the response. It does not mean permitting unsafe behaviour. You can say: “I see that you are angry. I will not let you hit.” Both parts matter.
Co-Regulation Comes Before Self-Regulation

Self-regulation means managing emotions, attention and behaviour. Young children are only beginning to develop this ability. Before children can calm themselves reliably, they often need co-regulation —support from a calmer adult. Co-regulation may look like:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: helping the child name the feeling;
guiding the child towards a safer action.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: The adult’s calm does not magically stop every tantrum. It provides the child with a model and a safe structure while the emotional storm passes. WHO describes responsive caregiving as noticing, understanding and responding appropriately to a child’s signals. This helps build trust and social relationships. WHO: Nurturing Care Practice Guide
Attachment Does Not Mean Constant Happiness
A securely connected child still:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Attachment is not demonstrated by a child who never becomes upset. It grows through repeated experiences such as:

items: freedom to explore from a safe base;
reassurance when the world feels overwhelming.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: You will not respond perfectly every time. No parent does. What matters is the overall pattern—and your willingness to reconnect when things go wrong.
Separation Anxiety

Separation anxiety is common during infancy and toddlerhood. It often becomes noticeable when a child understands that a caregiver can leave but does not yet fully understand when that person will return. The child may:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: follow you from room to room;

blocks.text: Separation anxiety is particularly common between approximately six months and three years. UNICEF: Managing Separation Anxiety
Helping with Separation
Try to:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: practise short separations with a trusted caregiver;
say when you will return in language the child understands;
allow a familiar comfort object when appropriate;

blocks.text: For a toddler, “after lunch” may be more meaningful than “at two o’clock.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: A Simple Goodbye

“Mama is going to work. Nani will stay with you. You will eat and play. I will come back after your nap.” The child may still cry. The goal of a goodbye ritual is not to prevent every tear. It is to build trust that departures have an explanation and caregivers return.
Preparing for Childcare
Before childcare begins:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: Visit the setting together if possible.
Let the child explore while you remain nearby.
Share feeding, sleep and comfort routines.
Explain words or gestures the child uses.
Tell the caregiver about allergies and medical needs.
Send an appropriate familiar object if permitted.
begin with shorter separations where practical.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: Avoid promising that the child will not cry.

blocks.text: Ask the childcare provider:

items: How do you comfort a distressed child?
How will you contact me if my child does not settle?
What is your approach to biting and hitting?
How are food, sleep and medicines managed?
What child-protection procedures do you follow?

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: A child’s distress at drop-off does not always mean the setting is wrong. But persistent extreme distress, unexplained injuries, fear of a particular person or concerning changes in behaviour deserve careful attention.
Why Tantrums Happen
Common tantrum triggers include:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Sometimes the best tantrum strategy begins before the tantrum.
Prevention May Include

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: offering food before the child becomes extremely hungry;
carrying a simple snack or familiar object;
childproofing so fewer safety-related prohibitions are needed.

blocks.text: Prevention does not eliminate tantrums. It reduces avoidable ones.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: What to Do During a Tantrum
Step 1: Make the Situation Safe
Move dangerous objects. Block hitting or kicking. Move away from traffic, stairs, water or crowded hazards. Say: “I will keep you safe.”
Step 2: Regulate Yourself
Lower your shoulders. Take one breath. Use fewer words. A louder adult usually creates a louder situation.
Step 3: Name the Feeling and Limit

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: “You are angry because playtime is finished.” “The answer is still no.” “I will not let you hit.”
Step 4: Offer Appropriate Comfort
Depending on the child, you may:
Some children want touch. Others need space. Do not force physical affection on a child who is pulling away.
Step 5: Wait

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: A toddler in intense distress may not be able to process explanations. This is not the moment for a lecture.
Step 6: Reconnect
When the child is calmer:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: “You were very angry. Hitting hurts. Next time, you can say ‘help’ or stamp your feet here.” Do not require a long apology from a child who does not yet understand it. Teach repair through action: “Let’s bring your sister the toy.” “Let’s check whether she is all right.”
Should a Tantrum Be Ignored?
Some attention-seeking behaviours may decrease when they stop receiving a large reaction. But ignoring behaviour should not mean abandoning a distressed young child. You must never ignore:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: For a safe protest tantrum, you may reduce attention to the performance while remaining available. For example:

items: keep your face and voice neutral;
respond warmly when the child begins to calm.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: The distinction is: Do not reward the demand. Do not withdraw safety or care.
Hitting
Toddlers may hit because they:
Respond immediately and calmly.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: Move the children apart if needed.
Attend to the person who was hurt.

blocks.text: “I will not let you hit. Hitting hurts. You can say ‘mine’ or ask for help.” Avoid hitting the child to teach them not to hit. That sends a confusing message: “Hitting is wrong when you do it, but acceptable when a larger person does it.”
Biting
Biting is upsetting, but it is not unusual in young children. Possible reasons include:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: When Biting Happens

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries
Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Say: “Biting hurts. I will not let you bite.” Then offer an alternative: “Bite this teether.” “Say ‘move.’” “Come and ask me for help.” If skin is broken, wash the area and seek medical advice as appropriate. Persistent or severe biting may need discussion with the child’s paediatrician or developmental professional.
Throwing

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Throwing is both a developmental experiment and sometimes an expression of frustration. Create places where throwing is allowed. “You may throw this soft ball into the basket.” When the child throws an unsafe object: “Blocks are for building. Balls are for throwing.” If throwing continues: “I’m moving the blocks until your body is ready to build safely.” The consequence is immediate, related and understandable. It is not revenge.
The Power of Clear Directions
Toddlers understand better when directions are:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries
Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Instead of: “Stop behaving badly.” Say: “Feet stay on the floor.” Instead of: “Don’t make a mess.” Say: “Food stays on the plate.” Instead of: “Will you please stop running away?” Say: “Hold my hand.” Instead of: “Be careful.” Say: “Sit while you eat.” Tell the child what to do—not only what to stop doing. CDC guidance on giving directions emphasises gaining the child’s attention, remaining close and stating the expected behaviour clearly. CDC: Giving Good Directions
Offer Choices—but Only Real Ones

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Choices give toddlers a healthy experience of control. Useful choices include:

items: “Walk to the bathroom or shall I carry you?”

blocks.text: Avoid offering a choice when there is none. “Would you like to leave the park?” is not a genuine question if you must leave. Instead say: “It is time to leave. Would you like to walk or hold my hand?” Keep choices limited. Too many options can overwhelm a young child.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: What a Boundary Sounds Like
A boundary is clear information about what will happen. Examples:

items: “I will not let you touch the stove.”
“We are leaving after one more turn.”
“I will help your body move away.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: A boundary becomes confusing if it changes after prolonged screaming. You can comfort the child without changing the answer. “You are disappointed. I know. The answer is still no.”
Natural and Related Consequences
A useful consequence is connected to the behaviour.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

rows: Throws a hard toy at someone
The toy is removed for now
Spills water deliberately after being asked to stop
The child helps wipe it with assistance
Play pauses while everyone becomes safe

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

rows: Crayons are put away and the child helps clean
Runs away in a car park
The child must hold an adult’s hand or be carried

blocks.text: Avoid unrelated punishments such as: “You threw your spoon, so no bedtime story.” The child is unlikely to understand the connection. Consequences should be:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Afterwards, return to warm interaction. The relationship does not need to remain cold to prove that the limit mattered.
Notice the Behaviour You Want More Often
Children receive a great deal of attention for difficult behaviour. Sometimes their cooperation goes unnoticed. Instead of only saying “Good girl” or “Good boy,” describe what the child did.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: “You put the cup on the table.”
“You held my hand near the road.”
“You were angry and you kept your hands safe.”

blocks.text: Specific acknowledgement teaches the child which action was helpful. Do not praise every movement constantly. A warm smile or simple “You did it” is often enough.
Naming Feelings
Toddlers benefit from simple emotional language. Begin with:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries
Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: You might say: “You are sad that Papa left.” “That loud sound frightened you.” “You are frustrated because the lid will not open.” Naming a feeling does not increase it. It gives the child a word that may eventually replace part of the behaviour. Do not insist: “You are not scared.” Try: “You feel scared. I’m here.” The aim is not to convince children that their feelings are wrong. It is to help them move through those feelings safely.
Feelings Are Allowed; Some Actions Are Not
This distinction becomes the foundation of respectful discipline.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: Anger is allowed. Hitting is not.
Sadness is allowed. Hurting yourself is not.
Frustration is allowed. Throwing a glass is not.
Disappointment is allowed. Running into the road is not.
Jealousy is allowed. Biting the baby is not.

blocks.text: When adults separate feelings from actions, children learn: “My inner experience is not dangerous, but I am responsible for how I act.” That lesson develops slowly. It must be repeated many times.
What If the Parent Loses Control?

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: You may shout. You may speak more harshly than you intended. You may handle a moment poorly. Repair matters. Once everyone is safe and calm, say: “I shouted. That was frightening. I’m sorry. I should have spoken more calmly. The rule about hitting is still important, and I will practise saying it without shouting.” An apology does not weaken parental authority. It demonstrates responsibility. Do not make the child comfort you or say: “You made me shout.” Own your action. Then decide what support might help you respond differently next time.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Physical Punishment and Humiliation

Hitting, slapping, shaking, threatening and humiliating do not teach emotional regulation. They may stop behaviour in the moment because the child is frightened. But fear is not the same as understanding. Avoid:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: locking a child alone in a frightening space;
calling the child bad, stupid or shameful;

blocks.text: A firm boundary does not require violence. WHO-supported parenting programmes promote nurturing relationships and alternatives to violent discipline. WHO: Parenting for Lifelong Health

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Consistency Between Caregivers
Perfect agreement is unrealistic. But caregivers should try to agree on essential limits, such as:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: A child becomes confused when one adult says: “We do not hit,” while another adult hits the child for hitting. Discuss the plan privately, not during the tantrum. Choose simple phrases everyone can use:

items: “You may be angry. The limit remains.”

blocks.text: When Behaviour May Signal More Than a Stage
Challenging behaviour can increase because of:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Ask what changed before assuming that the child simply needs stricter discipline. A behaviour record may help.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

rows: Child was hungry and shop was crowded

blocks.text: Patterns guide prevention.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: When to Seek Professional Support
Discuss behaviour with a qualified professional if:

items: the child frequently injures themselves or others;
tantrums are unusually prolonged or occur many times every day;

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: the child loses language, play or social skills;
the child does not respond to sound or social interaction as expected;
sleep, feeding or pain may be contributing;
you are afraid you may hurt the child;

blocks.text: Seeking help does not mean you have failed. It means the family needs more information and support.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Calling a Toddler Manipulative
Young children can learn which behaviours produce results, but they do not possess adult emotional control or reasoning.
Mistake 2: Giving In After a Long Protest
This can teach the child that the boundary changes if the protest becomes intense enough.
Mistake 3: Explaining Too Much During the Tantrum

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Use fewer words until the child is calm.
Mistake 4: Punishing the Feeling
Correct unsafe behaviour without shaming anger, sadness or fear.
Mistake 5: Giving Directions from Across the Room
Move closer, gain attention and use one clear instruction.
Mistake 6: Threatening Consequences You Will Not Use
Make limits realistic and follow through calmly.
Mistake 7: Forcing an Apology

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Model repair and help the child take an appropriate action.
Mistake 8: Expecting Perfect Consistency from the Child
Learning self-control requires repetition and maturity.
🚩 Red-Flag Warning
Seek help promptly if:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: behaviour changes suddenly alongside fear, withdrawal or sleep disturbance;
you suspect pain, hearing difficulty or another medical issue;
your child appears extremely fearful of a particular person or place;
a caregiver uses hitting, shaking, humiliation or threats;
you feel close to losing physical control.

blocks.text: If anger is rising dangerously:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: Make sure the child is in a safe place.
Step away briefly if another responsible adult is available.
Do not shake or strike the child.
Seek urgent support if you fear someone may be harmed.

blocks.text: Your Calm Boundary Formula
Use four short parts:
1. Notice
“You wanted the toy.”
2. Name
“You are angry.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: 3. Limit
“I will not let you hit.”
4. Guide
“You can stamp here or ask me for help.” The formula will not end every tantrum. It gives you a steady place to begin.
A Family Boundary Plan
Choose three important boundaries.
Boundary 1

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Boundary 2
Boundary 3

We will not allow: ________________________________ We will say: _____________________________________ We will help the child by: _________________________ Discuss these with every regular caregiver. A simple shared response is more useful than a complicated theory no one follows.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: 💛 Remember This

Your child is allowed to dislike a boundary. Your task is not to prevent every upset—it is to keep the limit safely and remain emotionally available. You can say no with kindness. You can comfort without surrendering the boundary. You can repair after losing patience. And you can guide behaviour without making the child feel that love has been withdrawn.
🌼 Try This Today

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Choose one instruction you repeat often. Perhaps: “Don’t run.” “Stop throwing.” “Don’t touch.” Now rewrite it as a short direction describing what the child should do. For example: “Walk beside me.” “Place the cup on the table.” “Hands stay away from the stove.” Write yours here: Instead of: ________________________________ I will say: ________________________________ A small change in language can make a boundary much easier for a young child to understand.
Authoritative References

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

items: World Health Organization — Nurturing Care Practice Guide
World Health Organization — Parenting for Lifelong Health
UNICEF — Supporting Positive Mental Health from Birth to Five
CDC — Positive Parenting Tips for Toddlers
CDC — Essentials for Parenting Toddlers and Preschoolers

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings, Early Boundaries

blocks.text: Editorial note for the Indian edition: Behavioural and developmental guidance should be reviewed against current recommendations from the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Indian Academy of Pediatrics and recognised child-development authorities before publication. Persistent aggression, regression, communication concerns or significant family distress warrants individual assessment. This chapter provides general education and does not replace medical, developmental or mental-health care.
PART IV
Health, Safety and Shared Care
Practical guidance, thoughtful reassurance and clear next steps.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child
pages.epigraph: “Safety is not about preventing exploration. It is about removing dangers a young child cannot yet understand.”

blocks.text: One afternoon, Nisha placed her eight-month-old son on the living-room mat and went to answer the door. She was gone for less than a minute. When she returned, he was no longer on the mat. He had crawled towards a low table and was trying to place a coin in his mouth. Until that day, he had never crawled more than a few centimetres. The coin had been left there because no one believed he could reach it. That is how many childhood injuries happen. Not because a family does not care. Because the child develops a new ability before the environment has been prepared for it.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: A baby who cannot roll today may roll tomorrow. A child who has never climbed may discover the skill while you are making tea. A toddler who has always remained beside you may suddenly run towards the road. Young children are curious, fast and unable to understand many consequences. Safety therefore depends on three things:
“Be careful” is not enough for a child who cannot yet recognise danger.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Babyproofing Is a Continuing Process
Babyproofing is not one afternoon spent covering electrical sockets. It changes as your child develops.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

rows: Falls from beds, sofas and changing surfaces
Hot drinks, tablecloths, cords and nearby objects
Small objects, sockets, stairs, floor-level chemicals

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

rows: Unstable furniture, televisions and accessible drawers
Doors, balconies, water, roads and sharp corners
Windows, shelves, gates, cupboards and furniture

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Review the home whenever your child gains a new ability. Get down to the child’s level and look around. Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: Which doors can now be opened?

blocks.text: UNICEF recommends managing the environment rather than relying on supervision alone. UNICEF: How to Babyproof Your Home
Supervision Has Different Levels
Not every activity needs the same supervision.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Active Supervision
You are:
This is essential around:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Nearby Supervision
You remain in the same safe area while the child explores independently. This may be suitable for:

items: floor play in a childproofed room;

blocks.text: Listening Is Not Enough Near Serious Hazards

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Drowning can be silent. A child can swallow a small object without calling out. A hot drink can be pulled down in a second. Near high-risk hazards, the adult must be close enough to intervene physically.
Preventing Falls
Falls are common while babies and toddlers learn to move. Many result in minor bumps, but some cause serious head or bone injuries.
Beds, Sofas and Changing Surfaces
Never leave a baby unattended on:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: A baby can roll for the first time without warning. If you must turn away, place the child in a safe cot, play space or on the floor.
A Bed Is Not a Safe Playpen
Pillows placed around a baby do not reliably prevent falling. They may also create a suffocation hazard for an infant.
Stairs
Where feasible:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: place gates at both the top and bottom;
avoid pressure-mounted gates at the top of stairs if they are unsuitable for that location;
keep stairs well lit and free from clutter.

blocks.text: Teach stair skills gradually under direct supervision.
Windows and Balconies

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: keep furniture away from windows and balcony railings;
never rely on an insect screen to prevent a fall;
keep balcony doors locked when not actively supervised;
do not allow a child to climb on balcony furniture.

blocks.text: Furniture and Televisions
Anchor heavy or tall items where appropriate, including:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: A toddler may pull out drawers and use them like steps. Keep heavier objects on lower shelves and prevent children from climbing furniture.
When a Fall Needs Urgent Attention
Seek urgent medical care after a fall if the child:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: becomes unusually sleepy or difficult to wake;
develops fluid or blood from the nose or ears;
has a deep wound or uncontrolled bleeding;
has a limb that appears deformed;
is an infant and you are worried about the impact.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Do not shake a child to check whether they are awake. If a neck or spinal injury is possible, avoid unnecessary movement and call emergency services.
Preventing Burns and Scalds
