Young children have thinner skin than adults and can burn quickly. Common sources include:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: In the Kitchen

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: turn pot handles away from the edge;
keep hot liquids away from table edges;
avoid holding a child while cooking or carrying a hot drink;
keep appliance cords out of reach;
create a child-free area near the stove;
do not place hot vessels on the floor;
keep pressure cookers and hot oil inaccessible;
store matches and lighters in a locked or high cupboard.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Be especially cautious during festivals and ceremonies involving flames, lamps or fireworks.
Bath Water

Check the temperature before placing the child in the water. Mix the water thoroughly and test it with your hand or an appropriate thermometer. Never add very hot water while the child is already sitting in the bath.
If a Burn Occurs

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: Move the child away from the heat source.
Cool the burn under cool running water for approximately 20 minutes.
Remove nearby clothing or jewellery unless stuck to the skin.
Keep the rest of the child warm.
Cover the area loosely with a clean, non-fluffy material.

blocks.text: Do not apply:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Do not burst blisters. Burns that are deep, extensive, electrical, chemical or located on the face, hands, genitals or major joints require urgent medical assessment. UNICEF: Burns and Scalds First Aid
Preventing Poisoning

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Children investigate with their mouths. A brightly coloured cleaning liquid may resemble a drink. Tablets can look like sweets. Common poisoning risks include:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child
Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Store Dangerous Products Safely

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: keep medicines and chemicals locked away;
never transfer chemicals into water or soft-drink bottles;
keep handbags belonging to visitors out of reach;

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Child-resistant packaging is helpful, but it is not childproof.
If Poisoning Is Suspected

items: remove the product from the child;
call emergency services or an appropriate poison-information service;
explain what was taken, how much and when;

blocks.text: Do not:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: give milk, salt water, lemon or another home remedy unless instructed;
wait for symptoms before asking for help;
perform mouth-to-mouth rescue breathing if the mouth is contaminated with a dangerous chemical without professional guidance and appropriate protection.

blocks.text: If the child is unconscious, having a seizure, struggling to breathe or collapsing, call emergency services immediately.
Batteries and Magnets

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Button batteries and strong magnets deserve special attention. They may be found in:

A swallowed button battery can cause severe internal injury quickly. Multiple magnets, or a magnet swallowed with another metal object, can attract across the bowel and cause serious damage. If you think a child has swallowed a button battery or magnet:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: bring the product packaging if available.

blocks.text: Secure battery compartments and regularly inspect devices.
Choking, Suffocation and Strangulation
Young children place objects in their mouths and may not chew safely.
Common Choking Hazards

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Food risk depends on its shape, hardness and preparation. Supervise meals and have children sit upright while eating.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Check Toys
Inspect toys for:
Older siblings’ toys can be especially hazardous to a crawling baby.
Strangulation and Suffocation Risks
Keep away from young children:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: pillows and loose blankets in an infant sleep space.

blocks.text: Move cots and furniture away from window cords. Follow safe-sleep practices during infancy.
Learn Choking First Aid

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Every regular caregiver should complete a practical infant and child first-aid course. A written chapter cannot provide the physical practice needed to respond confidently. UNICEF advises families to learn basic first aid and recognise that a choking child may be unable to cry, cough, breathe or make noise. UNICEF: Preventing Choking
Water Safety
A young child can drown quickly and silently in a very small amount of water. Risks include:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: During Bathing
Before placing the child in water:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: collect the soap, towel and clothes;
keep the child within arm’s reach;
ignore the door and phone if answering would require leaving;
take the child with you if you must move away.

blocks.text: A bath seat does not replace supervision. An older sibling should not be made solely responsible for supervising a baby in water.
Around the Home

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: empty buckets and tubs immediately after use;
cover wells and water tanks securely;
use barriers around pools and ponds;

blocks.text: Swimming Lessons Are Not Enough

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Water skills can be helpful when developmentally appropriate, but no young child becomes drown-proof. Supervision and barriers remain essential. WHO reports that children under five account for a substantial share of global drowning deaths. WHO: Drowning
Road and Vehicle Safety
Road safety begins before the journey.
In a Car
Use an age- and size-appropriate approved child restraint according to its instructions and current local law. Important principles include:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: place the child in the restraint for every journey;
ensure straps are correctly positioned and snug;
never hold a child on an adult’s lap as a substitute;
do not share one seat belt between an adult and child;
replace a restraint after a serious crash as advised by the manufacturer;
avoid bulky clothing that prevents the harness from fitting properly;
never leave a child alone in a parked vehicle.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: A child can become dangerously overheated inside a vehicle even when the weather does not feel extreme. Check the back seat every time before locking the car.
On Two-Wheelers
Two-wheelers expose young children to significant risk. Follow current Indian law regarding:
Never balance a sleeping infant between adults or carry a baby in one arm while riding. Where safer transport is available, use it.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Near Roads
Toddlers cannot judge speed or traffic distance.

items: use a stroller or safe carrier where appropriate;
keep the child on the side away from traffic;
never assume a toddler will remain beside you.

blocks.text: Electrical Safety

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: prevent children from inserting objects into sockets;
unplug appliances when not in use;
keep electrical devices away from water;
use appropriate socket protection compatible with your electrical system.

blocks.text: Do not overload sockets or use visibly damaged adapters. If a child receives an electrical shock:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: do not touch the child while they remain in contact with live electricity;
seek medical evaluation even if the child initially appears well.

blocks.text: Sharp Objects and Glass
Keep out of reach:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Place glass furniture and mirrors where they cannot fall or shatter onto the child. Use safety film or barriers where appropriate. If a wound is bleeding heavily:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: apply firm, direct pressure with a clean cloth;
do not remove a deeply embedded object yourself.

blocks.text: Animals
Even a familiar animal can react unpredictably. Teach children to avoid:

items: disturbing an animal while eating or sleeping;
placing their face close to an animal’s mouth.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Never leave a baby or young toddler alone with a dog or another animal. After a bite or scratch:

items: wash the wound thoroughly with soap and running water;
ask about rabies and tetanus management;
obtain information about the animal if it can be done safely.

blocks.text: Rabies exposure should never be managed by waiting for symptoms.
Outdoor and Playground Safety
Before play:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: look for sharp metal, glass or exposed concrete;
check that equipment matches the child’s ability;
avoid loose clothing or cords that could catch;
inspect sand and grass for animal waste or hazardous objects.

blocks.text: During hot weather:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: never cover a pram completely with a blanket, which may trap heat.

blocks.text: Festivals, Weddings and Crowded Gatherings
Busy celebrations create special risks:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Choose one clearly identified adult to supervise the young child at a time. Do not assume “everyone is watching.” When everyone is responsible, sometimes no one is.
Visitors and Grandparents’ Homes

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: A safe home can become unsafe when visiting another environment. Check for:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Speak respectfully but clearly. “We’re going to move these tablets while she is here because she puts everything in her mouth.” Safety is more important than avoiding a moment of awkwardness.
Choosing Childcare Safely
Ask a childcare setting:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: How are children signed in and out?
Are staff trained in first aid?
Are background checks and child-protection procedures used?
How are families informed about incidents?

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Visit at the time children are present if possible. Notice whether adults are attentive, calm and physically positioned to supervise.
Build a Family Emergency Plan
Keep the following information accessible:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: directions to the home if it is difficult to locate.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: India’s Emergency Response Support System uses 112 as the integrated national emergency number for urgent police, fire and medical assistance. Government of India: Emergency Response Support System Teach caregivers:

items: where the first-aid supplies are kept.

blocks.text: What Belongs in a Basic First-Aid Kit?
A family kit may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: medicines specifically advised for the child;

blocks.text: Keep medicines locked away from children. Check expiry dates regularly. A first-aid kit is useful only when caregivers also know how to use it.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Waiting Until the Child Can Reach a Hazard
Prepare for the next developmental ability.
Mistake 2: Relying Only on Verbal Warnings
Toddlers cannot consistently control curiosity or impulses.
Mistake 3: Leaving a Child “For Just a Second” Near Water
Drowning can occur quickly and silently.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Mistake 4: Storing Chemicals in Drink Bottles
This creates a serious risk of accidental swallowing.
Mistake 5: Assuming Child-Resistant Means Childproof
Locks and packaging can fail.
Mistake 6: Allowing an Older Child to Be the Only Supervisor
Older siblings may not recognise danger or respond effectively.
Mistake 7: Using Unsafe Home Remedies After Burns or Poisoning

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Use appropriate first aid and obtain professional guidance.
Mistake 8: Assuming Familiar Animals Are Always Safe
Supervision remains necessary.
🚩 Red-Flag Warning
Call emergency services immediately if a child:

items: is choking and cannot cough or make sound;
is unconscious or difficult to wake;

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: is drowning or has been pulled from water and is not breathing normally;
has swallowed a button battery, magnet or dangerous chemical;
has received a significant electric shock;
has been left in a hot vehicle;

blocks.text: In India, dial 112 for emergency assistance. Do not delay emergency care while searching online for a home treatment.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Your Room-by-Room Safety Walk
Kitchen
☐ Pot handles turned inward
☐ Hot drinks away from edges
☐ Knives and matches locked away
☐ Chemicals inaccessible
☐ Appliance cords secured
Bathroom
☐ Buckets emptied
☐ Medicines locked
☐ Cleaning products inaccessible
☐ Floor kept as dry as practical
☐ Child never left alone in water
Living Area
☐ Furniture anchored
☐ Small objects removed

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: ☐ Batteries secured
☐ Cords inaccessible
☐ Balcony and window locks checked
Bedroom
☐ Safe infant sleep space
☐ Medicines and cosmetics inaccessible
☐ Blind cords secured
☐ Windows protected
☐ No unstable furniture
Entrance and Outdoors
☐ Road access secured
☐ Gates close properly
☐ Water sources covered
☐ Tools and pesticides locked
☐ Vehicle safety equipment ready
Repeat the walk every few months. Your child will keep changing. The safety plan must change too.

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: 💛 Remember This

A safe environment does not limit healthy curiosity. It allows curiosity to grow without exposing a child to dangers they are too young to recognise. You cannot prevent every scrape. Nor should childhood be free from every manageable challenge. But falls from windows, burns from reachable drinks, swallowed medicines and unattended water are not lessons children need to learn through experience. Those are dangers adults must remove.
🌼 Try This Today

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Get down onto the floor in the room where your child spends the most time. Look from your child’s height. Find:

items: one object that could be swallowed;
one object that could be pulled down;
one item that needs to be locked away.

blocks.text: Correct those five risks today. Then place a reminder in your calendar to repeat the safety walk when your child begins the next movement stage.
Authoritative References

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

items: UNICEF — How to Babyproof Your Home
UNICEF — Preventing Choking, Suffocation and Strangulation
UNICEF — Burns, Scalds and Fire Safety
Government of India — Emergency Response Support System
Ministry of Road Transport and Highways — Child Safety Requirements for Two-Wheelers

pages.chapterTitle: Creating a Safer World for Your Child

blocks.text: Editorial note for the Indian edition: Emergency numbers, road-safety requirements, child-restraint standards, poison-information services and first-aid recommendations should be verified immediately before publication. Families should complete a recognised practical infant-and-child first-aid course. This chapter provides general prevention education and does not replace emergency or medical care.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell
pages.epigraph: “You do not need to diagnose every illness. You need to recognise discomfort, support recovery and know when your child needs help.”

blocks.text: At two in the morning, Sana touched her daughter’s forehead and felt heat. Within seconds, her mind filled with questions. How high was the fever? Was it teething? Should she give medicine? Should she wake her husband? Could she wait until morning? Her daughter was drinking, looking at her and protesting loudly against the thermometer. That gave Sana useful information. The temperature mattered. But so did the child in front of her. Over the next few years, most parents will face:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: uncertainty about whether to wait or seek care.

blocks.text: Confidence does not mean knowing exactly what every symptom means. It means having a simple process:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: When you are unsure, it is always appropriate to contact a qualified healthcare professional.
Look at the Child, Not Only the Symptom
A number on a thermometer does not tell the whole story. Neither does one cough, one loose stool or one episode of vomiting. Ask:
Appearance

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: Are they unusually pale, blue, grey or mottled?
Are they extremely sleepy or difficult to wake?

blocks.text: Breathing

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: Is there grunting, gasping or a harsh sound?
Can the child cry or speak normally?

blocks.text: Circulation and Hydration

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: Are there wet nappies or normal urination?
Are the hands and feet unusually cold with poor responsiveness?

blocks.text: Behaviour

items: Is the child interested in anything?
Is the cry unusually weak, high-pitched or continuous?
Is the child behaving very differently from usual?

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: A child with a moderate fever who is drinking and interacting may be less concerning than a child with a lower temperature who is limp, struggling to breathe or refusing every fluid.
General Danger Signs
Seek urgent medical care if your child:

items: is unconscious, unusually drowsy or difficult to wake;

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: becomes blue, grey or extremely pale;
has a stiff neck with illness;
has a rash with serious illness or abnormal responsiveness;
loses a skill or behaves neurologically differently.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: WHO’s Integrated Management of Childhood Illness identifies inability to drink, vomiting everything, convulsions and abnormal drowsiness or unconsciousness among important childhood danger signs. WHO: IMCI Danger Signs In India, dial 112 for urgent emergency assistance.
Understanding Fever

A fever is a rise in body temperature, usually in response to infection. Fever itself is not a disease. It is one part of the body’s immune response. For most children, the aim is not to force the temperature to become perfectly normal. The aim is to:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: identify whether medical assessment is needed.

blocks.text: Measuring Temperature
Use a reliable digital thermometer. The method may include:

items: orally in an older cooperative child;
another method recommended by your healthcare professional.

blocks.text: Temperature readings vary slightly according to the method used. Tell the clinician:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: whether medicine had already been given.

blocks.text: Do not rely only on touching the forehead. A child can feel warm without having a significant fever, and a seriously ill child may not always feel extremely hot.
Fever in a Young Infant
A temperature of 38°C or higher in a baby younger than three months requires prompt medical assessment. Do not assume it is:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Young infants can become seriously unwell quickly and may show fewer obvious symptoms.
Helping a Child with Fever
You can:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: keep the room at a comfortable temperature;
use fever or pain medicine only in the correct child-specific dose and according to professional advice.

blocks.text: Do not:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: wrap the child in heavy blankets to “sweat out” the fever;
give aspirin to a child unless a specialist has prescribed it for a specific medical condition.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Paracetamol and ibuprofen are different medicines with different precautions. The correct dose is generally based on the child’s weight and the product’s concentration—not simply the child’s age. Use the measuring device supplied with the medicine. Avoid ibuprofen in a dehydrated child or when a clinician has advised against it. If dengue is possible, seek medical advice before giving medicines because some can increase bleeding risk.
When Fever Needs Medical Advice
Seek advice promptly if the child:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: is younger than three months and has a temperature of 38°C or higher;
is unusually drowsy or difficult to comfort;
has fever after being left in a hot environment;

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: HealthyChildren.org: Fever Without Fear A fever that settles after medicine does not automatically prove that the illness is minor. Continue observing the child.
Coughs and Colds
Young children commonly develop viral respiratory infections. Symptoms may include:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: A simple cold often improves with time and supportive care.
Supportive Care
Depending on the child’s age and medical history, you may:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: use sterile or appropriately prepared saline nose drops;
keep the child away from smoke and strong fragrances;
offer honey for cough only after the first birthday.

blocks.text: Do not give honey to a baby younger than 12 months.
Cough and Cold Medicines

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Over-the-counter cough and cold mixtures can be unsafe for young children and often provide little benefit. Do not give them to a baby or toddler unless specifically advised by a qualified doctor who knows:
HealthyChildren.org: Coughs and Colds
Breathing Difficulty
Breathing difficulty requires more attention than the sound of an ordinary cough. Look for:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: ribs or lower chest pulling inward;
a harsh noise while breathing in;

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: If the child is struggling to breathe, becoming blue or unable to drink, call emergency services. Do not place a child over steam or boiling water. Steam can cause serious burns and has not been shown to cure the infection.
Vomiting
Babies may spit up small amounts after feeding. Vomiting is usually more forceful. When speaking with a doctor, note:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: whether the child can keep down fluids;
whether diarrhoea or fever is also present.

blocks.text: Seek Prompt Medical Care If Vomit Is

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: forceful and repeated in a young infant;
accompanied by a swollen or severely painful abdomen;
occurring after a significant head injury.

blocks.text: A child vomiting repeatedly may become dehydrated quickly.
Offering Fluids During Vomiting
If the child is alert and does not have a danger sign:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: offer an age-appropriate oral rehydration solution in small, frequent sips;
use a spoon, cup or oral syringe if needed;
increase slowly if the child keeps it down.

blocks.text: A large drink may trigger more vomiting. Small amounts offered frequently may be easier to tolerate. If the child cannot keep down any fluid, becomes lethargic or urinates much less, seek medical care.
Diarrhoea

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Diarrhoea generally means stools that are looser or more watery than usual, often with increased frequency. Breastfed babies naturally pass soft stools, so compare with the child’s normal pattern. The main immediate risk is dehydration.
Continue Feeding
During diarrhoea:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: give oral rehydration solution as advised;
avoid soft drinks and very sugary beverages;

blocks.text: Do not starve a child with diarrhoea. Nutrition supports recovery.
Oral Rehydration Solution
ORS replaces water and important salts lost through diarrhoea and vomiting. Use a recognised ORS packet and:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: mix it with exactly the amount of safe water stated on the packet;
do not add extra sugar or salt;
do not mix it into milk, juice or soft drinks;
use it within the storage time stated in the instructions;

blocks.text: If the child vomits, pause briefly and restart more slowly. India’s IMNCI guidance advises frequent small sips of ORS, continued feeding and continued breastfeeding. National Health Mission: IMNCI Health Worker Module

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Zinc

Indian childhood-diarrhoea guidance commonly includes a 14-day course of zinc in an age-appropriate dose. Use zinc only in the correct formulation and dose advised by a doctor or trained health worker. Do not estimate the amount using an adult supplement.
Avoid Anti-Diarrhoeal Medicine Without Advice
Medicines that attempt to stop bowel movement can be dangerous for young children. Do not use them unless a qualified clinician specifically prescribes them.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Signs of Dehydration
Watch for:

items: fewer wet nappies or reduced urination;
a sunken soft spot in a young baby;
cool hands and feet with poor responsiveness.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Dehydration can become serious quickly in infants. Seek care early if you are concerned. UNICEF: Vomiting and Diarrhoea in Babies
Diarrhoea Red Flags
Seek medical assessment if:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: there is blood in the stool;
the stool is black without an expected explanation;
the child has poor growth or another health condition.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Rashes
Rashes have many possible causes, including:
A photograph can be useful, but it cannot always establish the cause. Note:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: whether it is itchy or painful;
whether the child is otherwise well;
whether a new food, medicine or product was introduced;
whether breathing or facial swelling is affected.

blocks.text: Seek Emergency Help If a Rash Is Accompanied By

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: swelling of the lips, tongue or face;

blocks.text: Do not delay emergency care to perform repeated online “rash tests.”
Allergic Reactions
A mild local rash and a severe allergic reaction are not the same. Possible severe signs include:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: swelling of the tongue or throat;
repeated vomiting after a food or medicine;

blocks.text: Call emergency services. If the child has a prescribed adrenaline auto-injector and allergy action plan, follow it immediately. Anyone caring for the child should know:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Ear Pain
Young children may not be able to say that an ear hurts. Possible signs include:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Ear pulling alone does not prove an infection. Do not put oil, earbuds, matchsticks or unprescribed drops into the ear. Seek medical care for:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Constipation
Constipation is not defined only by how many days pass between stools. A child may be constipated if stools are:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: accompanied by cracks or a small amount of surface blood.

blocks.text: Support may include:

items: a calm toilet routine in older toddlers;

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Do not repeatedly use suppositories, enemas, herbal remedies or adult laxatives without professional guidance. Seek care if constipation is accompanied by:
Medicine Safety
Medicines help when they are used correctly. They can harm when the product, dose or timing is wrong.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Before Giving Medicine
Check:
Use the supplied oral syringe, cup or measuring spoon. A kitchen spoon is not an accurate measuring tool.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Keep a Medicine Record
This reduces accidental double-dosing when several adults are caring for the child.
Never

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: share one child’s prescription with another;
alter the dose because the child looks “very sick”;
crush or mix medicine without checking;
give adult formulations without explicit instruction.

blocks.text: Store medicines locked, out of sight and in their original containers.
Antibiotics

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Antibiotics treat particular bacterial infections. They do not treat ordinary viral colds. Using antibiotics unnecessarily can cause:

items: less effective treatment in the future.

blocks.text: Use antibiotics only when prescribed for the child’s current illness. Follow the prescribed:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Do not stop, extend or restart an antibiotic without discussing it with the prescriber. WHO identifies misuse and overuse of antimicrobial medicines as major drivers of resistance. WHO: Antimicrobial Resistance
Traditional and Home Remedies
Some family remedies provide comfort. Others can delay treatment or cause harm. Before using a remedy, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: Is it safe for this age?
Could it contain alcohol, heavy metals or another contaminant?
Is it replacing needed medical care?
Could it cause choking, burns or aspiration?
Is it being applied to broken skin?

blocks.text: Avoid giving infants:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: remedies recommended only through social media;
anything that causes vomiting or diarrhoea as a “cleansing” treatment.

blocks.text: Natural does not automatically mean safe.
Caring for a Sick Child at Home
If a clinician has advised home care:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: know which signs require an earlier return.

blocks.text: Write instructions down. Tired parents cannot be expected to remember every verbal detail.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Preventing Infection at Home
Useful habits include:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: washing hands before food preparation and after nappy changes;
cleaning frequently touched surfaces when needed;
keeping sick children away from vulnerable newborns;

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Do not attempt to create a completely germ-free home. Focus on high-value hygiene practices.
Questions to Ask at the Doctor’s Appointment

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: What do you think is causing the symptoms?
Which danger signs should I watch for?
What can I do at home?
What is the exact dose and duration?
What should I do if my child vomits the medicine?
Are any foods or medicines to be avoided?
When can my child return to childcare?

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Before leaving, repeat the plan in your own words. “So I will give this amount twice daily for five days and return earlier if breathing becomes difficult. Is that correct?” This allows mistakes to be corrected immediately.
What to Bring to a Medical Visit
Take:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: photographs or videos of intermittent symptoms;
the suspected medicine or product container after accidental ingestion.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Explain the timeline clearly: “Fever began yesterday evening. She vomited three times overnight and has passed urine once since morning.” Specific observations are more useful than saying only: “She is not well.”
Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Treating the Temperature Instead of Observing the Child
Comfort and danger signs matter alongside the number.
Mistake 2: Giving Several Combination Medicines

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Different products may contain the same ingredient and cause accidental overdose.
Mistake 3: Using Antibiotics for Every Fever or Cold
Many childhood infections are viral.
Mistake 4: Stopping Fluids During Vomiting or Diarrhoea
Small, frequent amounts are usually important unless a clinician advises otherwise.
Mistake 5: Mixing ORS Incorrectly
Use the exact water quantity printed on the packet.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Mistake 6: Waiting for a Very Sick Child to Improve Overnight
Danger signs require prompt assessment.
Mistake 7: Using Another Child’s Prescription
The diagnosis, dose and formulation may be wrong.
Mistake 8: Assuming Teething Explains Significant Illness
Teething should not be used to dismiss high fever, severe diarrhoea, breathing difficulty or unusual drowsiness.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: 🚩 Red-Flag Warning
Call emergency services or seek urgent medical care if your child:

items: becomes blue, grey or very pale;
is unconscious or difficult to wake;
has a stiff neck and appears very ill;

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: has swallowed poison, a button battery or magnets;
has a temperature of 38°C or higher and is younger than three months;
makes you feel that something is seriously wrong.

blocks.text: A parent’s concern does not replace examination. But it is a valid reason to seek one.
Your Illness Observation Sheet
Child’s Name

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Symptoms Began
Highest Temperature and Method
Drinking or Breastfeeding
Wet Nappies or Urination
Vomiting or Stools

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Breathing
Rash or Pain
Medicines Given
Changes Since Symptoms Began
________________________________ Keep this page near your child’s health records. Clear observations help clinicians make safer decisions.
💛 Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: You do not need to choose between panicking and ignoring a symptom. You can observe calmly, provide safe care and ask for help when something concerns you. Most minor childhood illnesses improve. Some do not. The skill is not predicting every outcome. It is recognising when the child’s appearance, breathing, hydration or behaviour tells you that waiting is no longer appropriate.
🌼 Try This Today
Create a note on your phone called: My Child’s Medical Information Include:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell
When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Then place a thermometer, oral medicine syringe and current health record somewhere adults can locate quickly—but medicines remain locked away. Preparation cannot prevent illness. It can make a worrying night much easier to manage.
Authoritative References

items: World Health Organization — IMCI Danger Signs
National Health Mission — IMNCI Participant Module for Health Workers

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

items: National Health Mission — Facility-Based IMNCI Module
UNICEF — Vomiting and Diarrhoea in Babies
HealthyChildren.org — When to Call Emergency Medical Services
Government of India — Emergency Response Support System

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Is Unwell

blocks.text: Editorial note for the Indian edition: Fever thresholds, medicine guidance, diarrhoea management, emergency pathways and national clinical recommendations must be reviewed by an Indian paediatrician immediately before publication. Medicine doses have deliberately not been provided because they depend on the child’s weight, formulation, health and clinical assessment. This chapter provides general education and does not replace medical care.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits
pages.epigraph: “Some of the most important things you do for your child happen before anything appears to be wrong.”

blocks.text: When Aditi’s son turned one, he looked healthy. He was active, eating, sleeping reasonably well and learning new things. A relative asked why she was taking him to the doctor when he was not ill. Aditi explained that the appointment was not for sickness. It was to review:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: questions that had been gathering for months.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: The visit identified no illness. But the paediatrician noticed that one vaccination had been delayed during an earlier fever, discussed iron-rich food and showed Aditi how to begin brushing the child’s new teeth. Preventive care often looks uneventful. That is its strength. It helps families protect health, notice concerns early and make small adjustments before difficulties become larger.
What Is Preventive Care?
Preventive care includes the routine actions that help a child stay healthy. It covers:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Preventive care does not guarantee that a child will never become ill. It reduces avoidable risks and helps identify problems earlier.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Routine Health Visits

Do not visit a healthcare professional only when your child has a fever. Regular well-child visits give families time to discuss areas that may be overlooked during an illness appointment. A routine visit may include:

items: measuring head circumference during relevant ages;

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: deciding whether screening or referral is needed.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: WHO describes scheduled well-care visits as a way to support children’s growth, development and wellbeing while guiding parents in the care they provide. WHO: Scheduled Child and Adolescent Well-Care Visits The exact visit schedule depends on:
Ask your child’s healthcare provider when the next routine visit should occur before leaving the clinic.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Growth Monitoring
Growth monitoring is not simply weighing a child. It involves:

items: plotting the measurement on an appropriate chart;
looking at the pattern over time;

blocks.text: Measurements Commonly Used

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: length for a child measured lying down;
head circumference during infancy and early childhood;
weight in relation to length or height.

blocks.text: One Number Is Not the Whole Story
A child does not need to remain on the middle line of a growth chart. Healthy children come in different sizes. What matters includes:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: whether the child is feeding and developing well;
whether illness or prematurity affects interpretation.

blocks.text: Do not compare a child’s weight casually with a cousin’s. A trained professional should interpret the child’s own growth pattern. WHO: Child Growth Standards
When Growth Deserves Attention
Ask for assessment if:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: the child appears unusually pale or tired;
the child has swelling of both feet;
you are worried about excessive weight gain.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Growth concerns should not be managed by forcing food or buying supplements without understanding the cause. The child may need:
Vaccination

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Vaccines train the immune system to recognise and respond to particular infections. They protect individual children and help reduce the spread of disease through communities. India’s Universal Immunisation Programme provides vaccines against several serious vaccine-preventable diseases through public-health services. National Health Mission: Immunisation Vaccination begins at birth and continues through childhood. Some vaccines require:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: catch-up if a dose is delayed.

blocks.text: Use the Current Indian Schedule
Vaccination schedules change when:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: For that reason, this book should not become the family’s only vaccination timetable. Use:

items: the Mother and Child Protection card;
advice from a qualified vaccination provider;
an individual catch-up plan if doses were delayed.

blocks.text: The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare publishes the current National Immunisation Schedule . Before every appointment, confirm that the version being used is current.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Government and Additional Vaccines

Parents may notice that a private paediatric clinic discusses vaccines beyond those listed in the national public programme. This does not necessarily mean one schedule is wrong. The national programme is designed for population-wide public-health delivery. A paediatric professional may discuss additional vaccines based on:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: Which disease does this vaccine prevent?
Is it part of the national schedule?
Is it routinely recommended by a recognised paediatric authority?
Why is it being considered for my child?
What happens if the dose is delayed?
Where will the dose be recorded?

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: The purpose is not to collect the largest number of vaccines. It is to make informed decisions using current, credible guidance.
Before a Vaccination Visit
Bring:
Tell the provider if the child:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: has had a severe allergic reaction;
may need an individual vaccination plan.

blocks.text: A minor illness does not always require vaccination to be postponed. Do not decide this yourself. Let the vaccination provider assess the child.
After Vaccination

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Common short-term reactions may include:
Ask the provider:

items: how to keep the child comfortable;

blocks.text: Keep the child’s vaccination record updated before leaving.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Seek Urgent Help After Vaccination If the Child Develops

items: swelling of the face, tongue or throat;
persistent inconsolable crying with concerning illness;
another severe or rapidly worsening reaction.

blocks.text: A serious reaction should be evaluated and documented. Do not allow fear of being judged to prevent you from reporting it.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Delayed or Missed Vaccinations
A missed appointment does not usually mean the entire vaccination series must be abandoned. Ask for a catch-up plan . Bring every available record. Do not:

items: postpone asking because you feel embarrassed.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Healthcare providers solve incomplete records regularly. The next best step is to review the child’s history now.
Pulse Polio and Campaign Doses

Families may be invited to participate in public-health vaccination campaigns, such as Pulse Polio activities. A campaign dose may be recommended even when routine vaccinations are current. Follow official campaign guidance. Do not assume that a campaign dose automatically replaces routine doses unless the health authority specifically says so. Keep routine immunisation appointments and records.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Vaccine Information and Misinformation
Vaccination misinformation often spreads through:

items: personal stories presented as scientific proof;
claims that one remedy replaces vaccination.

blocks.text: Before accepting a claim, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: Is it from the Ministry of Health, WHO, UNICEF or a recognised medical body?
Is the person qualified in vaccination?
Does the claim promise certainty or use fear?

blocks.text: Questions are healthy. Reliable answers come from transparent evidence—not from being told never to ask.
Developmental Surveillance and Screening

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Development should be discussed during routine health visits. A professional may ask about:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Developmental surveillance is ongoing observation. Developmental screening uses a structured tool at selected ages or when a concern arises. Screening is not a diagnosis. It helps determine whether further assessment may be useful. Bring examples from home and mention any:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Do not wait for school admission to discuss something you noticed in infancy.
Hearing
Hearing supports speech, language, learning and safety. A child may have hearing difficulty even if they respond to some sounds. Ask for assessment if the child:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: does not startle or respond to sound as expected;
watches faces closely but misses spoken information;
uses the television at unusually high volume;
has a medical risk factor for hearing loss.

blocks.text: Do not assume the child is “ignoring you.” A formal hearing assessment may be needed.
Vision

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Vision develops through early childhood. Discuss assessment if you notice:

items: an eye turning inward or outward persistently;
a white or unusual pupil reflection;

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: A child may not tell you they cannot see clearly because they do not know what normal vision looks like. A white pupil reflection in photographs or ordinary light deserves prompt medical assessment.
Oral Health Begins with the First Tooth
Baby teeth matter. They help with:
Tooth decay can cause:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Before Teeth Appear
You can gently clean the gums with a clean, soft cloth.
When the First Tooth Appears

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: use a very small smear of fluoride toothpaste according to current professional guidance;

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: For children younger than three, many paediatric dental authorities advise an amount approximately the size of a grain of rice. Ask a dentist about the appropriate toothpaste in your area, particularly where natural fluoride in water varies. HealthyChildren.org: Good Oral Health Starts Early
The First Dental Visit
Arrange a dental visit after the first tooth appears and ideally by the first birthday, or according to current local professional advice. A first visit can help with:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Do not wait for tooth pain. Preventive dentistry is easier than emergency dentistry.
Preventing Early Tooth Decay

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: do not dip a pacifier in honey or sugar;
avoid putting a child to sleep with a bottle containing milk, juice or sweet liquid;

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Frequent exposure matters. A child slowly sipping a sweet drink over several hours exposes teeth repeatedly.
Teething
Teething may cause:
Safer comfort options include:

items: medicine only when recommended in the correct dose.

blocks.text: Avoid:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: blaming significant fever, severe diarrhoea or serious illness on teething.

blocks.text: Teething should not prevent you from seeking medical care when the child is genuinely unwell.
Vitamin A, Iron and Other Micronutrients
India’s child-health programmes may provide or recommend:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: other interventions according to national or state programmes.

blocks.text: These are not ordinary sweets or general tonics. Give them according to the current schedule and dose advised by a qualified health worker. Do not combine several supplements without checking for duplication. Excess amounts of some vitamins and minerals can be harmful. Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: Why does my child need it?
Is it already contained in another product?
What should I do if a dose is missed?

blocks.text: Record it on the child’s health card.
Anaemia Prevention
Iron deficiency and anaemia can affect energy and development. Prevention may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: vitamin-C-rich foods alongside plant sources of iron;
avoiding excessive cow’s milk after 12 months;
screening when risk factors or symptoms exist.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Possible signs such as pallor, unusual fatigue or poor appetite require professional assessment. They do not confirm anaemia by themselves. Do not begin high-dose iron without advice. Accidental iron overdose can be dangerous.
Hygiene Without Fear
Children need clean care. They do not need a sterile childhood. Focus on moments when handwashing has high value:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: after coughing or sneezing into the hands;

blocks.text: Use soap and safe water. Teach toddlers by:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: placing a safe step where appropriate;
helping them rub all parts of the hands;

blocks.text: Avoid excessive use of harsh antibacterial products on a child’s skin unless medically advised.
Physical Activity and Outdoor Time
Young children need frequent opportunities to move. For babies, this includes:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: For toddlers, it includes:
Movement supports:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Outdoor time should be adapted for:
Avoid leaving a child restrained in a pram, high chair or car seat for prolonged periods when restriction is not needed.
Building a Reliable Health Record
Keep one home-based child health record. It may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits
Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: WHO recommends home-based records as a complement to health-facility records because they support communication and continuity of care. WHO: Home-Based Maternal, Newborn and Child Health Records
Paper and Digital Copies
Keep:

items: the original in a safe place;
a backup accessible to another trusted caregiver;
key emergency information on your phone.

blocks.text: A digital record should complement—not casually replace—the original where official documentation is required.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Preparing for a Well-Child Visit
A week before the visit, note:
Growth and Feeding
Development

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Health
Prevention
Family Wellbeing

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: The health of the caregiver affects the health of the child. It is appropriate to mention when the family is struggling.
Questions Worth Asking

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: Is my child’s growth pattern appropriate?
Should hearing or vision be assessed?
Does my child need iron, vitamin A or another supplement?
When should the next dental visit occur?
What safety change should we make for the next developmental stage?
What is the next routine appointment?
Which symptoms should bring us back earlier?

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Visiting Only During Illness
Routine care identifies risks that may not produce symptoms.
Mistake 2: Treating the Growth Chart Like a School Rank
The individual trend matters more than being on a particular line.
Mistake 3: Abandoning Vaccination After a Missed Dose
Ask for a catch-up plan.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Mistake 4: Keeping Several Incomplete Records
Choose one primary record and update it consistently.
Mistake 5: Waiting for Tooth Pain Before Visiting a Dentist
Dental care should begin preventively.
Mistake 6: Giving Supplements Because Another Child Takes Them
Needs and doses differ.
Mistake 7: Believing Hearing Is Normal Because the Child Responds to Loud Sounds

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Partial or fluctuating hearing difficulty may still affect language.
Mistake 8: Dismissing Developmental Concerns Until School
Early assessment can provide earlier support.
🚩 Red-Flag Warning
Be cautious if:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: someone offers vaccination without appropriate storage, records or trained administration;
you are asked to hide a previous severe reaction;
a clinic cannot explain what vaccine or medicine is being given;
a product claims to replace vaccination;

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: a supplement promises extraordinary intelligence, height or immunity;
a health record is altered without explanation;
significant growth or developmental concerns are repeatedly dismissed without assessment;
an eye develops a white pupil reflection;
hearing or vision appears to decline.

blocks.text: Preventive care should be transparent, documented and open to questions.

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Your Preventive-Care Checklist
Every Routine Visit
☐ Bring the health and vaccination record
☐ Review growth
☐ Discuss development
☐ Check vaccines due
☐ Review feeding and supplements
☐ Mention hearing or vision concerns
☐ Ask about teeth
☐ Discuss safety for the next stage
☐ Write down the follow-up date
At Home
☐ Keep records together
☐ Brush teeth twice daily after they appear
☐ Offer varied, age-appropriate food
☐ Provide daily movement and play

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: ☐ Practise hand hygiene
☐ Maintain a smoke-free environment
☐ Review home safety as abilities change
☐ Keep emergency information current
💛 Remember This

Preventive care is not evidence that you expect something to go wrong. It is how you give healthy development the best chance to continue. A vaccination recorded. A growth trend noticed. A hearing concern checked. A tooth brushed. A safety risk removed. These actions rarely feel dramatic. Over childhood, they become deeply important.
🌼 Try This Today

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Collect your child’s:

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Place them in one labelled folder. Then create a one-page summary: Child’s name: ________________________________ Date of birth: ________________________________ Allergies: ___________________________________ Medical conditions: ___________________________ Current medicines: ____________________________ Vaccinations current through: __________________ Next routine visit: ____________________________ Paediatrician: ________________________________ Emergency contact: ____________________________

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Photograph the page and share it with one trusted caregiver. Good records do not replace good care. They help good care follow your child wherever they go.
Authoritative References

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

items: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare — National Immunisation Schedule
National Health Mission — Universal Immunisation Programme
WHO — Scheduled Child and Adolescent Well-Care Visits
WHO — Home-Based Records for Maternal, Newborn and Child Health
HealthyChildren.org — Good Oral Health Starts Early

pages.chapterTitle: Preventive Care and Healthy Habits

blocks.text: Editorial note for the Indian edition: The National Immunisation Schedule, regional vaccines, vitamin A, iron, deworming, fluoride and other preventive-care guidance must be checked against the latest Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, National Health Mission, Indian Academy of Pediatrics and Indian dental guidance immediately before publication. This chapter deliberately directs families to current official schedules rather than reproducing a timetable that may later become outdated.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care
pages.epigraph: “A child does not lose a secure bond by being loved by more than one dependable caregiver.”

blocks.text: When Devika prepared to return to work, everyone had an opinion. One person told her a good mother would stay home longer. Another said the baby needed daycare to become independent. A relative insisted that only family could be trusted. A colleague recommended a nanny. Devika was already carrying her own mixed feelings. She missed her work. She worried about leaving her son. She wanted him to feel safe. She also wanted to remain connected to the part of herself that existed outside motherhood. The decision was not simply:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: “Should I work or stay home?” It was: “Who will care for my child?” “Will they notice what he needs?” “Will they keep him safe?” “How will we communicate?” “Will he still know that I am his mother?” Those are reasonable questions. A child can form loving relationships with parents, grandparents, nannies, childcare workers and other regular caregivers. The number of caregivers is not the main issue. What matters is the quality, safety, predictability and responsiveness of the care.
There Is No Single Correct Care Arrangement
Families may use:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Each option has strengths and limitations.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

rows: Is the parent supported and able to rest?
Are safety and care practices current and agreed?
How will the caregiver be verified, supervised and supported?

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

rows: What standards, supervision and emergency plans are used?
Multiple staff, structured systems, peer contact
What is the adult-to-child ratio and staff turnover?
Community access, nutrition, health links and early childhood services
Which services and timings are available locally?

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

rows: Can all caregivers follow essential routines and safety rules?

blocks.text: The best arrangement is not the one other families admire. It is the one that safely meets the needs of your child and family.
Start with Five Essential Questions
Before discussing toys, curriculum or cameras, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: Will my child be physically safe?
Will someone respond when my child is distressed?
Are feeding, sleep and hygiene handled safely?
Can I communicate openly with the caregiver?
What happens when something goes wrong?

blocks.text: A beautiful classroom does not compensate for inattentive care. An impressive activity calendar does not compensate for unsafe sleep. A caregiver’s affection does not remove the need for clear procedures. Look beneath the presentation.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: What Responsive Care Looks Like
A responsive caregiver:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: WHO’s nurturing-care framework identifies good health, adequate nutrition, safety, responsive caregiving and opportunities for early learning as connected foundations for healthy development. WHO: Nurturing Care Framework You are not looking for someone who never allows your child to cry. You are looking for someone who tries to understand why the child is crying and responds appropriately.
Choosing a Childcare Centre or Crèche

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Visit before enrolling the child. If possible, visit while children are present. Observe rather than relying only on the guided tour.
People
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: How many children does each adult supervise?
Does the ratio change during breaks?
Are staff references and background checks completed?
How long do staff members usually remain?
Is someone trained in infant and child first aid always present?
Who is responsible for each child during transitions?

blocks.text: Staff turnover matters. Children benefit from familiar, dependable adults.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Environment
Look for:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Use your senses. Does the space smell strongly of urine, smoke, chemicals or dampness? Are children left crying for long periods? Are adults looking at phones while supervising? Does the environment feel calm or chaotic?
Questions About Daily Care

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Feeding
Ask:

items: How are bottles, cups and utensils cleaned?
How is expressed breast milk labelled and stored?

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Sleep
Ask:

items: Are infants placed on their backs?
Is the sleep surface firm and flat?
Are pillows, loose blankets and soft toys kept out of infant sleep spaces?
Does each child have an individual safe sleep space?
What happens if a child will not sleep?

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Nappies and Toileting
Ask:

items: Are caregivers’ hands washed between children?
Are children ever shamed for accidents?

blocks.text: Illness
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: Which symptoms require a child to stay home?
How will the family be contacted?
Where does a sick child wait?
How are infectious illnesses communicated to families?

blocks.text: Safety and Emergency Procedures
The childcare provider should be able to explain:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: severe weather or local disaster plans;

blocks.text: Ask: “Please show me how an emergency would be handled.” A vague answer such as “Nothing has ever happened here” is not a safety plan.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Cameras: Helpful, but Not Proof of Safety
Some childcare centres provide live camera access. This may offer reassurance, but cameras have limits. They may not show:

items: who else can access the footage.

blocks.text: Ask about:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: A camera is one tool. It does not replace:
Choosing a Nanny or Home Caregiver

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Invite the person for more than one conversation if possible. Observe how they interact with the child.
Verify

items: police or background verification where available and appropriate;

blocks.text: Speak directly with previous employers. Ask specific questions:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: How long did the person work with you?
How did they respond to crying?

blocks.text: Do not rely only on a photocopied document or an agency’s promise.
Discuss Expectations Clearly
Cover:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care
Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Put essential expectations in writing. Clarity protects both the family and caregiver.
Trial Period and Supported Observation
A trial period helps everyone learn. Begin with you present. Observe whether the caregiver:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Then try a short separation. Build gradually. Do not create secret tests involving the child’s safety. Use transparent expectations and reasonable monitoring.
Sharing Care with Grandparents and Relatives

Family care can be loving and deeply valuable. It can also create disagreements. Grandparents may say: “We raised children without all these rules.” Respect their experience while remaining clear about current safety information. Begin with essential non-negotiables:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care
Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Try: “I know you kept us safe with the information available then. Some guidance has changed, so I need everyone to follow these particular rules.” Avoid correcting every small difference. A grandparent may sing a different song, serve food in another bowl or settle the child differently. Consistency matters most around health, safety and core boundaries. Children can adapt to more than one loving style.
Anganwadi Services

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: India’s Anganwadi system forms part of Mission Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0. Depending on age and local availability, services may include:
Implementation and facilities vary locally. Visit the nearest centre and ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: Which services are available at this centre?

blocks.text: Government of India: Mission Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 Public and private services can complement one another. Families should know which community resources exist.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Preparing the Child for a New Caregiver
A gradual transition is often helpful.
Before Care Begins

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: introduce the caregiver while you are present;
allow the caregiver to join familiar play;
let the caregiver participate in one routine;
send a familiar comfort item if safe and permitted.

blocks.text: Use Simple Language

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: “Nani will stay with you while Mama goes to work. You will eat, play and sleep. I will come back after your nap.” Do not say: “I will be back in one minute,” if you will be gone all day. Trust grows through accurate, repeated experiences.
The First Days of Separation
The child may:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: show anger towards the returning parent;

blocks.text: These reactions can occur during adjustment. Respond with:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Avoid leaving secretly. Disappearing may stop the immediate protest, but it can make the child more watchful because departure becomes unpredictable. UNICEF advises gradual preparation and consistent goodbyes when managing separation anxiety and returning to work. UNICEF: Returning to Work After a Baby
A Goodbye Should Be Short, Not Cold
A useful goodbye includes:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: “I know you feel sad. Rina aunty will take care of you. I will come back after snack time. I love you. Goodbye.” Then leave. Repeatedly returning after saying goodbye can make the child wonder whether greater distress will stop the departure. The caregiver can begin a familiar activity after you leave.
Reunion Matters
When you return:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: maintain a calm evening where possible.

blocks.text: A toddler who ignores you briefly or becomes angry may be expressing the difficulty of separation. You can say: “You wanted me. I came back.” The child does not need to demonstrate happiness in the expected way.
Creating a Written Care Plan
Every regular caregiver should receive the same essential information.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Child Information
Feeding

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Sleep
Communication

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: words and gestures the child uses;
ways the child asks for comfort.

blocks.text: Emergency
Update the plan as the child grows.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Expressed Breast Milk and Work
A mother who wishes to continue breastfeeding may discuss:

items: direct feeds before and after work;
feeding by cup or another suitable method;
an individual plan with a lactation professional.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Storage times depend on temperature, handling conditions and current guidance. Use an up-to-date written protocol from a recognised health authority rather than informal advice. Not every mother will choose or be able to express milk. A safe feeding plan should support the child without judging the mother.
Formula and Childcare
If the child receives formula:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: never leave a baby propped with a bottle.

blocks.text: Do not assume every caregiver understands preparation because they have fed another baby. Demonstrate the exact procedure.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Medicines at Childcare
Medication should be handled through a written process. Provide:
The provider should record:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Do not place unlabelled medicine in a child’s bag. Do not ask a childcare worker to “give a little if needed” without precise written guidance.
Discipline Across Caregivers
Agree that caregivers will not use:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Provide alternatives:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Ask a caregiver directly: “What would you do if my child hit another child?” “What would you do if she refused food?” “What would you do during a tantrum?” The answer reveals more than a general promise to “take good care.”
Safeguarding and Body Safety
Young children depend on adults to notice risk. A care setting should have rules about:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Parents can begin simple body-safety habits early:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: use clear names for body parts;
teach that caregiving touch should be explained;
tell the child they can seek help;

blocks.text: Avoid frightening children with the idea that all unfamiliar people are dangerous. Risk can involve known people too. The goal is body autonomy, communication and attentive adult protection.
Signs That Deserve Careful Attention
Take concerns seriously if the child:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: becomes extremely fearful of a particular person;
shows a sudden major change in behaviour;
appears excessively hungry, thirsty or unclean after care;
uses sexualised behaviour or language that concerns you;
repeatedly resists care in a way that feels different from ordinary separation distress;

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: These signs do not prove abuse by themselves. They do justify careful, prompt assessment. Remain calm. Listen without leading questions. Do not pressure the child to repeat a story multiple times. Prioritise safety and seek professional help. In India, Child Helpline 1098 operates under Mission Vatsalya and is integrated with emergency response number 112. Government of India: Child Helpline 1098
Communication with the Caregiver

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Agree on what should be reported daily. A simple update might include:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

rows: Play, outdoor time or new skills
Fever, cough, rash, vomiting or injury

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Do not require a caregiver to spend the entire day documenting and photographing instead of caring for the child. Choose the information that genuinely supports continuity.
When Something Goes Wrong
Minor incidents can occur even in good care. What matters is how the caregiver responds. Look for:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: review of how to prevent recurrence;

blocks.text: Be concerned by:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: blame placed entirely on the child;
retaliation after a parent raises a concern.

blocks.text: A trustworthy provider does not claim perfection. They demonstrate transparency.
Returning to Work
Returning to work can bring:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: None of these emotions determines whether the decision is right or wrong. Prepare practically.
Before Returning

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: reduce unnecessary commitments during the first weeks.

blocks.text: At Work
Where possible:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: identify a private feeding or expressing space if needed;
know whom to contact if childcare calls;
avoid expecting immediate pre-parenthood productivity from yourself.

blocks.text: Returning is a transition, not a switch.
When You Choose to Stay Home
Staying home is also a form of work. A parent providing full-time care needs:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Choosing parent care does not mean one person should be available without rest every hour of every day. Build a backup circle. Every primary caregiver needs someone who can safely step in.
Backup Care
Ask in advance:

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: Who can care for the child if the main caregiver is ill?
Who can collect the child unexpectedly?
Does the backup person know the child’s allergies?
Do they have written permission where needed?
Is the car seat or transport plan available?

blocks.text: An emergency is not the best time to introduce the care plan.
Common Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Mistake 1: Choosing Only by Distance or Cost
Practical factors matter, but safety and quality must remain central.
Mistake 2: Assuming Family Care Requires No Instructions
Relatives also need current information about feeding, sleep, medicine and safety.
Mistake 3: Disappearing to Avoid Tears
A predictable goodbye supports trust.
Mistake 4: Judging Care Only by Activities

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Responsive adults matter more than a crowded timetable.
Mistake 5: Avoiding Difficult Questions
Ask directly about discipline, injuries, staff checks and emergencies.
Mistake 6: Treating Cameras as Complete Protection
They cannot replace trained, accountable care.
Mistake 7: Giving Unclear Medicine Instructions
Use written, exact directions.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Mistake 8: Ignoring Your Own Persistent Concern
Observe, document, ask and reassess.
🚩 Red-Flag Warning
Do not leave your child in a care arrangement where:

items: babies are placed to sleep unsafely;

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: staff appear intoxicated or severely impaired;
children are left unsupervised around water, stairs or roads;
caregivers shame, threaten or humiliate children;
explanations repeatedly do not match what you observe;
you are prevented from asking reasonable safeguarding questions.

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: For an immediate emergency in India, call 112 . For a child in crisis or needing protection, contact Child Helpline 1098 .
Your Childcare-Visit Checklist
People
☐ Caregiver qualifications and experience reviewed
☐ References or checks completed
☐ Adult-to-child ratio understood
☐ First-aid training available
☐ Staff turnover discussed
Safety
☐ Secure entry and pickup
☐ Medicines and chemicals locked
☐ Water risks controlled
☐ Safe infant sleep

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: ☐ Fire and emergency plan
☐ Clean food and nappy areas
☐ Dangerous cords, stairs and balconies protected
Care
☐ Responsive feeding
☐ No force-feeding
☐ Age-appropriate play
☐ Clear discipline policy
☐ Child’s home language respected
☐ Comfort provided during distress
Communication
☐ Daily reporting agreed
☐ Injury policy explained
☐ Illness policy explained
☐ Parent contacts current
☐ Concerns can be raised safely
💛 Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Your bond with your child is not measured by whether you provide every minute of care yourself. It is built through the dependable relationship you continue to create—and the care you choose around your child. A child can miss you and still be safe. A child can cry at goodbye and still build a warm relationship with another caregiver. A mother can love her child and also value her work. A parent can stay home and still need help. Care does not become less meaningful when it is shared responsibly.
🌼 Try This Today

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care
Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Authorised pickup people: ______________________ Give the card to every regular caregiver and review it whenever your child’s needs change. Sharing care becomes safer when important knowledge is shared too.
Authoritative References

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

items: WHO — Nurturing Care for Early Childhood Development
UNICEF — Returning to Work After Having a Baby
Government of India — Mission Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0
Government of India — Early Childhood Care and Education
Government of India — Emergency Response Support System 112

pages.chapterTitle: Choosing Childcare and Sharing Care

blocks.text: Editorial note for the Indian edition: Childcare regulation, crèche requirements, employment protections, background-verification processes, Anganwadi services and child-protection procedures vary by state, employer and setting. These must be checked against current national and state requirements immediately before publication. This chapter provides general education and safeguarding guidance, not legal advice.
PART V
Individual Needs and Moving Forward
Practical guidance, thoughtful reassurance and clear next steps.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Needs Additional Support
pages.epigraph: “A different path is still a path filled with learning, connection and possibility.”

blocks.text: When the Usual Advice Does Not Quite Fit

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child Needs Additional Support
